Nutrient and Gas Exchange] K --> L[Systemic Veins] L --> A ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Organization of the Respiratory System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Respiratory System] --> B[Upper Airways] A --> C[Lower Airways] A --> D[Gas Exchange Zone] A --> E[Pump Musculature] B --> B1[Nasal Cavity] B --> B2[Pharynx] B --> B3[Larynx] C --> C1[Trachea] C --> C2[Bronchi] C --> C3[Bronchioles] D --> D1[Alveolar Ducts] D --> D2[Alveolar Sacs] D --> D3[Alveoli] E --> E1[Diaphragm] E --> E2[External Intercostals] E --> E3[Internal Intercostals] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cell at Resting Potential (Ion Distribution Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OUT[Extracellular Fluid ECF] NAo[Na+ 145 mM] Ko[K+ 4 mM] Clo[Cl- 110 mM] end subgraph MEM[Cell Membrane 7-10 nm] PUMP[Na+/K+ ATPase Pump
3 Na+ out 2 K+ in per ATP] KCH[K+ Leak Channels
High Permeability at Rest] NACH[Na+ Channels
Closed at Rest] end subgraph IN[Intracellular Fluid ICF] NAi[Na+ 10 mM] Ki[K+ 140 mM] Ani[A- Proteins 138 mM] end NAo ---|Low Permeability| MEM Ko ---|High Permeability| MEM MEM --- KCH MEM --- NACH MEM --- PUMP MEM --- NAi MEM --- Ki MEM --- Ani ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Cardiac Conduction System (ECG Generation Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SA Node
Pacemaker 70 to 100 bpm] -->|Impulse| B[AV Node
Delay 0.1 s] B -->|Bundle of His| C[Bundle Branches] C -->|Purkinje Fibers| D[Ventricular Myocardium] D -->|P Wave Atrial Depol| E[PR Segment 0.12 to 0.20 s] E -->|QRS Ventricular Depol| F[ST Segment] F -->|T Wave Ventricular Repol| G[QT Interval 0.36 to 0.44 s] ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Bioelectric Signal Generation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ionic Gradients
Na K Ca Cl] --> B[Na+/K+ ATPase Pump] B --> C[Resting Membrane Potential
Negative 70 to 90 mV] C --> D{Stimulus Threshold} D -->|Sub-threshold| E[Local Graded Potential] D -->|Supra-threshold| F[Action Potential] E --> G[Summed PSPs] F --> H[All or None Response] G --> I[ECG / EEG / EMG Signal] H --> I[ECG / EEG / EMG Signal] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define resting membrane potential. State the Nernst equation and calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37°C given $[K^+]_{\text{out}} = 5 \text{ mM}$ and $[K^+]_{\text{in}} = 150 \text{ mM}$.* **Model Answer:** The resting membrane potential (RMP) is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is not undergoing an action potential, typically **−70 mV to −90 mV (inside negative)**. Nernst Equation: $$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$ At 37°C for a monovalent ion: $$E_{K^{+}} = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{5}{150} = 61.5 \times (-1.477) = -90.84 \text{ mV} \approx -91 \text{ mV}$$ **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Briefly explain the conduction system of the heart with the sequence of electrical excitation.* **Model Answer:** The cardiac conduction system comprises specialized autorhythmic cells that initiate and propagate the action potential: 1. **SA Node** (in right atrium) — primary pacemaker, generates impulse at 70–100 bpm. 2. **Internodal Pathways** — conduct impulse across atria. 3. **AV Node** (atrioventricular node) — introduces 0.1 s delay for ventricular filling. 4. **Bundle of His** → **Bundle Branches** (right and left) → **Purkinje Fibers** — rapidly conduct impulse through ventricles, ensuring coordinated contraction from apex upward. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Forgetting the sign convention in the Nernst equation (always reference *inside* potential relative to *outside*). > - Mixing up $RT/F$ and $2.303 RT/F$ (natural vs common log) — KTU **explicitly expects** the $\log_{10}$ form with the 61.5 mV factor at 37°C. > - Confusing *concentration* and *amount* of ions. > - Failing to mention the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** role in sustaining the RMP — typically costs 2 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** *(a)* Explain the **sources of bioelectric potentials** in detail. Discuss the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and ionic permeability in establishing the resting potential. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* Derive the **Nernst equation** from electrochemical principles. Using it, compute the equilibrium potential for Ca²⁺ at 37°C given $[Ca^{2+}]_{\text{out}} = 2 \text{ mM}$ and $[Ca^{2+}]_{\text{in}} = 10^{-4} \text{ mM}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Sources of Bioelectric Potentials — 7 Marks Model Answer:** The cell membrane is a thin (~7–10 nm) lipid bilayer that is differentially permeable to ions. Bioelectric potentials originate from: 1. **Ionic Concentration Gradients:** The intracellular fluid (ICF) is high in K⁺ (~140 mM) and anionic proteins (A⁻); the extracellular fluid (ECF) is high in Na⁺ (~145 mM) and Cl⁻ (~110 mM). These gradients are established by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**, which hydrolyzes 1 ATP to expel 3 Na⁺ out and import 2 K⁺ in. 2. **Selective Permeability:** At rest, the membrane is highly permeable to K⁺ (high $P_K$), less to Cl⁻, and almost impermeable to Na⁺ and anionic proteins. K⁺ diffuses outward down its concentration gradient, leaving behind A⁻ → the inside becomes **negatively charged** → this is the **resting membrane potential (RMP)**. 3. **Donnan Equilibrium:** Large impermeable anions (proteins, organic phosphates) on the inside require counter-ions (mostly K⁺) for electroneutrality, sustaining an asymmetric distribution. 4. **Active Transport:** The electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺ pump (3:2 stoichiometry) directly contributes **~−4 to −10 mV** to the RMP, on top of the diffusion potential. **(b) Nernst Derivation and Ca²⁺ Calculation — 7 Marks Model Solution:** **Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, equating the chemical and electrical free energies at equilibrium: $$\Delta G_{\text{chem}} + \Delta G_{\text{elec}} = 0$$ $$RT \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} + zF E = 0$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}}$$ **For Ca²⁺ (z = 2) at 37°C (3 marks):** The constant for divalent ions at 37°C is: $$\frac{2.303 RT}{zF} = \frac{61.5}{2} = 30.75 \text{ mV}$$ $$E_{Ca^{2+}} = 30.75 \log_{10} \frac{2}{10^{-4}} = 30.75 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^4)$$ $$\log_{10}(2 \times 10^4) = \log_{10}(2) + 4 = 0.301 + 4 = 4.301$$ $$E_{Ca^{2+}} = 30.75 \times 4.301 = 132.26 \text{ mV} \approx +132 \text{ mV}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(a)* Describe the **cardiovascular system** with a neat block diagram. List the four heart valves and their functions. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* Explain the **mechanics of respiration** with a labelled diagram of lung volumes and capacities. Define TLC, VC, and FRC with typical values. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Cardiovascular System — 7 Marks Model Answer:** The cardiovascular system (see diagram in Section 4.1) consists of: - **Heart** — dual four-chambered pump. - **Arteries** — thick muscular/elastic walls, carry blood away from the heart under high pressure. - **Arterioles** — resistance vessels, regulate flow distribution. - **Capillaries** — single endothelial cell thick, site of exchange. - **Venules and Veins** — capacitance vessels, return blood to heart. - **Blood** — transport medium (~5 L in adult). **Heart Valves and Functions:** | Valve | Location | Function | |---|---|---| | **Tricuspid** | Right AV junction | Prevents backflow RA→RV during ventricular systole | | **Pulmonary Semilunar** | RV→Pulmonary Artery | Prevents backflow PA→RV during diastole | | **Mitral (Bicuspid)** | Left AV junction | Prevents backflow LA→LV during ventricular systole | | **Aortic Semilunar** | LV→Aorta | Prevents backflow Aorta→LV during diastole | **(b) Mechanics of Respiration and Lung Volumes — 7 Marks Model Answer:** **Inspiration (Active):** The diaphragm (innervated by phrenic nerve) contracts and flattens; external intercostals lift the rib cage → thoracic cavity volume increases → intrapleural pressure becomes more sub-atmospheric (~−8 cmH₂O) → alveolar pressure drops (~−1 cmH₂O) → air flows in. **Expiration (Quiet, Passive):** Diaphragm and external intercostals relax → thoracic volume decreases → alveolar pressure rises above atmospheric → air flows out. **Lung Volumes and Capacities (Adult Male):** | Symbol | Parameter | Value (mL) | |---|---|---| | V_T | Tidal Volume | 500 | | IRV | Inspiratory Reserve Volume | 3000 | | ERV | Expiratory Reserve Volume | 1100 | | RV | Residual Volume | 1200 | | **VC** | Vital Capacity = V_T + IRV + ERV | **4600** | | **FRC** | Functional Residual Capacity = ERV + RV | **2300** | | **TLC** | Total Lung Capacity = VC + RV | **5800** | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **RMP ≈ −70 mV (neuron), −90 mV (ventricular myocyte)** — always specify cell type. - ✅ **Nernst Equation:** $E = (RT/zF) \ln([X]_o/[X]_i) = 61.5 \log_{10}([X]_o/[X]_i)$ mV at 37°C for monovalent ions. - ✅ **GHK Equation** handles multi-ion RMP — remember that at rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na}$, so RMP ≈ $E_K$. - ✅ **Cardiac Output = HR × SV** (Fick Principle for oxygen: CO = VO₂ / [CaO₂ − CvO₂]). - ✅ **MAP = DBP + ⅓(SBP − DBP)**. - ✅ **Lung Capacities:** VC = TV + IRV + ERV; TLC = VC + RV; FRC = ERV + RV. - ✅ **ECG Order:** P → QRS → T (P = atrial depol, QRS = ventricular depol, T = ventricular repol). - ✅ **Conduction Sequence:** SA node → AV node (0.1 s delay) → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibers. - ✅ **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase:** 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP — electrogenic, contributes ~−4 to −10 mV. - ✅ **Cardiac Valves:** Tricuspid, Pulmonary, Mitral, Aortic — mnemonics: **"Try Pulling My Aorta"**. - ✅ **Respiratory Drive:** Inspiration is active (diaphragm); quiet expiration is passive. - ✅ **Sign Convention:** $E_{\text{ion}} = V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}$ — negative means inside is negative.

topics.title: Action Potential. Electrode Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Action Potential. Electrode Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Action Potential & Electrode Theory

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Action Potential — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> An **Action Potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing electrical signal that propagates along the membrane of an excitable cell (neuron or muscle cell). It is generated by a sudden, coordinated change in the ionic permeability of the cell membrane, causing the membrane potential to reverse polarity and then recover. The entire event typically lasts **1 to 2 milliseconds**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terms, the Action Potential is the **fundamental biophysical unit** that bridges cellular physiology with measurable bioelectric signals (ECG, EMG, EEG).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower & Valve" Model

Imagine a water tank on a hill (the cell) filled with positively charged ions (K⁺ inside, Na⁺ outside). A small trigger (threshold stimulus) is like pulling a lever:

- **Lever pulled (threshold reached):** A large valve (Na⁺ channel) opens, water rushes in → cell becomes positive inside.
- **Valve closes, another opens (K⁺ channels):** Water rushes out → cell returns to negative inside.
- **Refractory period:** The tank needs a moment to refill and reset before the next flush.

This is precisely how an Action Potential works — a self-propagating wave of ionic flux governed by voltage-gated ion channels.

### 1.3 Electrode Theory — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Electrode Theory** describes the electrochemical behaviour at the interface between a metallic conductor and an electrolyte (biological tissue/fluid). The electrode acts as a **transducer**, converting ionic currents in the body into electronic currents that can be measured by an instrument.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at a Border"

Think of an electrode as a **customs officer at a national border**:
- Inside the body: communication happens in "ion language" (ionic current).
- Outside (in the wire/amplifier): communication happens in "electron language" (electronic current).
- The electrode is the **translator** that enables this exchange at the metal-electrolyte interface.

Standard reference values used throughout biomedical electrode design:

- **Resting Membrane Potential ≈ −70 mV** (typical for mammalian neurons)
- **Threshold Potential ≈ −55 mV**
- **Peak Depolarization ≈ +30 to +40 mV**
- **Nernst Constant (RT/F) at 37°C ≈ 26.71 mV**
- **Body Temperature = 310 K (37°C)**
- **Faraday's Constant F ≈ 96,485 C/mol**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of the Action Potential

The Action Potential is conventionally divided into **5 sequential phases**, each governed by specific ion channels:

### Phase 1 — Resting State (≈ −70 mV)
- Membrane is polarized.
- Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump maintains ionic gradients (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in).
- Voltage-gated channels are **closed** but available.

### Phase 2 — Depolarization (Rising Phase)
- Stimulus pushes membrane potential from −70 mV to **threshold (−55 mV)**.
- Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open rapidly**.
- Na⁺ rushes in (electrochemical gradient).
- Membrane potential shoots up toward **+30 to +40 mV**.

### Phase 3 — Repolarization (Falling Phase)
- Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (close).
- Voltage-gated **K⁺ channels open** (delayed rectifier).
- K⁺ flows out, restoring negative interior.

### Phase 4 — Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)
- K⁺ channels close slowly → membrane briefly dips below −70 mV (≈ −80 mV).
- This is the **relative refractory period**.

### Phase 5 — Return to Resting Potential
- Na⁺/K⁺ pump and leak channels restore ionic distribution.
- Membrane is again excitable.

## 2.2 Nernst Equation — The Foundation

The **Nernst Equation** gives the equilibrium potential for a single ion across a permeable membrane:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}\right)$$

Converting to log base 10 (using 2.303 conversion factor):

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}\right)$$

Where:
- $E_{ion}$ = equilibrium (Nernst) potential in **Volts**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin**
- $z$ = Valency (charge number) of the ion
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $[ion]$ = Molar concentration

## 2.3 Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation

The **GHK Voltage Equation** gives the actual resting membrane potential when **multiple ions** contribute to permeability:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GHK over Nernst?** The Nernst equation assumes permeability of only ONE ion. In reality, biological membranes are permeable to Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻ simultaneously, each with different permeability ($P$). The GHK equation is the **weighted average** of all Nernst potentials based on these permeabilities.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Single-ion equilibrium potential |
| 2 | Nernst at 37°C | $E \approx \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV | Quick numerical estimate |
| 3 | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | Resting membrane potential |
| 4 | Half-Cell Potential | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10}[M^{n+}]$ | Electrode-electrolyte interface |
| 5 | Electrode Impedance | $Z = \sqrt{R_s^2 + \dfrac{1}{(2\pi f C_d)^2}}$ | Total electrode-electrolyte impedance |
| 6 | RC Time Constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Determines low-frequency cutoff |
| 7 | Cable Equation | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Length constant of nerve fibre |
| 8 | Conduction Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\lambda}{\tau}$ | AP propagation speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in designing **pacemakers, defibrillators, neural stimulators, EEG/ECG amplifiers, and brain-computer interfaces (BCI)**. Understanding AP dynamics is critical for any closed-loop neural implant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

### Step 1 — Setup the System
Consider an ion $X^{z+}$ at concentration $[X]_{in}$ inside the cell and $[X]_{out}$ outside, separated by a membrane permeable only to that ion.

### Step 2 — Electrochemical Equilibrium
At equilibrium, the **chemical potential** (driving force due to concentration gradient) is exactly balanced by the **electrical potential** (driving force due to voltage gradient).

$$\Delta \mu_{chemical} + \Delta \mu_{electrical} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Chemical Potential Gradient
For a concentration difference, the chemical work required to move one mole of ion:

$$\Delta \mu_{chemical} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Electrical Potential Energy
Work done moving charge $zF$ across potential difference $E$:

$$\Delta \mu_{electrical} = zFE$$

### Step 5 — Equate and Solve
Setting the sum to zero at equilibrium:

$$RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) + zFE_{ion} = 0$$

Solving for $E_{ion}$:

$$E_{ion} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Sign Convention Adjustment
By convention, $E_{ion}$ is defined as the **inside potential relative to outside**, so the sign is flipped:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

### Step 7 — Convert to base-10 logarithm
Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **final Nernst Equation**, valid for any single ion at any temperature.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — K⁺ Nernst Potential

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM
- $T = 310$ K (37°C body temperature)
- $z = +1$ for K⁺

**Step 1 — Plug into Nernst Equation:**

$$E_K = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485} \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310 = 5933.4$$

**Step 3 — Divide by F:**

$$\frac{5933.4}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) = \log_{10}(0.0357) = -1.447$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.447) = -89.0 \text{ mV}$$

> **Result:** $E_K \approx -89$ mV. This matches the experimental value of ~−90 mV, confirming the Nernst equation's accuracy.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — GHK Resting Potential

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 10$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$
- $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 110$ (note inverted for Cl⁻)
- $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$
- $T = 310$ K

**Step 1 — Plug into GHK Voltage Equation:**

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{(1)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(110)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Numerator computation:**

$$5 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.6$$

**Step 3 — Denominator computation:**

$$140 + 0.4 + 49.5 = 189.9$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{12.6}{189.9} = 0.0664$$

**Step 5 — Take natural log:**

$$\ln(0.0664) = -2.711$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by RT/F (≈ 26.71 mV):**

$$V_m = 26.71 \times (-2.711) = -72.4 \text{ mV}$$

> **Result:** $V_m \approx -72$ mV. This is **remarkably close** to the experimentally observed resting potential of **−70 mV**. ✓

## 3.4 Electrode Half-Cell Potential Derivation

For a metal M in equilibrium with its ion $M^{n+}$ in solution, the **Nernst Equation for Electrodes** gives the half-cell potential:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln[M^{n+}]$$

Or in log base 10 form:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10}[M^{n+}] \quad (\text{at } 25°C)$$

Where:
- $E^0$ = Standard electrode potential (relative to Standard Hydrogen Electrode, SHE)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred
- $[M^{n+}]$ = Activity (≈ concentration) of metal ion

**Example for Ag/AgCl Electrode:**

$$E = E^0_{Ag/AgCl} + \frac{0.0592}{1} \log_{10}[Ag^+]$$

With $E^0_{Ag/AgCl} = +0.222$ V vs SHE — making it a stable, low-polarization reference electrode.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Comparison

| Property | Ag/AgCl | Pt | Stainless Steel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Half-cell potential | **+0.222 V** | Variable | Variable |
| Polarization | Low | Medium | High |
| Stability | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Use | ECG, EEG reference | Stimulation | Disposable, short-term |
| Cost | Moderate | High | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Action Potential — Phase-by-Phase Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus Applied] --> B[Resting Potential -70 mV]
    B --> C{Membrane Potential crosses Threshold -55 mV?}
    C -->|No| B
    C -->|Yes| D[Voltage-gated Na+ Channels OPEN]
    D --> E[Na+ Influx - Rapid Depolarization]
    E --> F[Peak +30 to +40 mV]
    F --> G[Na+ Channels INACTIVATE]
    G --> H[Voltage-gated K+ Channels OPEN]
    H --> I[K+ Efflux - Repolarization]
    I --> J[Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    J --> K[Relative Refractory Period]
    K --> L[Na+/K+ ATPase Restores Gradients]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Explanation:** This flowchart represents the closed-loop regenerative cycle of an Action Potential. Note the **feedback return to Resting State** — every AP resets itself, ensuring the system is ready for the next stimulus.

## 4.2 Electrode-Electrolyte Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Metal Electrode] -->|R_s + Half-Cell Potential| B[Double Layer Capacitance C_d]
    B --> C[Tissue / Electrolyte Resistance R_t]
    C --> D[Biological Tissue]
    D --> E[Second Electrode]
    E --> F[Measuring Instrument]
    
    subgraph ElectrodeInterface[Electrode-Electrolyte Interface]
    A
    B
    C
    end
    
    style A fill:#ffd700
    style B fill:#87ceeb
    style C fill:#90ee90
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The interface behaves electrically as a **parallel RC network**: $R_s$ (charge-transfer resistance) in parallel with $C_d$ (Helmholtz double-layer capacitance). The total impedance $Z$ decreases with frequency, which is why **biopotential amplifiers use high input impedance** at low frequencies.

## 4.3 Ion Channel State Machine During AP

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Resting
    Resting --> Threshold_Crossed: Stimulus reaches -55 mV
    Threshold_Crossed --> Na_Open: Na+ channels activate
    Na_Open --> Peak_Depolarization: Rapid Na+ influx
    Peak_Depolarization --> Na_Inactivated: Na+ channels inactivate
    Na_Inactivated --> K_Open: K+ channels open
    K_Open --> Repolarization: K+ efflux
    Repolarization --> Hyperpolarized: Overshoot below -70 mV
    Hyperpolarized --> Restoring: Na+/K+ pump active
    Restoring --> Resting: Ionic gradients restored
    Resting --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Biopotential Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitable Cell / Tissue] -->|Ionic Current| B[Electrode Pair]
    B -->|Electronic Signal| C[Differential Amplifier]
    C --> D[Band-Pass Filter 0.05 to 1500 Hz]
    D --> E[Isolation Amplifier Safety Barrier]
    E --> F[ADC and Signal Processor]
    F --> G[Display / Recording]
    
    style A fill:#ffb6c1
    style B fill:#ffd700
    style C fill:#87ceeb
    style D fill:#90ee90
    style E fill:#dda0dd
    style F fill:#f0e68c
    style G fill:#ffa07a
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Action Potential. List its phases.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

An **Action Potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing reversal of the resting membrane potential of an excitable cell (neuron/myocyte), caused by the sequential opening and closing of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels.

**Phases:**
1. **Resting State** (≈ −70 mV): Polarized membrane, ion pumps active.
2. **Depolarization**: Na⁺ channels open → rapid Na⁺ influx → membrane potential rises.
3. **Repolarization**: Na⁺ channels inactivate, K⁺ channels open → K⁺ efflux.
4. **Hyperpolarization** (≈ −80 mV): Brief undershoot due to slow K⁺ channel closure.
5. **Return to Resting Potential**: Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase restores ionic gradients.

---

### Question 2: What is a Polarizable vs Non-Polarizable Electrode? Give one example of each.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Polarizable Electrode | Non-Polarizable Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| Charge Transfer | Capacitive only (no faradaic current) | Both capacitive and faradaic |
| DC Current Flow | Negligible | Significant |
| Half-cell Potential | Unstable, varies with current | Stable, well-defined |
| Example | **Platinum (Pt)** | **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** |

> **Non-polarizable electrodes** are preferred for **biopotential recording** (ECG, EEG) because they exhibit low and stable offset potentials, minimizing motion artifacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the Nernst Equation. Calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37°C, given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Start with the condition of electrochemical equilibrium:**

At equilibrium, the net flux of ions across the membrane is zero. The free energy change for moving 1 mole of ion $X^{z+}$ from inside to outside must vanish:

$$\Delta G = RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) + zFE = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for E (the equilibrium potential):**

$$E = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sign convention** (E defined as inside relative to outside):

$$E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert ln to log₁₀** ($\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$):

$$E = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** ✓

**Step 5 — Numerical Calculation for K⁺:**

Given: $T = 310$ K, $z = +1$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM

$$E_K = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485} \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right)$$

$$E_K = \frac{5933.4}{96485} \log_{10}(0.0357)$$

$$E_K = 0.0615 \times (-1.447)$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -89.0 \text{ mV}}$$

This matches the experimental value ≈ −90 mV, validating the equation.

---

#### (b) Explain the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation. Why is it preferred over the Nernst equation for calculating the resting membrane potential? (7 Marks)

**Definition:**

The **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** is a generalization of the Nernst equation that accounts for the simultaneous permeability of a membrane to **multiple ion species** (typically Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻):

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

**Why GHK is preferred over Nernst:**

| Aspect | Nernst Equation | GHK Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Ions considered | Only one ion at a time | Multiple ions simultaneously |
| Permeability | Assumes 100% for one ion | Weighted by relative permeabilities $P_K, P_{Na}, P_{Cl}$ |
| Realism | Idealized | Reflects actual biological membrane |
| Output | Single-ion equilibrium potential | Actual resting membrane potential |

**Physical Interpretation:**

The membrane potential $V_m$ is essentially a **weighted average** of the individual Nernst potentials of Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻, with weights equal to their relative permeabilities. Since the resting membrane is **highly permeable to K⁺** ($P_K$ is dominant), $V_m$ lies close to $E_K$ (−90 mV) but is **depolarized** to ≈ −70 mV due to the small but finite Na⁺ permeability.

**Substituting typical values yields $V_m \approx -72$ mV**, in excellent agreement with experiment.

---

### 🔹 Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe the different phases of an Action Potential with the help of a neat diagram. Explain the role of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels. (7 Marks)

**Phase-wise Description:**

```
       +40 ┤              ╭──╮ Peak
            │             ╱    ╲
   Membrane │            ╱      ╲
   Potential│           ╱        ╲
   (mV)     │          ╱          ╲
            │         ╱            ╲
       -55 ┤- - - - ╱- - - - - - - ╲- - Threshold
            │      ╱                ╲
       -70 ┤──────╱──────────────────╲────── Resting
            │                          ╲___
       -80 ┤                              ╲__ Hyperpolarization
            └──────────────────────────────────► Time (ms)
              0    1    2    3    4    5
```

**Phase 1 — Resting Potential (−70 mV):**
Membrane polarized; Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase maintains gradients (high K⁺ inside, high Na⁺ outside). Voltage-gated channels are **closed but available**.

**Phase 2 — Depolarization (Rising Phase):**
When stimulus crosses threshold (−55 mV):
- Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open rapidly** (activation gates swing open).
- Na⁺ rushes in down its electrochemical gradient.
- Membrane potential reverses to ~+30 to +40 mV.

**Phase 3 — Repolarization (Falling Phase):**
- Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (inactivation gates close).
- Voltage-gated **K⁺ channels open** (delayed).
- K⁺ flows out, restoring negative interior.

**Phase 4 — Hyperpolarization:**
- K⁺ channels close slowly → membrane briefly dips to ~−80 mV.
- This creates the **relative refractory period**.

**Phase 5 — Recovery:**
- Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump (using ATP) restores the original ionic distribution.
- Membrane becomes fully excitable again.

**Role of Voltage-Gated Channels:**

| Channel | Activation | Inactivation | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Na⁺ channel** | Fast (ms) | Fast | Triggers depolarization |
| **K⁺ channel** | Slow (delayed) | Very slow | Drives repolarization |

> The **sequential kinetics** of these channels (Na⁺ opens before K⁺, and Na⁺ inactivates while K⁺ is still open) is the biophysical basis of the AP waveform.

---

#### (b) With the help of an equivalent circuit, explain the electrode-electrolyte interface. Differentiate between polarizable and non-polarizable electrodes. (7 Marks)

**Equivalent Circuit Model:**

The electrode-electrolyte interface can be modelled as a **parallel RC network** in series with the tissue resistance $R_t$:

- **$E_{hc}$** = Half-cell potential (DC source)
- **$R_s$** = Solution/charge-transfer resistance
- **$C_d$** = Helmholtz double-layer capacitance
- **$R_t$** = Tissue (electrolyte) resistance

The total impedance magnitude is:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R_s^2 + \frac{1}{(2\pi f C_d)^2}}$$

> At **low frequencies**, $C_d$ acts as an open circuit, so $\vert Z \vert \approx R_s$ (high). At **high frequencies**, $C_d$ shorts out $R_s$, reducing impedance. This is why **biopotential amplifiers need high input impedance** at low frequencies (e.g., ECG at 0.05 Hz).

**Polarizable vs Non-Polarizable Electrodes:**

| Feature | Polarizable Electrode | Non-Polarizable Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| Charge transfer across interface | Negligible (capacitive) | Free (faradaic) |
| DC current passes? | No | Yes |
| Half-cell potential stability | Poor | Excellent |
| Behaviour under AC | Like a capacitor | Like a resistor |
| Examples | Platinum, Stainless Steel | **Ag/AgCl**, Calomel |
| Biopotential use | Stimulation only | **Recording (ECG, EEG, EMG)** |

**Why Ag/AgCl is Preferred:**

The reaction at the Ag/AgCl interface is **reversible and rapid**:

$$Ag \leftrightarrow Ag^+ + e^-$$
$$Ag^+ + Cl^- \leftrightarrow AgCl$$

Because both forward and reverse reactions occur freely, **no net polarization** builds up — making it the gold standard for biopotential recording.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in this topic:
> 1. **Sign error in Nernst:** Forgetting that the inside concentration goes in the **denominator** for cations. Always double-check the formula.
> 2. **Confusing polarizable with non-polarizable:** Pt is **polarizable**, not non-polarizable. Students frequently swap them.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always express final answer in **mV**, not Volts, for biopotentials.
> 4. **Missing the "Why" in GHK:** Examiners want you to state that GHK is used because the membrane is permeable to **multiple ions** simultaneously — not just one.
> 5. **Diagram without axis labels:** Always label membrane potential (mV) on Y-axis and time (ms) on X-axis in AP diagrams. Unlabelled diagrams lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Na⁺/K⁺ pump confusion:** The pump is **not** responsible for repolarization — that's done by K⁺ efflux. The pump only **restores** gradients after the AP.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ⚡ **Resting Membrane Potential = −70 mV** (mammalian neuron)
- ⚡ **Threshold Potential = −55 mV** (all-or-nothing trigger)
- ⚡ **Peak AP Amplitude = +30 to +40 mV**
- ⚡ **AP Duration ≈ 1–2 ms**
- ⚡ **Nernst Equation:** $E = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$
- ⚡ **At 37°C (310 K):** $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \approx 61.5$ mV (for $z = 1$)
- ⚡ **Goldman Equation** = Nernst + multiple ions weighted by permeability
- ⚡ **Resting Membrane Permeability:** $P_K \gg P_{Cl} > P_{Na}$ → resting potential is closest to $E_K$
- ⚡ **Depolarization = Na⁺ channels open → Na⁺ influx**
- ⚡ **Repolarization = K⁺ channels open → K⁺ efflux**
- ⚡ **Refractory Periods:** Absolute (cannot fire) + Relative (needs stronger stimulus)
- ⚡ **Ag/AgCl Electrode** = Most widely used **non-polarizable** biopotential electrode
- ⚡ **Pt Electrode** = Polarizable; used for **stimulation**, not recording
- ⚡ **Half-Cell Potential of Ag/AgCl = +0.222 V** vs SHE
- ⚡ **Electrode Impedance is Frequency-Dependent:** High at low f, low at high f
- ⚡ **Equivalent Circuit:** $R_s \parallel C_d$ in series with $R_t$
- ⚡ **ECG Frequency Range:** 0.05 – 150 Hz | **EEG:** 0.5 – 100 Hz | **EMG:** 20 – 5000 Hz
- ⚡ **Cable Equation Length Constant:** $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ (determines AP propagation)
- ⚡ **Clinical Relevance:** Pacemaker mimics SA-node AP; defibrillator resets chaotic ventricular APs
- ⚡ **KTU Hot Topic:** Always draw a labelled AP waveform with mV and ms axes (3 marks guaranteed)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nernst Equation and Various Types of Electrodes. Bioelectric Signals - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nernst Equation and Various Types of Electrodes. Bioelectric Signals - ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Nernst Equation — Formal Definition

The **Nernst Equation** is a fundamental electrochemistry relation that quantifies the equilibrium reduction potential ($E$) of an electrochemical half-cell (or, equivalently, the transmembrane potential of a biological cell) as a function of the ionic activities (concentrations) on either side of a selectively permeable membrane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Nernst Equation is the *mathematical foundation* of all bioelectric signal generation. Every ECG, EEG, and EMG waveform is a direct macroscopic manifestation of the microscopic ion gradients governed by the Nernst relation.

Mathematically stated:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

Where the parameters represent the standard electrochemical state variables used universally in KTU board examinations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tower & Pressure" Model

Imagine two water tanks connected by a semi-permeable membrane that allows only sodium ions to pass. The water levels in both tanks try to equalize due to gravity, but the membrane restricts flow to a specific ion — this creates a measurable *pressure difference* (voltage) across the wall.

- **High concentration outside** → ion "pushes" inward → **positive potential inside**.
- **High concentration inside** → ion "pushes" outward → **negative potential inside**.
- The **Nernst Equation** simply tells you *exactly how much pressure (voltage)* develops for a given difference in tank levels (concentrations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The Nernst potential is the *equilibrium voltage* at which the electrical driving force exactly balances the chemical diffusion force, resulting in **zero net ionic current**.

### 1.3 Bioelectric Electrodes — Formal Definition

A **bioelectric electrode** is a transduction interface that converts the ionic current flow inside the body (carried by ions like Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) into electronic current flow in the measuring instrument (carried by electrons in metal wires), enabling the safe acquisition of bioelectric signals such as ECG, EEG, and EMG.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention:** KTU board examiners expect the use of the silver/silver-chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrode as the *de facto* reference in all biomedical applications because of its superior stability, low half-cell potential, and low polarization noise.

### 1.4 Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Formal Definition

The **Electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG)** is the graphical time-domain recording of the electrical activity of the heart, acquired non-invasively using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. It represents the *summated action potentials* of millions of cardiac myocytes, viewed from the body surface.

**Standard Physical Constants (Bolded for Quick Reference):**
- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96,485$ C/mol
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- **Body Temperature (Normal):** $T = 310$ K ($37^{\circ}$C)
- **Standard Room Temperature:** $T = 298$ K ($25^{\circ}$C)
- **Thermal Voltage at 310 K:** $V_T = \frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71$ mV
- **Thermal Voltage at 298 K:** $V_T \approx 25.69$ mV
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of the Bioelectric Potential

The human body is an **electrolyte solution** — a salt-water environment rich in dissociated ions. Every cellular membrane acts as a selective diffusion barrier. The sequence of physical events generating a bioelectric potential is as follows:

1. **Ionic Gradient Establishment:** Active transport pumps (e.g., the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump) maintain asymmetric ionic concentrations across the cell membrane — high K⁺ inside, high Na⁺ outside.
2. **Selective Permeability:** The membrane at rest is highly permeable to K⁺ and almost impermeable to Na⁺.
3. **Diffusion Flux:** K⁺ ions diffuse outward down their concentration gradient, leaving behind unbalanced negative charge (Cl⁻ and organic anions cannot follow).
4. **Equilibrium Reached:** A small *transmembrane potential* (typically $\sim -90$ mV inside relative to outside) develops, sufficient to halt further K⁺ efflux.
5. **Nernst Equilibrium:** This final stable potential equals the **Nernst Potential** for the dominant permeant ion.

### 2.2 The Two Logical Forms of the Nernst Equation

**Form 1 — Natural Logarithm (Thermodynamic form):**

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

**Form 2 — Base-10 Logarithm (Engineering form, preferred in KTU problems):**

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

### 2.3 Numerical Simplification at Body & Room Temperatures

**At Body Temperature (T = 310 K):**

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0615}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right) \quad \text{(in Volts)}$$

**At Standard Room Temperature (T = 298 K):**

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0591}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right) \quad \text{(in Volts)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When the question specifies "body temperature" or "37°C", *always* use the coefficient **0.0615**, not 0.0591. This is one of the most common single-mark errors in KTU board evaluations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Units / Typical Value | Application |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (natural log) | $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[\text{Ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{Ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Volts (V) | General thermodynamic form |
| 2 | Nernst Equation (log10, body) | $E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0615}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{Ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{Ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Volts (V) | KTU default at 310 K |
| 3 | Nernst Equation (log10, room) | $E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0591}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{Ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{Ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Volts (V) | KTU default at 298 K |
| 4 | Thermal Voltage $V_T$ | $V_T = \frac{RT}{F}$ | $\approx 25.69$ mV (298 K), $\approx 26.71$ mV (310 K) | Energy scale of one electron charge |
| 5 | Nernst Potential for K⁺ | $E_{K} = -61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{in}}}{[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{out}}}\right)$ mV | $\approx -90$ mV (resting) | Dominant at cardiac resting state |
| 6 | Nernst Potential for Na⁺ | $E_{Na} = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ mV | $\approx +60$ mV | Dominant during depolarization |
| 7 | Goldmann-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{K}[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{out}} + P_{Na}[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{out}} + P_{Cl}[\text{Cl}^{-}]_{\text{in}}}{P_{K}[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{in}} + P_{Na}[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{in}} + P_{Cl}[\text{Cl}^{-}]_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | mV | Multi-ion membrane potential |
| 8 | Half-Cell Potential (Ag/AgCl) | $E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} \approx +0.222$ V vs SHE | Volts | Reference electrode baseline |
| 9 | ECG Heart Rate | $\text{HR} = \frac{1500}{\text{RR interval in mm}} \approx \frac{60}{\text{RR interval in s}}$ | bpm (beats per minute) | 25 mm/s paper speed |
| 10 | Einthoven's Law | $V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}$ | Volts | Vector closure of limb leads |
| 11 | Cardiac Axis | $\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{Lead I net QRS}}{\text{Lead aVF net QRS}}$ | Degrees (°) | Mean electrical axis calculation |

### 2.5 Classification of Bioelectric Electrodes

Electrodes are classified by the KTU syllabus along three orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — By Application / Location:**
- **Surface (Skin) Electrodes:** Disposable Ag/AgCl pads for ECG, EEG.
- **Needle Electrodes:** Fine-gauge needles inserted into muscle for EMG.
- **Microelectrodes:** Glass micropipettes (tip diameter $< 1$ μm) for intracellular recording.
- **Invasive Implantable Electrodes:** Pacemaker leads, deep-brain stimulators.

**Axis 2 — By Electrochemical Behavior:**
- **Perfectly Polarizable Electrodes:** No actual charge crosses the electrode–electrolyte interface; current is carried by displacement. Example: Platinum.
- **Perfectly Non-Polarizable Electrodes:** Current freely crosses the interface with negligible overpotential. Example: Ag/AgCl — *preferred for ECG*.
- **Real Electrodes:** Exhibit both polarizable and non-polarizable behavior; modeled as a parallel RC circuit.

**Axis 3 — By Reference Function:**
- **Reference Electrodes:** Provide a stable, known half-cell potential (e.g., Standard Hydrogen Electrode, Ag/AgCl).
- **Working / Active Electrodes:** Measure the variable bioelectric potential.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Engineering Application |
|:--|:--|
| Cardiology | 12-lead ECG machines, Holter monitors, defibrillators |
| Neurology | EEG headsets (clinical & consumer-grade) |
| Rehabilitation | EMG-controlled prosthetics, functional electrical stimulators |
| Sports Science | Heart-rate variability monitors, lactate threshold sensing |
| Pharmaceutical | Ion-selective electrodes for blood electrolyte analysis |
| Industrial Bioprocess | pH, dissolved-O₂ probes in bioreactors |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Thermodynamic Form)

The Nernst Equation is derived from the equality of electrochemical potentials at equilibrium. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation that a KTU board examiner expects for full marks.

**Step 1 — Free Energy of an Electrochemical Reaction:**

The Gibbs free energy change of a redox reaction is the sum of a chemical term and an electrical term:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln(Q)$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient and $\Delta G^{0}$ is the standard-state free energy change.

**Step 2 — Relate Free Energy to Cell Potential:**

By the fundamental thermodynamic identity linking free energy to electrical work, with $n$ electrons transferred and $z$ the ionic charge:

$$\Delta G = -zFE$$

$$\Delta G^{0} = -zFE^{0}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Free Energy Expression:**

$$-zFE = -zFE^{0} + RT \ln(Q)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for E:**

$$zFE = zFE^{0} - RT \ln(Q)$$

$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{zF} \ln(Q)$$

**Step 5 — Express Q for a Transmembrane Ion:**

For a single ionic species diffusing across a membrane from *outside* to *inside*:

$$Q = \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Q into the Equation:**

$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Invert the Logarithm Argument:**

Using the identity $\ln(a/b) = -\ln(b/a)$:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the final canonical form of the Nernst Equation.** The sign convention here treats $E$ as the *inside-relative-to-outside* potential — a convention universally followed in KTU biomedical courses.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem Type

**Problem:** Calculate the Nernst potential for a potassium ion at body temperature (37°C), given $[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{inside}} = 140$ mM and $[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{outside}} = 5$ mM. Take $E^{0} = 0$ V.

**Step 1 — Identify Constants:**
- $T = 310$ K
- $z = +1$ (for K⁺)
- $E^{0} = 0$ V (standard biological reference)

**Step 2 — Select the Correct Equation:**

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0615}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$E = 0 + \frac{0.0615}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Ratio:**

$$E = 0.0615 \cdot \log_{10}(0.03571)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.4472$$

**Step 6 — Final Multiplication:**

$$E = 0.0615 \cdot (-1.4472) = -0.0890 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Millivolts and Report:**

$$E \approx -89.0 \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** This result closely matches the experimentally measured resting membrane potential of cardiac ventricular cells ($\approx -90$ mV), confirming that K⁺ is indeed the dominant permeant ion at rest.

### 3.3 Derivation of the ECG Lead Relationships

**Step 1 — Define Lead Vectors:**

By Einthoven's hypothesis, the heart acts as a single equivalent dipole located at the center of an equilateral triangle formed by the right arm (RA), left arm (LA), and left leg (LL) electrodes. The leads measure the *projections* of this dipole vector onto the respective lead axes.

**Step 2 — Bipolar Limb Lead Definitions:**

$$V_{I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Step 3 — Derive Einthoven's Law (Vector Closure):**

Adding Lead I and Lead III:

$$V_{I} + V_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$

$$V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Right-Hand Side:**

By definition, $V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}$. Therefore:

$$V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Rule:** If a KTU numerical problem provides two of the three bipolar leads, the third is *always* obtainable from this identity — without any additional measurement.

### 3.4 Augmented Unipolar Lead Derivation

**Step 1 — Wilson's Central Terminal:**

Define a reference point as the average of the three limb electrode potentials:

$$V_{CT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Augmented Vector Magnitude Enhancement:**

Because the augmented leads measure between *one* electrode and the *average of the other two*, the resulting signal is amplified by a factor of 3/2 to compensate for the averaging. Hence:

$$V_{aVR} = \frac{3}{2}(V_{RA} - V_{CT})$$

$$V_{aVL} = \frac{3}{2}(V_{LA} - V_{CT})$$

$$V_{aVF} = \frac{3}{2}(V_{LL} - V_{CT})$$

**Step 3 — Mean Electrical Axis Formula:**

Using the net QRS areas from Lead I and Lead aVF:

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{A_{QRS}^{aVF}}{A_{QRS}^{I}}\right)$$

### 3.5 Electrode Equivalent Circuit — Polarization Model

A real electrode in contact with an electrolyte is modeled by the following series-parallel network:

$$V_{\text{measured}} = V_{\text{half-cell}} + I \cdot R_{s} + V_{\text{polarization}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{half-cell}}$ = DC offset (e.g., 0.222 V for Ag/AgCl)
- $R_{s}$ = series resistance of electrolyte and skin
- $V_{\text{polarization}}$ = overpotential (ideally zero for non-polarizable electrodes)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Ag/AgCl electrode's low half-cell potential drift ($\ll 1$ mV/hour) and minimal polarization overpotential are *the* engineering reasons for its dominance in clinical ECG practice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Nernst Equation — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ionic Gradient Input] --> B[Concentration Ratio Calculation]
    B --> C[Apply Logarithm Function]
    C --> D[Multiply by RT over zF]
    D --> E[Add Standard Potential E0]
    E --> F[Output Equilibrium Potential E]
    G[Body Temperature 310K] --> D
    H[Valence z] --> D
    I[Universal Gas Constant R] --> D
    J[Faraday Constant F] --> D
```

### 4.2 Bioelectric Electrode — Hierarchical Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bioelectric Electrodes] --> B[By Location]
    A --> C[By Electrochemistry]
    A --> D[By Reference Role]
    B --> B1[Surface Electrodes]
    B --> B2[Needle Electrodes]
    B --> B3[Microelectrodes]
    B --> B4[Implantable Electrodes]
    C --> C1[Polarizable Pt]
    C --> C2[Non-Polarizable AgAgCl]
    C --> C3[Hybrid Real Electrodes]
    D --> D1[Reference SHE]
    D --> D2[Reference AgAgCl]
    D --> D3[Working Active Electrode]
```

### 4.3 ECG Lead System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RA[Right Arm RA] --> CT[Wilson Central Terminal]
    LA[Left Arm LA] --> CT
    LL[Left Leg LL] --> CT
    RA --> L1[Lead I Bipolar]
    LA --> L1
    RA --> L2[Lead II Bipolar]
    LL --> L2
    LA --> L3[Lead III Bipolar]
    LL --> L3
    RA --> AVR[Lead aVR Augmented]
    CT --> AVR
    LA --> AVL[Lead aVL Augmented]
    CT --> AVL
    LL --> AVF[Lead aVF Augmented]
    CT --> AVF
    L1 --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    L2 --> AMP
    L3 --> AMP
    AVR --> AMP
    AVL --> AMP
    AVF --> AMP
    AMP --> OUT[12 Lead ECG Output]
```

### 4.4 ECG Waveform Components — Functional Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[P Wave] --> A1[Atrial Depolarization]
    B[PR Segment] --> B1[AV Nodal Delay]
    C[QRS Complex] --> C1[Ventricular Depolarization]
    D[ST Segment] --> D1[Ventricular Repolarization Plateau]
    E[T Wave] --> E1[Ventricular Repolarization]
    F[U Wave] --> F1[Purkinje Repolarization Rarely Seen]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
```

### 4.5 Electrode–Electrolyte Interface — Functional Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Body Tissue Electrolyte] --> B[Double Layer Capacitance Cdl]
    B --> C[Charge Transfer Resistance Rct]
    C --> D[Metal Electrode Surface]
    D --> E[Half Cell Potential Vhc]
    F[Faradaic Current If] --> C
    G[Capacitive Current Ic] --> B
    H[External Measurement Circuit] --> D
    E --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the Nernst Equation and state its significance in the generation of bioelectric signals.

**Model Answer:**
The Nernst Equation gives the equilibrium transmembrane potential ($E$) developed across a selectively permeable membrane due to an ionic concentration gradient:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

**Significance:** It is the foundational equation describing how ionic gradients across excitable cell membranes generate the action potentials of the heart (ECG), brain (EEG), and muscle (EMG). Without the Nernst relation, no bioelectric signal could exist.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** List any three differences between polarizable and non-polarizable electrodes, and identify which type is preferred for clinical ECG recording.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Polarizable Electrode | Non-Polarizable Electrode |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Charge transfer | Displacement only (no electron transfer) | True faradaic (electron transfer) |
| DC offset | Large and unstable | Small and stable |
| Polarization noise | High | Very low |
| Example | Platinum | Ag/AgCl |
| ECG preference | Not suitable | **Preferred (Ag/AgCl)** |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, describe the generation of an ECG signal. Explain the significance of the P wave, QRS complex, and T wave in a normal ECG waveform.

**A(b) [7 Marks]:** Derive the Nernst Equation from thermodynamic first principles. Calculate the Nernst potential for Na⁺ at 37°C given $[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{inside}} = 12$ mM and $[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{outside}} = 145$ mM. Take $E^{0} = 0$ V.

**Model Solution for A(a):**

The ECG is generated by the rhythmic electrical depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle, picked up by surface electrodes, amplified by a differential amplifier, and recorded on a time base.

| Wave / Complex | Physiological Origin | Typical Duration | Clinical Significance |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (SA node firing → atrial contraction) | 80–100 ms | Atrial enlargement, rhythm disorders |
| **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal conduction delay | 120–200 ms | AV block assessment |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization (masked atrial repolarization) | 80–120 ms | Bundle branch blocks, ventricular hypertrophy |
| **ST segment** | Plateau phase of ventricular action potential | 80–120 ms | Ischemia, infarction (elevation/depression) |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | Electrolyte imbalances, ischemia |
| **U wave** | Purkinje fiber repolarization (often invisible) | ~200 ms | Hypokalemia indicator |

**Block Diagram:** [Refer to Section 4.3 ECG Lead System Architecture]

**Model Solution for A(b):**

**Derivation:** [Refer to Section 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation — 7 steps, all must be shown]

**Numerical Calculation:**

Step 1 — Identify constants: $T = 310$ K, $z = +1$, $E^{0} = 0$ V.

Step 2 — Apply the log10 form at body temperature:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0615}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{inside}}}\right)$$

Step 3 — Substitute:

$$E = 0 + \frac{0.0615}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right)$$

Step 4 — Compute ratio:

$$E = 0.0615 \cdot \log_{10}(12.083)$$

Step 5 — Evaluate logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(12.083) = 1.0822$$

Step 6 — Final answer:

$$E = 0.0615 \cdot 1.0822 = +0.0666 \text{ V} \approx +66.6 \text{ mV}$$

This is the Nernst equilibrium potential for Na⁺ — close to the peak of the cardiac action potential ($\approx +60$ mV).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**B(a) [7 Marks]:** Classify bioelectric electrodes in detail. Explain with diagrams the working principle of the Ag/AgCl electrode and justify why it is the standard choice for clinical ECG.

**B(b) [7 Marks]:** A cardiac cell has the following intracellular and extracellular concentrations at 37°C: $[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{in}} = 150$ mM, $[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{out}} = 4$ mM; $[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{in}} = 12$ mM, $[\text{Na}^{+}]_{\text{out}} = 145$ mM; $[\text{Cl}^{-}]_{\text{in}} = 4$ mM, $[\text{Cl}^{-}]_{\text{out}} = 120$ mM. Calculate the Nernst potentials for each ion. Which ion dominates the resting membrane potential, and why?

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Classification of Bioelectric Electrodes:**

1. **By Location:**
   - **Surface Electrodes** — Ag/AgCl disposable pads, used for ECG, EEG.
   - **Needle Electrodes** — Bipolar concentric needles for EMG.
   - **Microelectrodes** — Glass micropipettes with tip diameter $< 1$ μm for intracellular recording.
   - **Implantable Electrodes** — Pacemaker leads, DBS probes.

2. **By Electrochemical Behavior:**
   - **Polarizable** — Current is capacitive (displacement); e.g., Pt, stainless steel.
   - **Non-Polarizable** — Current is faradaic (charge transfer); e.g., Ag/AgCl.
   - **Hybrid** — Real electrodes with both components.

3. **By Reference Role:**
   - **Reference** — Provide stable baseline (e.g., SHE, Ag/AgCl).
   - **Working** — Measure the variable bioelectric potential.

**Working Principle of Ag/AgCl Electrode:**

The Ag/AgCl electrode consists of a silver wire coated with a layer of silver chloride, immersed in a KCl electrolyte gel. The half-cell reaction is:

$$\text{AgCl}_{(s)} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}^{-}_{(aq)}$$

This reaction is highly reversible, so the electrode behaves as a **non-polarizable** interface with a stable half-cell potential of $E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} \approx +0.222$ V vs. SHE.

**Justification for ECG Use:**
- **Low and stable half-cell potential** → minimal DC offset.
- **Non-polarizable** → low noise, especially in the 0.05–150 Hz ECG band.
- **Ionic–electronic transduction** is direct and reversible → no rectification distortion.
- **Disposable, safe, and inexpensive** → ideal for clinical workflow.

**Model Solution for B(b):**

**For K⁺ (z = +1):**

$$E_{K} = \frac{0.0615}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{150}\right) = 0.0615 \cdot (-1.5740) = -96.8 \text{ mV}$$

**For Na⁺ (z = +1):**

$$E_{Na} = \frac{0.0615}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right) = 0.0615 \cdot 1.0822 = +66.6 \text{ mV}$$

**For Cl⁻ (z = −1):**

$$E_{Cl} = \frac{0.0615}{-1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{120}{4}\right) = -0.0615 \cdot 1.4771 = -90.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Interpretation:**

At rest, cardiac cell membranes are most permeable to K⁺ (relative permeability $P_K \gg P_{Na} \gg P_{Cl}$). Since the Nernst potential for K⁺ ($-96.8$ mV) is closest to the experimentally measured resting membrane potential ($\approx -90$ mV), **K⁺ is the dominant ion governing the resting potential**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Sign of valence $z$:** For anions like Cl⁻, students often forget that $z$ is *negative* (−1), which inverts the sign of the computed potential. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Using 0.0591 instead of 0.0615** when the question specifies body temperature. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to include the units** in the final numerical answer (mV vs. V). **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Inverting the concentration ratio** (putting inside over outside instead of outside over inside) — flips the sign of the potential. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Skipping intermediate logarithmic steps** in derivations — KTU examiners expect *every* numerical substitution to be visible. **Lose up to 2 marks.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nernst Equation** quantifies the equilibrium transmembrane potential of a single ionic species across a selectively permeable membrane.
- **Two practical forms:** natural-log form (thermodynamic origin) and base-10 form (engineering convenience). The base-10 coefficient is **0.0615 V at 37°C** and **0.0591 V at 25°C**.
- **For monovalent ions (z = ±1)** at body temperature, the Nernst Equation simplifies to $E = \pm 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}(\text{ratio})$ mV.
- **Resting potential ≈ −90 mV** is dominated by K⁺ permeability, as the Nernst potential for K⁺ is the closest to the measured value.
- **Action potential peak ≈ +30 to +40 mV** is dominated by Na⁺ permeability, as $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.
- **Ag/AgCl is the gold standard** for clinical ECG electrodes because it is *non-polarizable* and has a *low, stable* half-cell potential of $\approx +0.222$ V vs. SHE.
- **ECG lead system** comprises 3 bipolar limb leads (I, II, III), 3 augmented unipolar limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF), and 6 precordial chest leads (V1–V6) — totaling **12 leads**.
- **Einthoven's Law:** $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ — a vector-closure identity that KTU examiners test frequently.
- **ECG waveform order:** P → QRS → T → (optional U); the QRS complex masks atrial repolarization due to its much larger amplitude.
- **Standard paper speed:** 25 mm/s; each small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s; each large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s.
- **Goldmann-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation** is the *multi-ion generalization* of the Nernst equation and is the more accurate predictor of the actual resting potential when multiple ion species contribute.
- **Polarizable vs non-polarizable:** A *perfectly polarizable* electrode transfers no real charge across the interface; a *perfectly non-polarizable* electrode does so without any overpotential. Real electrodes lie between these two ideal limits.
- **Sign convention in biology:** Potentials are always reported as *inside-relative-to-outside*; a negative value means the cell interior is electrically negative compared to the exterior.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMG and ERG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMG and ERG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electromyography (EMG)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition Block**
> **Electromyography (EMG)** is the electrodiagnostic technique concerned with the detection, recording, analysis, and clinical interpretation of the **bioelectric signals** generated during voluntary or stimulated contraction of skeletal muscle fibers. The recorded waveform is termed the **Electromyogram**, and the underlying electrical event is the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The String Orchestra of the Body"
Imagine a large orchestra (the skeletal muscle) made of thousands of tiny violin strings (muscle fibers). Each string is plucked by a section leader (the motor neuron). When a few strings are plucked, you hear a faint hum; when all sections play together, the sound is loud and rich. The **EMG machine acts like a microphone placed inside the concert hall** — it does not produce the music, it only *listens* to the electrical chatter that drives the strings. A normal muscle produces a clean, rhythmic hum; a damaged nerve produces noisy, irregular, or silent sections.

> [!NOTE] **Key Physiological Source**
> The EMG signal originates from the **depolarization and repolarization** of the sarcolemma of muscle fibers, summed spatially and temporally across an entire **motor unit** (one motor neuron + the muscle fibers it innervates).

### Standard Quantitative Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Signal Amplitude | **50 µV to 5 mV** |
| Frequency Bandwidth | **20 Hz to 10 kHz** |
| Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity | **3 to 6 m/s** |
| Duration of a Single MUAP | **2 to 15 ms** |
| Rise Time of MUAP | **30 to 500 µs** |
| Skin–Electrode Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** (after prep) |
| Power Line Interference | **50/60 Hz** (to be rejected) |

---

## 1.2 Electroretinography (ERG)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition Block**
> **Electroretinography (ERG)** is a non-invasive ophthalmological diagnostic procedure that measures the **electrical response of the retina** to a standardized light stimulus. The summed bioelectric field potential, captured via a corneal electrode, is the **Electroretinogram**, which represents the mass activity of millions of retinal cells.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Camera's Self-Diagnostic Test"
Think of the human eye as a digital camera sensor. When light hits a pixel, the pixel generates a tiny voltage. Now imagine a chip made of **130 million pixels (photoreceptors)**, each firing simultaneously in response to a camera flash. You cannot probe a single pixel, but you can measure the **summed current drawn from the entire chip**. That summed current is the **ERG**. A normal retina draws a predictable, well-shaped current curve; a diseased retina (e.g., in retinitis pigmentosa) draws a distorted or reduced curve.

> [!NOTE] **Key Physiological Source**
> The ERG originates from the **transmembrane ionic currents** flowing through the retinal layers — primarily the photoreceptors (rods and cones), the bipolar cells, the Müller (glial) cells, and the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) — in response to a precisely controlled flash of light.

### Standard Quantitative Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Signal Amplitude (b-wave) | **100 µV to 1 mV** |
| Signal Duration | **< 250 ms** per flash |
| Stimulus Standard | **1.5 to 3.0 cd·s/m²** (photopic) |
| Scotopic Stimulus | **0.01 to 0.1 cd·s/m²** |
| Corneal Electrode Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** |
| Recommended Dark Adaptation | **≥ 20 min** |
| Reference Standard | **ISCEV (International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision)** |

---

## 1.3 Comparative Overview

> [!NOTE] **Why Both Are Studied Together in Module 1**
> KTU groups EMG and ERG because both are **evoked/active bioelectric recording systems** that share an identical front-end chain: (Electrode → Differential Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Display). The student is expected to recognize this **common biomedical instrumentation template** across different organ systems.

| Feature | EMG | ERG |
|---|---|---|
| Tissue Recorded | Skeletal Muscle | Retina |
| Stimulus | Voluntary / Electrical | Light Flash |
| Signal Range | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **100 µV – 1 mV** |
| Frequency Band | **20 Hz – 10 kHz** | **0.1 Hz – 300 Hz** |
| Electrode Type | Surface / Needle | Corneal Contact Lens |
| Clinical Use | Neuropathy, Myopathy | Retinal Dystrophy, Glaucoma |
| Reference Standard | SENIAM / AAEM | ISCEV |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of EMG

### 2.1.1 Origin of the EMG Signal — Motor Unit Anatomy
The smallest functional contractile unit that can be electrically recorded is the **Motor Unit (MU)**. It comprises:

1. An **alpha motor neuron** (cell body in the spinal cord ventral horn)
2. Its **axon** traveling peripherally via a mixed peripheral nerve
3. The **neuromuscular junctions (NMJs)** at the end-plate
4. The **group of extrafusal muscle fibers** it innervates

When the motor neuron fires, a single depolarization wave travels down each axonal branch, triggers acetylcholine release at every NMJ, and produces a **muscle fiber action potential (MFAP)**. The surface or needle electrode records the **algebraic sum** of all these MFAPs, each delayed by the differing conduction distances — this sum is the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

### 2.1.2 Signal Decomposition Logic
* **Recruitment** — Gradual increase in the *number* of active motor units as force increases.
* **Rate Coding** — Gradual increase in the *firing frequency* of already-recruited motor units.
* **Synchronization** — Simultaneous firing of multiple MUs (rare in healthy muscle; pathological when excessive).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight**
> From an instrumentation standpoint, the EMG is a **superposition problem**. The recorded voltage $V(t)$ at any electrode is the **convolution** of the intracellular action potential shape with the volume-conductor impulse response. Therefore, EMG electrode placement, inter-electrode distance, and tissue filtering all dramatically alter the waveform.

### 2.1.3 EMG Recording Modes

| Mode | Electrode | Depth Recorded | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface EMG (sEMG)** | Ag/AgCl skin electrodes | Whole muscle, superficial | Kinesiology, prosthetics, ergonomics |
| **Needle EMG (Clinical)** | Concentric / Monopolar needle | Single motor unit territory | Neuropathy vs myopathy diagnosis |
| **Intramuscular (Fine Wire)** | Bipolar fine wire | Deep or small muscles | Dynamic motion studies |
| **Macro EMG** | Modified concentric | Entire motor unit | Motor unit size estimation |

### 2.1.4 Signal Conditioning Chain
1. **Pre-amplification** (gain ≈ 100) close to the electrode to lift signal above cable noise.
2. **Differential amplification** (gain ≈ 1000) for 50/60 Hz line rejection. Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) required: **> 90 dB**.
3. **Band-pass filtering** — 20 Hz high-pass to remove motion artifact, 10 kHz low-pass (anti-aliasing).
4. **Notch filter** at 50/60 Hz (Q ≈ 30).
5. **Rectification + Smoothing (RMS envelope)** for amplitude estimation.
6. **ADC** with at least **12-bit** resolution and sample rate **≥ 20 kS/s** for sEMG.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of ERG

### 2.2.1 The Cellular Origin of Each Wave
The ERG waveform is a composite of overlapping cellular currents, each peaking at a different latency. The student must memorize the *origin* of each peak because **the entire clinical interpretation rests on which wave is reduced, delayed, or absent.**

| Wave | Latency (ms) | Cellular Origin | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **a-wave** | **0 – 10 ms** | Photoreceptor hyperpolarization (outer segment current) | Rod/cone dystrophy |
| **b-wave** | **30 – 50 ms** | ON-bipolar cells + Müller (glial) cell K⁺ siphoning | Inner retinal disease, diabetic retinopathy |
| **c-wave** | **2 – 4 s** | Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE) c-wave current | RPE / chloroquine toxicity |
| **d-wave** | After light off | OFF-bipolar cells | Cone system assessment |
| **Scotopic threshold response (STR)** | **> 200 ms** | Rod-driven amacrine cells | Early glaucoma, rod dystrophy |
| **Photopic negative response (PhNR)** | **~ 60 ms after b** | Retinal ganglion cells | Glaucoma, optic neuropathy |

### 2.2.2 Stimulus Standardization — The ISCEV Protocol
The International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision (ISCEV) defines a **standard 6-step clinical protocol**:

1. **Dark-adapted 0.01 ERG** (rod response)
2. **Dark-adapted 3.0 ERG** (combined rod-cone, max response)
3. **Dark-adapted 3.0 oscillatory potentials (OPs)**
4. **Dark-adapted 10.0 ERG** (strong flash)
5. **Light-adapted 3.0 ERG** (cone response)
6. **Light-adapted 3.0 flicker ERG** (30 Hz, isolated cone)

> [!NOTE] **Why This Order?**
> Progression is from the **most sensitive** (dim, scotopic) to the **most intense** (flicker) stimulus. The patient's retina is dark-adapted once, then progressively light-adapted within the same session, minimizing total recording time and patient discomfort.

### 2.2.3 Types of ERG
* **Ganzfeld (Full-Field) ERG** — Stimulus illuminates the *entire* retina uniformly. Gold standard.
* **Focal / Foveal ERG** — Stimulus localized to the macula; records foveal cone activity.
* **Pattern ERG (PERG)** — Checkerboard pattern reversal; isolates **retinal ganglion cells**.
* **Multifocal ERG (mfERG)** — Topographical map of local cone responses (61–241 hexagons).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mandatory Memorization for ESE & Series Exams**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMG Amplitude (peak-to-peak) | $V_{p-p} \in [50\,\mu V,\; 5\,mV]$ | Surface or needle |
| 2 | EMG Bandwidth | $f \in [20\,Hz,\; 10\,kHz]$ | HP + LP filter corners |
| 3 | SNR of EMG amplifier | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB — minimum 20 dB at input |
| 4 | CMRR | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)$ | **> 90 dB** for EMG |
| 5 | Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity | $CV = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t}$ | m/s; $d$ = inter-electrode spacing |
| 6 | RMS Amplitude (sEMG envelope) | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$ | V; computed over sliding window |
| 7 | Mean Rectified Value (MRV) | $MRV = \dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_{0}^{T} \vert v(t) \vert\, dt$ | V; computationally cheaper than RMS |
| 8 | Median Frequency (MDF) | $\int_{0}^{f_{m}} P(f)\, df = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\int_{0}^{\infty} P(f)\, df$ | Hz; fatigue indicator |
| 9 | ERG b-wave amplitude | $A_{b} \in [100\,\mu V,\; 1\,mV]$ | Measured from a-wave trough to b-wave peak |
| 10 | ERG a-wave implicit time | $t_{a} \in [0,\; 10\,ms]$ | Delay of a-wave trough from flash onset |
| 11 | Photopic stimulus intensity | $L = 3.0\;\dfrac{cd\cdot s}{m^{2}}$ | ISCEV standard flash |
| 12 | Corneal electrode DC offset | $V_{DC} < 1\,mV$ | Must be within amplifier range |
| 13 | Light Energy → Photon flux | $N_{ph} = \dfrac{E \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$ | photons; $h$ = Planck const., $c$ = speed of light |
| 14 | Beer–Lambert (tissue light absorption) | $I(x) = I_{0}\,e^{-\alpha x}$ | $\alpha$ = absorption coeff. of retinal layers |
| 15 | Retinal Illuminance | $E_{ret} = \pi \cdot L_{source} \cdot \tau$ | lux; $\tau$ = ocular media transmittance |

> [!NOTE] **Pitfall Alert**
> When writing $|v(t)|$ in KTU answer sheets, students often write $\vert v(t) \vert$ with the typographic vertical bar; this is correct. However, when you transcribe such an expression into a *markdown table cell* (as the KTU answer-portal uses), the **single vertical pipe `|` will break the markdown table**. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Beyond the Exam Hall**
> * **EMG**: Prosthetic limb control (decode finger intent from sEMG), exoskeleton actuation, ergonomic risk assessment in industry, sports biomechanics, myoelectric speech recognition, silent-speech communication.
> * **ERG**: Drug toxicity screening (chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine), pediatric cataract pre-surgery retinal assessment, gene therapy efficacy trials (e.g., Luxturna for RPE65), AI-based early glaucoma detection, telemedicine retinal screening in rural Kerala (KHEP & IKEB programs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Amplifier CMRR for EMG Front-End

> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently asks students to prove that the **higher the CMRR, the better the line-noise rejection** in EMG and ERG systems. The derivation connects the textbook concept to the *measurement chain*.

### Step 0 — Define the Two Inputs
A differential bio-amplifier has two inputs:

$$V_{1} = V_{CM} + \dfrac{V_{d}}{2}, \qquad V_{2} = V_{CM} - \dfrac{V_{d}}{2}$$

where $V_{CM}$ is the common-mode voltage (chiefly the 50 Hz mains pickup) and $V_{d}$ is the differential biopotential (the actual EMG/ERG signal).

### Step 1 — Output of the Amplifier
The output is the linear combination of the two inputs weighted by the differential gain $A_{d}$ and the common-mode gain $A_{c}$:

$$V_{out} = A_{d}\,V_{d} + A_{c}\,V_{CM}$$

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$
Adding and subtracting the input definitions:

$$V_{out} = A_{d}\,(V_{1} - V_{2}) + A_{c}\,\left(\dfrac{V_{1} + V_{2}}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into a Single Formula

$$\boxed{V_{out} = A_{d}\,V_{d}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2\,CMRR}\right)}$$

where we have used the standard definition of Common-Mode Rejection Ratio:

$$CMRR = \dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}$$

### Step 4 — Conversion to Decibels (KTU Format)

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
For an EMG pre-amplifier: $A_{d} = 1000$, $A_{c} = 0.1$. Then:

$$CMRR = \dfrac{1000}{0.1} = 10{,}000$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20\,\log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80\,dB$$

> [!NOTE] **Examiner Expectation**
> For an EMG system, KTU expects a CMRR of **at least 80 dB (preferably > 90 dB)** so that 50 Hz mains interference (which can be several hundred millivolts at the skin) does not swamp a few-hundred-µV EMG signal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV) from Surface EMG

### Step 0 — Set Up the Geometry
Two surface electrodes (E1, E2) are placed along the muscle fiber direction at a known inter-electrode distance $d$ (typically 10–30 mm). The same action potential passes under E1 first, then E2 after a delay $\Delta t$.

### Step 1 — Conduction Velocity Definition

$$CV = \dfrac{\text{Distance travelled}}{\text{Time elapsed}} = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 2 — Express $\Delta t$ from the Cross-Correlation Function
In practice, the delay is estimated by finding the lag $\tau$ that maximizes the cross-correlation $R_{12}(\tau)$ between the two electrode signals $x_{1}(t)$ and $x_{2}(t)$:

$$R_{12}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x_{1}(t)\,x_{2}(t+\tau)\,dt$$

The peak of $R_{12}(\tau)$ occurs at $\tau^{\*} = \Delta t$.

### Step 3 — Final KTU Form

$$\boxed{CV = \dfrac{d}{\tau^{*}} \;\; \dfrac{m}{s}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
Given $d = 0.020\;m$ and a measured delay $\tau^{\*} = 5\;ms = 5 \times 10^{-3}\;s$:

$$CV = \dfrac{0.020}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.0\;\dfrac{m}{s}$$

> This is the **typical healthy value for biceps brachii** at skin temperature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RMS Value of the sEMG Envelope

### Step 0 — Why Envelope?
Raw sEMG is bipolar (positive and negative excursions) and oscillates rapidly. To extract the **muscle activation level**, we compute a sliding RMS envelope.

### Step 1 — Definition Over a Window of Length $T$

$$V_{RMS}(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_{t}^{t+T} v^{2}(\tau)\, d\tau}$$

### Step 2 — Discrete-Time Form (for ADC implementation)

$$V_{RMS}[n] = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\!\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v^{2}[n-k]}$$

where $N$ is the number of samples inside the sliding window.

### Step 3 — Relationship to Force (KTU Bonus)
For isometric, sub-maximal contractions, the sEMG RMS amplitude is **approximately linear** with force:

$$V_{RMS} \approx k\cdot F + b$$

where $k$ is the gain (V/N) and $b$ is the resting baseline noise. This is the foundation of **myoelectric force estimation** in prosthetics.

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
For $N = 200$ samples within a 10 ms window, with a sample of $v^{2}[k]$ values: $[10, 20, 30, ..., 20]$ (in µV²):

$$\sum v^{2} = 2500\;\mu V^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2500}{200}} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.54\;\mu V$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Photon Flux in ERG Stimulation

### Step 0 — Why This Matters
