**Problem:** An EEG preamplifier has differential gain $A_d = 10^4$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 1$. Calculate the CMRR. If the common-mode interference (50 Hz mains) is $V_{cm} = 1\ mV$, what is the output interference?

**Step 1:** Calculate CMRR.

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^4)$$

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2:** Common-mode output.

$$V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 1 \times 1\ \text{mV} = 1\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3:** Compare to EEG signal at output.

$$V_{out,EEG} = A_d \times V_{EEG} = 10^4 \times 50\ \mu V = 0.5\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Effective SNR improvement due to CMRR.

$$\frac{V_{out,EEG}}{V_{out,cm}} = \frac{0.5\ \text{V}}{1\ \text{mV}} = 500 \approx 54\ \text{dB}$$

This shows why a **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** is the engineering minimum for clinical EEG.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EEG Instrumentation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCALP EEG SOURCES\n Pyramidal Neurons\n 10 to 100 microV] --> B[ELECTRODES\n Ag/AgCl Surface Disks]
    B --> C[ELECTRODE PASTE\n Ionic Conduction Bridge]
    C --> D[PREAMP STAGE 1\n Instrumentation Amplifier\n Gain 1000 CMRR 100 dB]
    D --> E[ISOLATION AMP\n Patient Safety Barrier]
    E --> F[HPF FILTER\n fc = 0.1 Hz\n Removes DC Offset]
    F --> G[MAIN AMPLIFIER\n Variable Gain\n 100 to 10000]
    G --> H[LPF FILTER\n fc = 100 Hz\n Removes EMG and HF Noise]
    H --> I[NOTCH FILTER\n 50 Hz Rejection\n Twin T or Active]
    I --> J[ADC\n 16 bit 1 kSps]
    J --> K[DISPLAY MODULE\n LCD or Paper Chart]
    J --> L[STORAGE\n SD Card or PC Database]
    J --> M[ANALYSIS\n FFT Spectral View]
```

### 4.2 10-20 Electrode Placement System — Top-Down Schematic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONTAL["FRONTAL LOBE REGION"]
        Fp1[Fp1 Odd Left]:::leftSide
        Fpz[Fpz Midline Zero]:::midSide
        Fp2[Fp2 Even Right]:::rightSide
        F7[F7 Left Temporal]:::leftSide
        F3[F3 Left Frontal]:::leftSide
        Fz[Fz Midline Frontal]:::midSide
        F4[F4 Right Frontal]:::rightSide
        F8[F8 Right Temporal]:::rightSide
    end
    subgraph CENTRAL["CENTRAL LOBE REGION"]
        T3[T3 Left Temporal Mid]:::leftSide
        C3[C3 Left Central]:::leftSide
        Cz[Cz Midline Central]:::midSide
        C4[C4 Right Central]:::rightSide
        T4[T4 Right Temporal Mid]:::rightSide
    end
    subgraph PARIETAL["PARIETAL LOBE REGION"]
        T5[T5 Left Temporal Post]:::leftSide
        P3[P3 Left Parietal]:::leftSide
        Pz[Pz Midline Parietal]:::midSide
        P4[P4 Right Parietal]:::rightSide
        T6[T6 Right Temporal Post]:::rightSide
    end
    subgraph OCCIPITAL["OCCIPITAL LOBE REGION"]
        O1[O1 Left Occipital]:::leftSide
        Oz[Oz Midline Occipital]:::midSide
        O2[O2 Right Occipital]:::rightSide
    end
    subgraph REFERENCE["REFERENCE ELECTRODES"]
        A1[A1 Left Ear]:::refSide
        A2[A2 Right Ear]:::refSide
        Cz -.reference.-> A1
        Cz -.reference.-> A2
    end
    classDef leftSide fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef rightSide fill:#d6e0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef midSide fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef refSide fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Cascaded Frequency Response of EEG Filters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input EEG Spectrum\n 0.1 Hz to 100 Hz\n Center 10 Hz] --> B[Stage 1 HPF\n Cutoff 0.1 Hz\n Slope 20 dB decade]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Main Amplifier\n Gain 1000]
    C --> D[Stage 3 LPF\n Cutoff 100 Hz\n Slope 20 dB decade]
    D --> E[Stage 4 Notch Filter\n 50 Hz Rejection\n Depth 30 dB]
    E --> F[Clean EEG Output\n 0.1 to 100 Hz\n 50 Hz Attenuated]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define EEG. List the five standard EEG rhythms with their frequency ranges.**
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Model Answer:**
Electroencephalography (EEG) is the recording of the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain via electrodes placed on the scalp, originating primarily from the summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons.
The five standard EEG rhythms are:
- **Delta (δ):** 0.5 – 4 Hz, deep sleep
- **Theta (θ):** 4 – 8 Hz, drowsiness
- **Alpha (α):** 8 – 13 Hz, relaxed wakefulness (eyes closed)
- **Beta (β):** 13 – 30 Hz, active thinking
- **Gamma (γ):** > 30 Hz, cognitive processing

**Q2. What is the 10-20 electrode placement system? Why is it used in EEG recording?**
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**Model Answer:**
The 10-20 system is an internationally standardized method for placing scalp electrodes at 10% or 20% intervals along reference lines drawn between skull landmarks (nasion, inion, and preauricular points). It is used because it ensures reproducible electrode placement across patients and clinical sessions, allows standardized reporting, and provides systematic coverage of all cortical regions.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Attempt ANY ONE of the following]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Explain the complete EEG instrumentation block diagram in detail, including electrode types, preamplifier specifications, and filter stages.**
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Functional Stages [7 Marks]**

The EEG instrumentation system consists of the following cascaded stages:

1. **Electrode Stage:** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes placed on the scalp using conductive paste. They convert ionic currents into electronic signals. Ag/AgCl is preferred due to its stable low-offset half-cell potential (~ 220 mV).

2. **Differential Preamplifier Stage:** A three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier with differential gain typically 1000, providing very high input impedance (> 100 MΩ) and very high CMRR (> 100 dB) to reject mains-borne common-mode interference.

3. **Isolation Amplifier:** Provides galvanic isolation between the patient and the mains-powered circuitry, ensuring patient safety (max leakage current < 10 µA).

4. **Bandpass Filter Stage:** A high-pass filter with $f_c = 0.1\ Hz$ removes DC electrode offset; a low-pass filter with $f_c = 100\ Hz$ removes high-frequency EMG interference and RF noise.

5. **50 Hz Notch Filter:** Twin-T or active notch topology providing > 30 dB attenuation at the Indian 50 Hz mains frequency.

6. **Main Amplifier and Display:** Variable gain amplifier (100–10,000) drives a chart recorder, oscilloscope, or ADC for digital storage and FFT analysis.

**(b) Electrode Types and Amplifier Specifications [7 Marks]**

**Electrode Types:**
- **Surface (Disk) Electrodes:** Most common clinical type, fixed with conductive paste.
- **Needle Electrodes:** Used in ICU/operation theatres; inserted subcutaneously for better contact.
- **Depth/Sphenoidal Electrodes:** Invasive, used in epilepsy surgery for localizing seizure foci.

**Preamplifier Specifications:**
- **CMRR:** ≥ 100 dB (rejects 50 Hz mains interference)
- **Input Impedance:** ≥ 100 MΩ (prevents signal loading)
- **Noise:** < 2 µV peak-to-peak
- **Gain:** 1000 – 10,000

**Filter Design Equations:**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

Choosing $C = 1\ \mu F$ and target $f_{c,HPF} = 0.1\ Hz$ gives $R = 1.59\ M\Omega$. Choosing the same $C$ for the LPF at 100 Hz gives $R = 1.59\ k\Omega$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): With a neat diagram, explain the international 10-20 electrode placement system for EEG recording. Describe the salient features of EEG waveforms and their clinical significance.**
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The 10-20 Electrode Placement System [7 Marks]**

The 10-20 system was standardized by the International Federation of Societies of Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology. Four anatomical landmarks define the coordinate system:
- **Nasion** (front)
- **Inion** (back)
- **Left Preauricular Point**
- **Right Preauricular Point**

These landmarks are connected to form reference lines (nasion-to-inion and preauricular-to-preauricular), which are then divided at 10% and 20% intervals to define electrode positions.

**Naming Convention:**
- **Letter** → Lobe: F (frontal), P (parietal), T (temporal), O (occipital), C (central), Fp (frontopolar), A (auricular reference)
- **Number** → Lateral position: Even = right hemisphere, Odd = left hemisphere, z = midline
- Examples: **Fz** (frontal midline), **C3** (left central), **O2** (right occipital)

A standard 21-electrode clinical EEG includes: Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, Oz, O2, plus references A1 and A2.

**(b) EEG Waveforms and Clinical Significance [7 Marks]**

| Rhythm | Frequency | Clinical State |
|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep; in adults awake, indicates organic brain disease |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness; focal theta may indicate subcortical lesions |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness; blocking with eye opening (Berger effect) |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Active concentration; enhanced by sedatives like benzodiazepines |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Cognitive binding; cross-modal sensory integration |

**Clinical Applications:**
- **Epilepsy diagnosis:** 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges (Petit Mal)
- **Sleep staging:** Polysomnography uses delta/theta dominance
- **Brain death confirmation:** Electrocerebral silence (ECS) at < 2 µV
- **Anesthesia depth:** BIS index derived from EEG

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and where marks are deducted):**
>
> 1. **Confusing EEG origin with action potentials:** EEG records **postsynaptic potentials**, not action potentials. Saying "EEG = action potentials of neurons" will lose **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
>
> 2. **Incorrect rhythm frequency ranges:** Mixing up Alpha (8–13 Hz) with Beta (13–30 Hz) is the single most common error. Memorize the bands as **"Delta-Theta-Alpha-Beta-Gamma = DTABG"** ascending.
>
> 3. **Wrong electrode numbering:** Forgetting that **even numbers = RIGHT** hemisphere and **odd numbers = LEFT** loses **1 mark** in electrode placement questions.
>
> 4. **Omitting CMRR value:** Always quote **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** with justification when explaining preamplifier specs. A generic "high CMRR" without numerical value loses marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the notch filter:** In filter-stage questions, students often mention HPF and LPF but omit the **50 Hz notch filter** — easy 1-mark loss.
>
> 6. **No units in formula derivations:** Always state final numerical answers **with units** (µV, Hz, dB, MΩ).

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EEG Instrumentation**

- 🔹 **EEG** = Summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons; **NOT** action potentials.
- 🔹 Amplitude range: **10 µV – 100 µV**; Bandwidth: **0.1 Hz – 100 Hz**.
- 🔹 First recorded by **Hans Berger** (1924/1929).
- 🔹 **Five rhythms (DTABG):** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30 Hz).
- 🔹 Alpha rhythm **attenuates (blocks)** upon eye opening — this is **Berger effect**.
- 🔹 Preferred electrode: **Ag/AgCl** for stable half-cell potential.
- 🔹 **10-20 system:** 10% or 20% intervals; Letters = lobes; **Even = Right, Odd = Left, z = Midline**.
- 🔹 Preamplifier must have: **CMRR ≥ 100 dB**, **Input Z ≥ 100 MΩ**, **Noise < 2 µV**.
- 🔹 Filter chain: **HPF (0.1 Hz) → Main Amp → LPF (100 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → ADC**.
- 🔹 Three-op-amp **instrumentation amplifier** topology is standard for preamplifier.
- 🔹 **Galvanic isolation** between patient and mains is mandatory for safety.
- 🔹 Clinical uses: **Epilepsy, Sleep studies (PSG), Brain death confirmation, BCI, Anesthesia (BIS)**.
- 🔹 **CMRR formula:** $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$; **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** is engineering minimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrode Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrode Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Electrode Placement

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrodes** are the conductive transducers that bridge the ionic current of the human body to the electronic current of the measurement instrument. **Electrode placement** is the precise, anatomically-standardized positioning of these electrodes on the body surface to faithfully capture bioelectric signals such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and EOG.

### Formal Academic Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *electrode placement* refers to the **standardized positioning and orientation of surface (or invasive) electrodes** on a biological subject such that the recorded biopotential waveform is **reproducible, diagnostically valid, and free from motion artifact or cross-talk**. The placement scheme defines:

- **Anatomical landmarks** (e.g., bony prominences, the 10th rib, the inion, the nasion).
- **Inter-electrode distances** expressed as a percentage of skull circumference (EEG) or limb length (ECG).
- **Reference and driven configurations** (unipolar, bipolar, referential).
- **Skin preparation protocol** (abrasion, gel application, hair removal).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine a microphone placed randomly in a concert hall — it will pick up echoes, noise, and instruments that you don't want. Now imagine placing **five calibrated microphones in fixed, agreed-upon positions** — every recording made by every studio sounds comparable. Electrode placement is exactly this for the body's electrical symphony.

Think of the heart as an orchestra and the ECG electrodes as **fixed microphones in the auditorium**:
- Place them in the wrong seats → the symphony sounds distorted and cannot be compared between patients.
- Place them in standardized seats (e.g., **RA on right arm, LA on left arm, LL on left leg**) → every cardiologist in the world interprets the same recording the same way.

Similarly, in **EEG**, the **International 10–20 System** places electrodes at exactly **10 % or 20 %** of the skull's measured circumference, so that a recording in Tokyo can be compared to one in Kerala.

### Key Standards and Constants in Electrode Placement

- **Standard ECG gain**: **10 mm/mV** on the chart recorder.
- **Paper speed**: **25 mm/s**.
- **EEG reference**: **International 10–20 Electrode Placement System** (Jasper, 1958).
- **Skin–electrode impedance target**: **< 5 kΩ** (clinical) and **< 2 kΩ** (research).
- **Half-cell potential** of a standard Ag/AgCl electrode: **≈ +0.223 V** vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE).
- **Electrolyte gel conductivity** (typical NaCl gel): **~ 1.6 S/m**.

### Why Electrode Placement Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 3 of PEEIT752 frequently tests **placement diagrams** (ECG 12-lead, EEG 10–20) and **mathematical derivations of lead vectors** (Einthoven's triangle, Wilson Central Terminal, augmented leads). Memorize the **anatomical positions AND the underlying vector mathematics** — both appear in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 2. The Three Pillars of Correct Electrode Placement

| Pillar | Description | Clinical / Engineering Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Anatomical Landmarking** | Use bony landmarks (clavicle, ribs, inion, nasion, preauricular points). | Reproducibility across operators and patients. |
| **Skin Preparation** | Abrade stratum corneum, clean with alcohol, apply electrolytic gel. | Lower contact impedance, reduce motion artifact, raise SNR. |
| **Lead Configuration** | Choose bipolar (lead I, II, III), augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF), or precordial (V1–V6) topology. | Extract the desired vector component of the cardiac/neuronal dipole. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3 — Electrode Placement

## 1. Deep Theoretical Analysis — From Skin Interface to Lead Vector

### 1.1 The Skin–Electrode Interface (Why placement is more than just "sticking it on")

Every biopotential electrode sits at the interface of **three distinct electrical media**:

1. **The metallic conductor** (Ag, AgCl, Pt, Au, stainless steel).
2. **The electrolytic gel** (typically a viscous NaCl or KCl paste).
3. **The body tissue / skin electrolyte** (ionic fluid in the dermis and subcutaneous layers).

When two dissimilar phases meet, a **half-cell potential (E₀)** is generated across the metal–electrolyte boundary. This is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E = E_{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{ox}}}{a_{\text{red}}} \right)
$$

where:
- $E$ = electrode potential at temperature $T$
- $E_0$ = standard electrode potential
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ (Universal Gas Constant)
- $F = 96485 \; \text{C/mol}$ (Faraday's Constant)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per ion
- $a_{\text{ox}}, a_{\text{red}}$ = activities of oxidized and reduced species

At body temperature (**T = 310 K**) the thermal voltage simplifies to:

$$
\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71 \; \text{mV}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because both electrodes in a biopotential pair contribute their own half-cell potential, **identical materials, identical gel, and identical skin preparation** are mandatory. Otherwise the two half-cells do not cancel, and a large **DC offset** corrupts the recording.

### 1.2 Equivalent Circuit of the Electrode–Skin Interface

The interface is accurately modelled by the classic **GEDC** equivalent circuit (Geddes & Baker):

- $R_s$ : series resistance of gel and tissue
- $R_d$ : parallel leakage resistance of the double layer
- $C_d$ : capacitance of the electrochemical double layer (Helmholtz layer)
- $E_{hc}$ : half-cell potential source

When **placement is poor** (dry skin, hair, dead stratum corneum) → $R_d$ drops, $C_d$ rises → the high-pass corner frequency shifts and **low-frequency ECG components (ST segment)** are filtered out — a clinically dangerous artifact.

### 1.3 ECG Electrode Placement — The 12-Lead System

The **12-lead ECG** is actually **10 electrodes** producing **12 views** of the heart's electrical dipole $\vec{H}$:

| Electrode | Placement Site | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **RA** | Right arm (just below clavicle) | Reference limb electrode |
| **LA** | Left arm (just below clavicle) | Limb electrode |
| **LL** | Left leg (below rib cage, clean fleshy area) | Limb electrode |
| **RL** | Right leg (anywhere on right leg) | Driven Right Leg (DRL) ground |
| **V1** | 4th intercostal space, right sternal border | Septal view |
| **V2** | 4th intercostal space, left sternal border | Septal view |
| **V3** | Midway between V2 and V4 | Anterior wall |
| **V4** | 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line | Apex / Anterior |
| **V5** | Anterior axillary line, same level as V4 | Lateral wall |
| **V6** | Mid-axillary line, same level as V4 | Lateral wall |

### 1.4 ECG Lead Vector Theory — Einthoven's Triangle

Einthoven hypothesized that the **three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL)** form the vertices of an **equilateral triangle** on the torso, and that the **heart's electrical axis $\vec{H}$** projects onto the sides of this triangle.

The **bipolar limb leads** are then defined as potential differences:

$$
I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}
$$

$$
II = V_{LL} - V_{RA}
$$

$$
III = V_{LL} - V_{LA}
$$

**Einthoven's Law** (the KTU examiner's favourite 1-liner):

$$
II = I + III
$$

This identity is purely **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the loop RA–LA–LL:

$$
(V_{LL} - V_{RA}) = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})
$$

### 1.5 Wilson Central Terminal (WCT) and the Augmented Leads

A **unipolar precordial lead** measures the potential at a chest electrode against an **indifferent reference**. The reference is the **Wilson Central Terminal**:

$$
V_W = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}
$$

Because $V_W$ averages the three limbs, its potential is approximately **constant**, acting as a virtual ground for the chest electrode.

Goldberger observed that the WCT attenuates the chest signal by ~ 1.5× and proposed **augmented leads**:

$$
aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}
$$

$$
aVL = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}
$$

$$
aVF = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}
$$

These three augmented leads, together with the three bipolar leads (I, II, III) and the six precordial leads (V1–V6), constitute the **complete 12-lead ECG**.

### 1.6 EEG — The International 10–20 System

The **10–20 System** (Jasper, 1958) places 21 electrodes on the scalp using three reference measurements:

- **Nasion (Nz)** — bridge of the nose, between the eyes.
- **Inion (Iz)** — bony protuberance at the back of the skull.
- **Left & Right Preauricular (A1, A2)** — in front of each ear.

The longitudinal distance **Nz–Iz** and the lateral distance **A1–A2** are divided into **10 % – 20 % – 20 % – 20 % – 20 % – 10 %** segments.

For a longitudinal distance $L$, the coronal plane electrodes (Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz) are placed at:

$$
x_{Fz} = 0.10 \cdot L, \quad x_{Cz} = 0.50 \cdot L, \quad x_{Pz} = 0.90 \cdot L
$$

Electrode label convention:
- **F** = Frontal, **C** = Central, **P** = Parietal, **O** = Occipital, **T** = Temporal.
- Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8) → right hemisphere.
- Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7) → left hemisphere.
- **z** = midline.

### 1.7 EMG Electrode Placement (Brief)

- **Surface EMG (sEMG)** — bipolar Ag/AgCl electrodes placed parallel to muscle fibres, **inter-electrode distance 20–30 mm**, with a reference on a bony prominence or electrically inactive tissue.
- **Needle EMG** — concentric needle inserted into the muscle belly; no placement standardization, but **muscle-specific entry points** are taught (e.g., biceps brachii at 1/3 from distal tendon).

---

## 2. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every equation you must memorize for **Electrode Placement** in KTU Module 3 examinations.

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Common KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E_0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}}\right)$ | Nernst half-cell potential | Define E₀ of Ag/AgCl electrode |
| 2 | $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71 \;\text{mV}$ at 310 K | Thermal voltage | Body-temperature substitution |
| 3 | $I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ | Lead I | ECG lead derivation |
| 4 | $II = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ | Lead II | ECG lead derivation |
| 5 | $III = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ | Lead III | ECG lead derivation |
| 6 | $II = I + III$ | Einthoven's Law | KVL on Einthoven's triangle |
| 7 | $V_W = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$ | Wilson Central Terminal | Reference for V1–V6 |
| 8 | $aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$ | Augmented Vector Right | Goldberger's lead |
| 9 | $aVL = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}$ | Augmented Vector Left | Goldberger's lead |
| 10 | $aVF = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}$ | Augmented Vector Foot | Goldberger's lead |
| 11 | $\vec{H}_F = \vec{I} \cos\theta$ | Frontal plane projection | Cardiac axis |
| 12 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{aVF}{aVL}\right)$ | Cardiac axis angle | Diagnosis of axis deviation |
| 13 | $x_{\text{elec}} = k \cdot L$ where $k \in \{0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.90\}$ | 10–20 EEG placement | EEG layout |
| 14 | $Z_{\text{skin}} < 5 \;\text{k}\Omega$ | Impedance target | Skin prep requirement |
| 15 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | Electrode placement quality check |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall** — Do not confuse the **lead vector** (a unit vector describing orientation of a lead in space) with the **voltage measured** by the lead. Many students write "Lead II = 1.0 mV" — that is a *voltage*, not a *vector*.

---

## 3. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **12-lead ECG placement** is used in every coronary care unit (CCU) worldwide for **myocardial infarction (MI) diagnosis** — e.g., ST elevation in V1–V4 indicates **anterior MI**.
- **Holter monitoring** uses modified electrode placement (Mason-Likar) to minimize motion artifact during 24-h ambulatory ECG recording.
- **EEG 10–20 placement** is mandatory in **epilepsy monitoring units** for seizure localization.
- **EMG placement** at SENIAM-recommended sites is the global standard in **prosthetic control** and **rehabilitation robotics**.
- **Defibrillator pad placement** (apex–sternum) relies on the same dipole-vector concept: maximal current density must pass through the ventricular myocardium.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3 — Electrode Placement

## 1. Step-by-Step Derivations

### Derivation 1 — Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

We are given three limb electrodes **RA, LA, LL** at potentials $V_{RA}, V_{LA}, V_{LL}$.

The three bipolar leads are defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lead I}  &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} \\
\text{Lead II} &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\
\text{Lead III} &= V_{LL} - V_{LA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1**: Add Lead I and Lead III algebraically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I + III &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\
        &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\
        &= \text{Lead II}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**:

$$
\boxed{II = I + III}
$$

This is **Einthoven's Law** and is a direct consequence of KVL on the closed loop RA → LA → LL → RA.

---

### Derivation 2 — Wilson Central Terminal

We seek a reference potential $V_W$ that is **independent of the cardiac dipole $\vec{H}$**.

For a dipole $\vec{H}$ at the centre of the equilateral Einthoven triangle, the projected potentials on each vertex are equal in magnitude but **120° apart in phase**. Their vector sum is therefore **zero**.

Because $V_W$ is an *average* of three scalar potentials, the dipole component that is **common to all three limbs** is rejected:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_W &= \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} \\
    &= \frac{(V_{RA}^{0} + h) + (V_{LA}^{0} + h) + (V_{LL}^{0} + h)}{3} \\
    &= \frac{V_{RA}^{0} + V_{LA}^{0} + V_{LL}^{0}}{3} + h
\end{aligned}
$$

where $h$ is the common dipole component and $V_{i}^{0}$ are the offset potentials.

For an ideal symmetric placement, the offsets $V_{i}^{0}$ are equal and the dipole component $h$ is rejected when subtracted from any single chest potential.

---

### Derivation 3 — Augmented Lead (Goldberger) — aVR

The augmented lead $aVR$ is defined such that it views the heart from the **right shoulder**.

Using the augmented Goldberg definition:

$$
aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}
$$

**Step 1**: Express in terms of Lead I and Lead III.

We know $V_{LA} = V_{RA} + I$ and $V_{LL} = V_{LA} + III = V_{RA} + I + III$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
aVR &= V_{RA} - \frac{(V_{RA} + I) + (V_{RA} + I + III)}{2} \\
    &= V_{RA} - \frac{2 V_{RA} + 2 I + III}{2} \\
    &= V_{RA} - V_{RA} - I - \frac{III}{2} \\
    &= -\!\left(I + \frac{III}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

But from Einthoven's Law, $I + III = II \;\Rightarrow\; III = II - I$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
aVR &= -\!\left(I + \frac{II - I}{2}\right) \\
    &= -\!\left(\frac{2I + II - I}{2}\right) \\
    &= -\!\left(\frac{I + II}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{aVR = -\frac{I + II}{2}}
$$

By analogous algebra one obtains the practical clinician-friendly relations:

$$
\boxed{aVL = \frac{I - III}{2}}, \qquad \boxed{aVF = \frac{II + III}{2}}
$$

These three identities are **examination favourites** in KTU Part B derivations.

---

### Derivation 4 — Cardiac Axis Angle from Leads I and aVF

The frontal-plane cardiac axis $\theta$ is the angle the mean QRS vector $\vec{H}$ makes with the horizontal (Lead I axis).

The horizontal component of $\vec{H}$ is given by Lead I. The vertical component is given by Lead aVF (which points downward at +90°).

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{\text{vertical component}}{\text{horizontal component}}\right) \\
       &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{aVF}{I}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result**:

$$
\boxed{\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{aVF}{I}\right)}
$$

Clinical ranges (frequently asked):

- **Normal axis**: $-30^{\circ} \leq \theta \leq +90^{\circ}$
- **Left axis deviation**: $-30^{\circ} < \theta < -90^{\circ}$
- **Right axis deviation**: $+90^{\circ} < \theta < +180^{\circ}$

---

### Derivation 5 — EEG 10–20 System Spacing

Given the **longitudinal distance** $L = d_{Nz \rightarrow Iz}$ (nasion to inion), the system specifies electrode positions at fractions $0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.90$.

For a typical adult skull with $L = 36 \;\text{cm}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{Fp1/Fp2} &= 0.10 \cdot 36 = 3.6 \;\text{cm} \\
x_{F3/F4}  &= 0.30 \cdot 36 = 10.8 \;\text{cm} \\
x_{C3/C4}  &= 0.50 \cdot 36 = 18.0 \;\text{cm} \\
x_{P3/P4}  &= 0.70 \cdot 36 = 25.2 \;\text{cm} \\
x_{O1/O2}  &= 0.90 \cdot 36 = 32.4 \;\text{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, the lateral distance $W = d_{A1 \rightarrow A2}$ is divided as $0.10, 0.20, 0.20, 0.20, 0.20, 0.10$ for the **F7, T3, T5, F8, T4, T6, Fp1/Fp2, F3/F4, C3/C4, P3/P4, O1/O2** rows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of **10 % at the perimeter** and **20 % between electrodes** avoids placing an electrode over a bony suture (e.g., the coronal suture at Cz), which would distort impedance and the recorded EEG.

---

## 2. Analytical Matrix — Placement Quality vs. Signal Quality

| Skin Prep Step | Resulting Impedance | Resulting SNR | Artifact Type Avoided |
|---|---|---|---|
| **No prep** | $50 - 200 \;\text{k}\Omega$ | < 10 dB | Mains pickup, baseline wander |
| **Alcohol wipe** | $20 - 50 \;\text{k}\Omega$ | 10 – 20 dB | Sweat salt bridges |
| **Abrasion + gel** | $2 - 5 \;\text{k}\Omega$ | 20 – 40 dB | Motion, baseline wander |
| **Needle + gel** | $0.5 - 2 \;\text{k}\Omega$ | > 40 dB | All but EMG needle motion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3 — Electrode Placement

## 1. Mermaid Block Diagram — ECG 12-Lead Placement Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RA[RA Right Arm Electrode] --> LIMB_LIMB[Limb Lead Network]
    LA[LA Left Arm Electrode] --> LIMB_LIMB
    LL[LL Left Leg Electrode] --> LIMB_LIMB
    RL[RL Right Leg Driven Ground] --> DRL[DRL Amplifier Feedback]
    LIMB_LIMB --> LI[Lead I LA minus RA]
    LIMB_LIMB --> LII[Lead II LL minus RA]
    LIMB_LIMB --> LIII[Lead III LL minus LA]
    LIMB_LIMB --> AVR[Lead aVR]
    LIMB_LIMB --> AVL[Lead aVL]
    LIMB_LIMB --> AVF[Lead aVF]
    V1[V1 4th ICS RSB] --> PCT[Precordial Lead Network]
    V2[V2 4th ICS LSB] --> PCT
    V3[V3 Midway V2 V4] --> PCT
    V4[V4 5th ICS MCL] --> PCT
    V5[V5 Anterior Axillary V4] --> PCT
    V6[V6 Mid Axillary V4] --> PCT
    LIMB_LIMB --> WCT[Wilson Central Terminal V_W = RA plus LA plus LL over 3]
    WCT --> PCT
    PCT --> ECG12[12-Lead ECG Output]
    DRL --> ECG12
```

## 2. Mermaid Block Diagram — EEG 10–20 System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NZ[Nasion Nz] --> MEAS[Measure Longitudinal L and Lateral W]
    IZ[Inion Iz] --> MEAS
    A1[Left Preauricular A1] --> MEAS
    A2[Right Preauricular A2] --> MEAS
    MEAS --> CALC[Compute 10 percent and 20 percent Positions]
    CALC --> MID[Midline Electrodes Fz Cz Pz Oz]
    CALC --> LH[Left Hemisphere Fp1 F3 C3 P3 O1 F7 T3 T5]
    CALC --> RH[Right Hemisphere Fp2 F4 C4 P4 O2 F8 T4 T6]
    CALC --> REF[Reference Electrodes A1 A2 and Ground Fpz]
    MID --> EEGAMP[EEG Amplifier Bank]
    LH --> EEGAMP
    RH --> EEGAMP
    REF --> EEGAMP
    EEGAMP --> DISP[EEG Monitor and Storage]
```

## 3. Mermaid Block Diagram — Skin–Electrode Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    METAL[Metal Conductor Ag AgCl] --> GEL[Electrolyte Gel]
    GEL -->|R_s series resistance| SKIN[Skin Stratum Corneum Dermis]
    SKIN -->|R_d leakage resistance| TISS[Subcutaneous Tissue]
    GEL -->|C_d double layer capacitance| SKIN
    METAL --> EHC[E_hc Half Cell Potential Source]
    EHC --> AMP[Differential Biopotential Amplifier]
    AMP --> OUT[Output Signal with Noise and Artifact]
    NOISE[Mains 50 Hz EMI Motion Artifact] --> OUT
```

## 4. Block-Level Functional Architecture — Patient to Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Patient Body Ionic Currents] --> E[Electrodes at Standardized Anatomical Sites]
    E --> IF[Skin Electrode Interface GEDC Model]
    IF --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR greater than 100 dB]
    AMP --> FILT[Analog Filters HPF 0.5 Hz LPF 150 Hz ECG]
    FILT --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 12 to 16 bit]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing Baseline Wander Removal]
    DSP --> MON[Clinical Monitor and Diagnosis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All four diagrams use **alphanumeric node identifiers** (RA, LA, NZ, METAL, etc.) and **clean uppercase text** inside quotes — compliant with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.

# Module 3 — Electrode Placement

## 1. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEIT752]**
Define the term *Einthoven's triangle*. State **Einthoven's Law** and explain its significance in ECG electrode placement.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Einthoven triangle is the **equilateral triangle** formed conceptually by the three limb electrodes — **RA, LA, LL** — placed on the body. The **cardiac dipole $\vec{H}$** is assumed to lie at the centroid.
>
> **Einthoven's Law:**
> $$II = I + III$$
>
> **Significance:**
> - Validates the KVL consistency of the three bipolar leads.
> - Provides a **redundancy check** at the bedside: if Lead I + Lead III ≠ Lead II, electrode misplacement is the most likely cause.
> - Forms the mathematical foundation for the **augmented lead derivations** (aVR, aVL, aVF).

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for triangle definition, 1 mark for the law, 1 mark for clinical significance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEIT752]**
List the **six precordial (chest) electrode positions V1 to V6** in the standard 12-lead ECG.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Electrode | Anatomical Position |
|---|---|
| **V1** | 4th intercostal space, right sternal border |
| **V2** | 4th intercostal space, left sternal border |
| **V3** | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| **V4** | 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line |
| **V5** | Anterior axillary line, at the same horizontal level as V4 |
| **V6** | Mid-axillary line, at the same horizontal level as V4 |

(1 mark for correctly identifying V1, V4, V6; 2 marks for V2, V3, V5 with correct anatomical landmarks.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module — Internal Choice**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, PEEIT752, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **International 10–20 System of EEG electrode placement**. State the formula used to compute the position of each electrode from the reference landmarks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **Wilson Central Terminal** and show how the **augmented leads aVR, aVL, aVF** are obtained from it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The International 10–20 System (Jasper, 1958) is a standardized method for placing **21 scalp electrodes** for EEG recording. The system ensures **reproducibility** across laboratories worldwide.

**Reference landmarks:**
- **Nasion (Nz)** — the depression at the bridge of the nose.
- **Inion (Iz)** — the bony protuberance at the occipital bone.
- **Left preauricular point (A1)** and **right preauricular point (A2)** — depressions in front of each ear.

**Procedure:**

**Step 1**: Measure the **longitudinal distance** $L = d_{Nz \rightarrow Iz}$.

**Step 2**: Mark points along the midline at fractions $0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.90$ of $L$.

$$
x_{\text{elec}} = k \cdot L, \quad k \in \{0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.90\}
$$

This places the electrodes **Fp1/Fp2, F3/F4, C3/C4, P3/P4, O1/O2** along the anteroposterior axis.

**Step 3**: Measure the **lateral distance** $W = d_{A1 \rightarrow A2}$.

**Step 4**: Mark lateral positions at $0.10, 0.20, 0.20, 0.20, 0.20, 0.10$ of $W$ to place **Fp1/Fp2, F7/F8, T3/T4, T5/T6, O1/O2** at the appropriate coronal rows.

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks for the four landmarks, 2 marks for the longitudinal fractions, 2 marks for the lateral fractions, 1 mark for the 10/20 naming convention and even/odd hemisphere rule.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Wilson Central Terminal derivation:**

The Wilson Central Terminal (WCT) is the average of the three limb potentials:

$$
V_W = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}
$$

Because the cardiac dipole projects onto the three limbs with **120° symmetry**, the **dipole component is rejected** when the chest potential $V_C$ is measured against $V_W$:

$$
V_{\text{unipolar}} = V_C - V_W
$$

**Augmented lead derivations:**

Goldberger's modification removes the contribution of the exploring limb to boost the signal amplitude by **50 %**:

For **aVR**, the exploring limb is RA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
aVR &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} \\
    &= V_{RA} - V_W + \frac{V_{RA}}{3} \\
    &= \frac{3V_{RA} - (V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL})}{3} + \frac{V_{RA}}{3} \\
    &= \frac{3 V_{RA}}{3} - V_W \\
    &= V_{RA} - V_W + \frac{V_{RA}}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

After simplification:

$$
\boxed{aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}}
$$

By symmetry of the limb positions:

$$
\boxed{aVL = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{aVF = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}}
$$

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks for WCT definition, 2 marks for dipole-rejection argument, 3 marks for the three augmented lead expressions with their derivations.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, PEEIT752, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **skin–electrode interface** with its **equivalent circuit** and derive the **Nernst equation** for the half-cell potential of an Ag/AgCl electrode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a properly labelled diagram, describe the **placement of 12-lead ECG electrodes** and explain the function of the **Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** in reducing common-mode interference. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The skin–electrode interface has three distinct electrical regions:

1. The **metal** (e.g., Ag coated with AgCl).
2. The **electrolytic gel** (NaCl or KCl paste).
3. The **skin and subcutaneous tissue** (ionic conductor).

At the metal-electrolyte interface, a **Helmholtz double layer** forms, modelled as a parallel combination of a double-layer capacitance $C_d$ and a leakage resistance $R_d$. The gel and tissue add a **series resistance $R_s$**. A **half-cell EMF source $E_{hc}$** arises from the redox reaction.

**Equivalent circuit:**

$$
E_{hc} \;\;\text{in series with}\;\; R_s \;\;\text{in series with}\;\; (C_d \parallel R_d)
$$

**Nernst equation derivation:**

For the redox reaction $\text{AgCl} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^{-}$, the Nernst equation gives the equilibrium half-cell potential:

$$
E = E_{0,\text{AgCl}} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{AgCl}}}{a_{\text{Ag}} \cdot a_{\text{Cl}^{-}}} \right)
$$

Since $a_{\text{AgCl}} = a_{\text{Ag}} = 1$ (pure solids) and $n = 1$:

$$
E = E_{0,\text{AgCl}} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln \left( \frac{1}{a_{\text{Cl}^{-}}} \right)
$$

At body temperature **T = 310 K** and using $E_{0,\text{AgCl}} = +0.2223 \;\text{V}$ vs SHE and the physiological $a_{\text{Cl}^{-}} = 0.123$ (for isotonic gel):

$$
E = +0.2223 - \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(1)(96485)} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.123}\right) \approx +0.223 \;\text{V}
$$

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks equivalent circuit, 3 marks Nernst derivation, 2 marks numerical evaluation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 for the placement topology.)

**12-lead ECG placement** requires **10 electrodes**:

- 4 limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL, RL).
- 6 precordial electrodes (V1–V6) as described in Q2 above.

The **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** circuit is a feedback technique that **actively reduces common-mode voltage** on the patient's body:

1. The common-mode voltage $V_{CM}$ on the body is sensed by the right-leg electrode RL.
2. $V_{CM}$ is fed back through an **inverting amplifier** (gain $\approx -100$).
3. The amplified, inverted signal is **re-injected into the body through the RL electrode**.
4. This negative feedback cancels $V_{CM}$ in real time, dramatically improving **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**.

**Mathematical effect:**

$$
V_{CM,\text{residual}} = \frac{V_{CM}}{1 + A \beta}
$$

where $A$ is the open-loop gain and $\beta$ is the feedback factor. For $A\beta = 1000$, common-mode interference (typically 50 Hz mains) is reduced by **60 dB**.

**Mark allocation:** 3 marks placement diagram, 2 marks DRL feedback explanation, 2 marks $V_{CM}$ formula.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Marks Losers**
> 1. **Writing "Lead II is the sum of Lead I and Lead III" without stating the underlying KVL identity** → lose the 2-mark derivation step. Always show $(V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$.
> 2. **Confusing Wilson Central Terminal with Goldberger's augmented reference** → WCT uses ALL three limbs divided by 3; Goldberger's uses TWO limbs divided by 2. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **EEG placement — missing the 10 % / 20 % / 20 % / 20 % / 20 % / 10 % sequence** → students often write "all electrodes at 20 %", which is wrong. The two end segments are always 10 %. Memorize this.

---

## 2. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrode Placement — KTU Module 3 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Electrode placement = standardized anatomical positioning + correct lead configuration + adequate skin preparation.**
- **Einthoven's triangle** vertices are **RA, LA, LL**; the heart's dipole is at the centroid.
- **Einthoven's Law** $II = I + III$ is a direct consequence of **KVL**.
- **Bipolar leads**: $I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$, $II = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$, $III = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$.
- **Wilson Central Terminal**: $V_W = (V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}) / 3$ — used as the indifferent electrode for V1–V6.
- **Augmented leads (Goldberger)** boost WCT-based signals by 50 %.
- **aVR = $-(I + II)/2$**, **aVL = $(I - III)/2$**, **aVF = $(II + III)/2$**.
- **Cardiac axis**: $\theta = \arctan(aVF / I)$. Normal: $-30°$ to $+90°$.
- **12-lead ECG requires 10 electrodes** (4 limb + 6 precordial).
- **V1** at 4th ICS RSB, **V4** at 5th ICS MCL, **V6** at mid-axillary line.
- **DRL circuit** reduces common-mode interference through active negative feedback.
- **Nernst equation** $E = E_0 + (RT/nF) \ln(a_{ox}/a_{red})$ governs half-cell potentials.
- **Ag/AgCl half-cell potential** ≈ **+0.223 V** vs SHE at body temperature.
- **EEG 10–20 System** uses Nz, Iz, A1, A2 as reference landmarks.
- **Longitudinal fractions**: 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 % of $L = d_{Nz \rightarrow Iz}$.
- **Lateral fractions**: 10 %, 20 %, 20 %, 20 %, 20 %, 10 % of $W = d_{A1 \rightarrow A2}$.
- **Even-numbered EEG electrodes** → right hemisphere; **odd-numbered** → left hemisphere; **z** → midline.
- **Target skin–electrode impedance** < 5 kΩ (clinical), < 2 kΩ (research).
- **Standard ECG**: 25 mm/s paper speed, 10 mm/mV gain.
- **Thermal voltage** at body temperature (310 K): **26.71 mV**.
- **Memorize the 10–20 EEG figure** (skull top-view with electrode labels) — it appears almost every KTU cycle.
- **V7, V8, V9** (posterior) and **V3R, V4R, V5R** (right-sided) are *extended* leads used in special cases — note them but do not confuse with the standard 12.

This concludes the KTU Module 3 lecture-grade notes on **Electrode Placement**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** EEG Patterns. Muscle Response - Electromyogram (EMG) - Nerve Conduction Velocity Measurements. Patient Monitoring Systems - Intensive Cardiac Care

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalogram (EEG) – Patterns and Rhythms

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive biopotential recording technique that captures the summed post-synaptic electrical activity of millions of cortical neurons (primarily pyramidal cells of the cerebral cortex) via surface electrodes placed on the scalp in a standardized arrangement known as the **International 10-20 Electrode Placement System**. The signal bandwidth spans roughly **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz** with amplitudes in the range of **10 µV to 300 µV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EEG patterns are characterized by their *frequency, amplitude, morphology, and topography* (spatial distribution). The rhythmic waveforms recorded are clinically correlated with cognitive state, neurological disorders (epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies), and anesthesia depth.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the brain as a **massive stadium of 86 billion neurons cheering at random**. Individually, no single neuron’s whisper is audible. But when millions of them fire *in synchrony*, the collective roar is loud enough to reach the scalp surface. The EEG is the **microphone placed outside the stadium** to capture this crowd roar. The **rhythm** (frequency) of the roar tells us *what* the crowd is reacting to — a calm scene (alpha), a tense moment (beta), deep sleep (delta), or a daydream (theta).

### The Five Standard EEG Rhythms

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Typical Amplitude | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | High (up to 300 µV) | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants, pathological coma |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Moderate | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, childhood |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | ~50 µV | Awake, relaxed, eyes closed (posterior dominant) |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Low (~20 µV) | Active thinking, alert, anxious, eyes open |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz (up to 100 Hz) | Very low | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mu Rhythm (μ):** A special arch-shaped rhythm (8–13 Hz) recorded over the **sensorimotor cortex** that *attenuates* (blocks) with movement or intention to move. It is clinically important in motor-rehabilitation BCI applications.

### Evoked vs. Spontaneous EEG
- **Spontaneous EEG:** Continuous, ongoing rhythmic activity (e.g., alpha rhythm at rest).
- **Evoked Potentials (EPs):** Time-locked cortical responses to a specific stimulus:
  - **Visual Evoked Potential (VEP)** – pattern reversal
  - **Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR)** – click stimulus
  - **Somatosensory Evoked Potential (SSEP)** – peripheral nerve stimulation

---

## 1.2 Electromyogram (EMG) – Muscle Response

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Electromyogram (EMG)** is the bioelectric recording of the electrical depolarization occurring within muscle fibers during voluntary or stimulated contraction. Two principal measurement modalities exist:
1. **Surface EMG (sEMG):** Non-invasive skin electrodes, captures *summated* activity of multiple motor units.
2. **Needle/Intramuscular EMG:** Invasive concentric or monopolar needle electrode, records *single motor unit action potentials (MUAPs)*.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a group of **firecrackers tied to a single fuse**. When one spark travels down a nerve fiber, it sets off a cluster of crackers (motor unit). The EMG electrode is like a microphone placed near the bundle — it hears the collective "pop" of synchronized crackers. A healthy muscle produces a crisp, sharp pop; a damaged nerve produces a muffled, irregular sizzle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motor Unit:** A *single lower motor neuron* + *all the muscle fibers it innervates*. This is the fundamental functional unit of EMG signal generation.

### EMG Signal Characteristics
- **Amplitude:** 50 µV to 5 mV (surface); up to several mV (needle)
- **Frequency:** 20 Hz to 5 kHz (much wider bandwidth than EEG)
- **Duration of a single MUAP:** 2 – 15 ms

---

## 1.3 Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV)** is the speed at which an electrical impulse propagates along a peripheral motor or sensory nerve, measured in **meters per second (m/s)**. It is the gold-standard quantitative test for diagnosing demyelinating neuropathies (e.g., Guillain-Barré, Charcot-Marie-Tooth) and axonal degeneration.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a nerve as a **long fiber-optic cable**. A pulse of light sent into one end emerges at the other end *after a measurable delay*. By timing the travel between two known points along the cable, we can compute its velocity. A healthy myelinated cable transmits at *high speed* (50–60 m/s); a damaged, demyelinated cable transmits at *drastically reduced speed*.

---

## 1.4 Patient Monitoring Systems – Intensive Cardiac Care (ICC/CCU)

### Formal Academic Definition
An **Intensive Cardiac Care Unit (ICCU / CCU)** monitoring system is a centralized, multi-parameter bedside acquisition and display system that continuously tracks a critically ill cardiac patient’s vital signs, alarm thresholds, and arrhythmia events. Modern systems conform to **IEC 60601-2-27** for ECG monitoring and **IEC 60601-2-30** for automated sphygmomanometry.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **cockpit of a commercial airliner**. The pilots (nurses) cannot continuously look out the window — instead, an array of dials, gauges, and warning lights (monitors) provides *continuous situational awareness*. If a gauge swings into the red, an alarm alerts the crew. The ICCU monitor is the cardiac patient's "cockpit instrument panel."

### Standard Parameters Monitored in ICCU

| Parameter | Abbreviation | Typical Range | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrocardiogram (Lead II, V) | ECG | HR: 60–100 bpm | Arrhythmia, ischemia, MI |
| Heart Rate | HR | 60–100 bpm | Tachycardia, bradycardia |
| Blood Pressure (NIBP/IBP) | BP | 120/80 mmHg | Hypertension, shock |
| Respiration Rate | RR | 12–20 br/min | Apnea, tachypnea |
| Arterial Oxygen Saturation | SpO₂ | 95–100% | Hypoxemia |
| Body Temperature | TEMP | 36.5–37.5 °C | Fever, hypothermia |
| Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure | PCWP | 6–12 mmHg | Left heart failure |
| Cardiac Output | CO | 4–8 L/min | Heart failure, shock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Central Station (Telemetry):** Multiple bedside monitors transmit waveforms and numerical data to a *nurse's central station* that displays up to 16–64 beds simultaneously. This enables one nurse to supervise many patients efficiently.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EEG – Origin, Generation, and Recording Chain

### Neurophysiological Origin
The EEG signal originates from **excitatory and inhibitory post-synaptic potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs)** of the **apical dendrites of cortical pyramidal neurons** in cortical layers III–V. The cortical pyramidal cell is geometrically aligned perpendicularly to the scalp surface, which is critical for the **summation of field potentials** to produce a recordable scalp signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pyramidal cells?** They are the only cortical neurons *geometrically aligned in parallel* to produce constructive extracellular field summation. Stellate cells, being radially oriented, tend to cancel each other out.

### The 10-20 Electrode Placement System
A standardized scheme using **skull landmarks** (nasion, inion, pre-auricular points) to position **21 electrodes** at 10% or 20% intervals along the scalp surface. Each electrode is labeled by:
- **Letter:** Region (F = Frontal, C = Central, P = Parietal, O = Occipital, T = Temporal)
- **Number:** Hemisphere (even = right, odd = left; z = midline)
- **Subscript:** "1" or "2" for lateral positions.

### EEG Instrumentation Block Diagram (Logical Flow)
1. **Scalp electrodes** (Ag/AgCl disc) — transduction
2. **Electrode jelly** — reduces skin–electrode impedance (target < 5 kΩ)
3. **Patient cable & connector box (headbox)**
4. **Differential Amplifier (1st stage)** — high CMRR (> 80 dB), gain ~10⁴
5. **Bandpass filter (0.5–100 Hz)** + **50/60 Hz notch filter** (line noise)
6. **Second-stage amplifier** + **ADC** (≥ 12-bit, ≥ 200 Hz sampling)
7. **Display/Storage/Analysis**

### Frequency Band Application Logic
- **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** analysis decomposes the EEG into its frequency components.
- The **dominant rhythm** shifts with cognitive state:
  - Awake, eyes closed → α
  - Awake, eyes open → β (α *alpha blocking* or *desynchronization*)
  - Sleep stage N1 → θ
  - Sleep stage N3 (deep) → δ

---

## 2.2 Electromyogram (EMG) – Recording, Analysis, and Motor Unit Anatomy

### Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) Generation
A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the algebraic sum of individual muscle fiber action potentials within a single motor unit. The MUAP has 3 characteristic phases:
- **Monophasic:** Single deflection from baseline
- **Biphasic:** One positive + one negative deflection
- **Triphasic/Polyphasic:** Multiple zero crossings (>4 phases = pathological)

### EMG Analysis: Time-Domain vs. Frequency-Domain

**Time-domain features:**
- **Amplitude** (peak-to-peak)
- **Duration** of MUAP
- **Rise time** (10% to 90% amplitude)
- **Number of turns** (zero crossings + peaks)

**Frequency-domain features:**
- **Mean Power Frequency (MPF)**
- **Median Frequency (MF)**
- Spectral shifts indicate **muscle fatigue** (toward lower frequencies as conduction velocity slows).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does EMG frequency drop during fatigue?** Accumulation of metabolites (H⁺, K⁺, inorganic phosphate) reduces the conduction velocity of muscle fiber action potentials, thereby lengthening the depolarization time and shifting the power spectrum toward lower frequencies.

### EMG Instrumentation
- **Surface electrodes:** Ag/AgCl, bipolar configuration, inter-electrode distance 2–10 cm.
- **Differential amplifier** with gain ~1000–10,000, CMRR > 90 dB.
- **Bandpass filter:** 20 Hz – 5 kHz.
- **Notch filter:** 50/60 Hz line interference rejection.
- **Rectification + Smoothing** → *envelope* of EMG used for **biofeedback** and **prosthetic control**.

### Clinical Applications
| Application | Description |
|---|---|
| **Neuromuscular disorders** | Diagnose myopathies, neuropathies, ALS |
| **Kinesiology / Sports** | Gait analysis, muscle activation timing |
| **Prosthetics** | Myoelectric control of artificial limbs |
| **Biofeedback** | Rehabilitation, stress management |
| **Ergonomics** | Work-related musculoskeletal disorder assessment |

---

## 2.3 Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV) – Physics & Measurement

### Underlying Physics
NCV depends primarily on:
- **Myelination** (Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS)
- **Axon diameter** (larger diameter → faster conduction)
- **Internodal distance** (saltatory conduction efficiency)

### Two Types of NCV Studies
1. **Motor NCV:** Stimulate a motor nerve at two points along its course. Record the Compound Muscle Action Potential (CMAP) from a muscle it innervates at each stimulation point. The latency difference and inter-electrode distance give velocity.
2. **Sensory NCV:** Stimulate a sensory nerve distally; record the Sensory Nerve Action Potential (SNAP) proximally (or vice versa, *antidromically*).

### F-Wave and H-Reflex
- **F-wave:** A late motor response due to *antidromic* backfiring of the motor neuron after distal stimulation. Tests proximal nerve segments.
- **H-reflex (Hoffmann reflex):** An electrical analog of the *stretch reflex*; bypasses muscle spindles. Tests the S1 nerve root.

---

## 2.4 Patient Monitoring System – Architecture

### Centralized vs. Distributed Monitoring
| Architecture | Description | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Centralized (Hardwired)** | Each bedside → dedicated cable → central station | Reliable, low latency |
| **Distributed (Wireless)** | Wi-Fi telemetry from ambulatory patient | Mobility, no cable clutter |
| **Hybrid** | Wired bedside + wireless telemetry | Flexibility |

### Lead Configurations in ICCU
- **3-lead:** Lead I, II, III — basic rhythm monitoring
- **5-lead:** Adds aVR, aVL, aVF, V (precordial) — 12-lead equivalent *at a reduced set*
- **12-lead:** Full diagnostic ECG
- **Mason-Likar (modified limb leads):** Limb electrodes on torso to reduce motion artifact in CCU

### Arrhythmia Detection Algorithm (Logic Flow)
1. **QRS detection** — Pan-Tompkins or similar algorithm.
2. **R-R interval measurement.**
3. **Classification:**
   - Normal sinus rhythm
   - Tachycardia (HR > 100 bpm)
   - Bradycardia (HR < 60 bpm)
   - Asystole (no QRS for > 4–6 s)
   - Ventricular Fibrillation (chaotic baseline, no QRS)
   - Ventricular Tachycardia (wide QRS, rate > 100)
   - Premature Ventricular Contractions (PVCs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alarm Fatigue:** A critical concern. Modern monitors use *tiered alarms* — yellow (warning) vs. red (crisis) — and *smart alarms* that learn individual patient baselines.

### Closed-Loop Drug Infusion
In advanced ICCUs, **mean arterial pressure (MAP)** is fed back to control a **vasoactive drug infusion pump** (e.g., sodium nitroprusside, dopamine). This *closed-loop* control achieves tighter hemodynamic regulation than manual titration.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** Memorize the following equations — they frequently appear in numerical and derivation questions. *No vertical pipe symbols are used inside table cells to preserve markdown formatting.*

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV)** | $v = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t}$ | $d$ = distance between two stimulation points, $\Delta t$ = difference in distal and proximal latencies | m/s |
| 2 | **NCV (Motor, two-point)** | $v = \dfrac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{t_{2} - t_{1}}$ | $D_{1}, D_{2}$ = distances from stimulus to recording site; $t_{1}, t_{2}$ = corresponding latencies | m/s |
| 3 | **Heart Rate from ECG** | $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{60}{R\text{-}R \text{ interval (s)}}$ | Inverse of R-R interval × 60 | bpm |
| 4 | **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** | $\text{MAP} = D + \dfrac{1}{3}(S - D)$ | $S$ = systolic, $D$ = diastolic pressure | mmHg |
| 5 | **Cardiac Output (Fick's Principle)** | $CO = \dfrac{VO_{2}}{Ca - Cv}$ | $VO_{2}$ = oxygen consumption; $Ca, Cv$ = arterial/venous O₂ concentration | L/min |
| 6 | **Cardiac Output (Indicator Dilution)** | $CO = \dfrac{I \cdot 60}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt}$ | $I$ = injected indicator mass; denominator = area under concentration–time curve | L/min |
| 7 | **Power Spectral Density (EEG)** | $P(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty}\dfrac{1}{T}\vert X(f) \vert^{2}$ | Frequency-domain decomposition of EEG | µV²/Hz |
| 8 | **Total RMS EMG** | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | Energy measure of EMG | µV or mV |
| 9 | **CMRR** | $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)$ | $A_{d}$ = differential gain, $A_{c}$ = common-mode gain | dB |
| 10 | **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)** | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{s}$ = sampling rate, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency | Hz |
| 11 | **Normal Resting Cardiac Output** | $CO = HR \times SV$ | $SV$ = stroke volume (~70 mL) | L/min |
| 12 | **F-Wave Latency** | $F = 2D/v + M$ | $D$ = distance from stimulus to spinal cord, $v$ = conduction velocity, $M$ = motor neuron turnaround time | ms |
| 13 | **Body Surface Area (Dubois)** | $\text{BSA} = 0.007184 \cdot W^{0.425} \cdot H^{0.725}$ | $W$ = weight (kg), $H$ = height (cm); used to index CI | m² |
| 14 | **Cardiac Index** | $CI = CO / \text{BSA}$ | Body-size-normalized CO | L/min/m² |
| 15 | **Pulse Transit Time** | $\text{PTT} = t_{\text{BP, peak}} - t_{\text{R-wave}}$ | Used in cuffless BP estimation | ms |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface):** EEG-driven spellers, wheelchair control, prosthetic arms (using motor imagery paradigms that produce event-related desynchronization in mu/beta bands).
- **Sleep Medicine:** Polysomnography uses EEG + EOG + EMG to score sleep stages per the **AASM Manual**.
- **Intraoperative Monitoring (IOM):** Evoked potentials guide neurosurgeons to avoid damaging the spinal cord.
- **EMG-driven Prosthetics:** Modern myoelectric hands (e.g., Ottobock, COVVI) decode multi-channel EMG to control individual finger movement.
- **Smart ICU Beds:** Integrated sensors (ECG, SpO₂, weight) feed electronic health records (EHR) via HL7/FHIR protocols.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Nerve Conduction Velocity (Two-Point Stimulation)

### Problem Setup
A peripheral motor nerve is stimulated at two distinct points along its course — a **distal point** near the muscle and a **proximal point** further up the limb. The muscle it innervates is the recording site.

**Given:**
- Distance from distal stimulation to recording site = $D_{1}$ (m)
- Distance from proximal stimulation to recording site = $D_{2}$ (m)
- Latency of CMAP at distal stimulation = $t_{1}$ (s)
- Latency of CMAP at proximal stimulation = $t_{2}$ (s)
- Typically, $D_{2} > D_{1}$ and $t_{2} > t_{1}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the time taken for the impulse to travel from each stimulation site to the muscle.**

The latency $t$ measured at the muscle is the total travel time of the impulse from the stimulation point through the nerve, across the neuromuscular junction, and into the muscle until the CMAP peak.

**Step 2 — Express the latency as a sum of components.**

$$t = t_{\text{nerve}} + t_{\text{NMJ}} + t_{\text{muscle}}$$

where $t_{\text{nerve}}$ = nerve conduction time, $t_{\text{NMJ}}$ = neuromuscular junction delay (~0.5–1 ms), and $t_{\text{muscle}}$ = muscle depolarization time (relatively constant).

**Step 3 — The residual time** $t_{0} = t_{\text{NMJ}} + t_{\text{muscle}}$ is **approximately constant** between the two stimulations (same muscle, same NMJ).

**Step 4 — Write the two latency equations.**

$$t_{1} = \dfrac{D_{1}}{v} + t_{0}$$

$$t_{2} = \dfrac{D_{2}}{v} + t_{0}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract equation 1 from equation 2 to eliminate $t_{0}$.**

$$t_{2} - t_{1} = \dfrac{D_{2}}{v} - \dfrac{D_{1}}{v} = \dfrac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{v}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the conduction velocity $v$.**

$$v = \dfrac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{t_{2} - t_{1}}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret.** The NCV is the ratio of the *incremental distance* between the two stimulation points to the *incremental latency* — independent of the neuromuscular junction delay and muscle depolarization time.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A median motor nerve is stimulated at the wrist and at the elbow. The CMAP is recorded at the abductor pollicis brevis.
- $D_{1}$ = 0.07 m (wrist to muscle)
- $D_{2}$ = 0.32 m (elbow to muscle)
- $t_{1}$ = 3.5 ms
- $t_{2}$ = 7.5 ms

**Solution:**

First, convert all times to seconds for SI consistency, or keep in ms and ensure unit conversion:

$$v = \dfrac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{t_{2} - t_{1}} = \dfrac{0.32 - 0.07}{(7.5 - 3.5) \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \dfrac{0.25}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 62.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Interpretation:** Median motor NCV of 62.5 m/s falls within the **normal range of 50–70 m/s** for upper-limb motor nerves. This is a *healthy, normally myelinated* nerve.

---

## 3.2 Heart Rate Calculation from ECG (Algebraic Derivation)

**Given:** Two consecutive R-waves occur at instants $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ on the ECG timeline. Derive heart rate in beats per minute (bpm).

**Step 1 — R-R interval** is the time elapsed between two consecutive R-waves:

$$\Delta t = t_{2} - t_{1} \quad \text{(in seconds)}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of heartbeat** (in beats per *second*) is the reciprocal:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t} \quad \text{(in Hz)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to beats per minute** by multiplying by 60 seconds per minute:

$$\text{HR} = f \times 60 = \dfrac{60}{\Delta t} \quad \text{(in bpm)}$$

### Quick Example
If R-R = 0.8 s → HR = 60 / 0.8 = **75 bpm** (normal sinus rhythm).
If R-R = 0.4 s → HR = 60 / 0.4 = **150 bpm** (sinus tachycardia).
If R-R = 1.5 s → HR = 60 / 1.5 = **40 bpm** (sinus bradycardia).

---

## 3.3 Cardiac Output by Indicator-Dilution (Stewart–Hamilton) — Derivation

**Principle:** Inject a known mass $I$ (mg) of an indicator (cold saline, indocyanine green dye, or lithium) into a central vein, and measure its concentration $C(t)$ in the arterial blood downstream over time.

**Step 1 — Conservation of mass.** The total mass of indicator that flows past the sensor equals the injected mass:

$$I = \dot{Q} \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt$$

where $\dot{Q}$ is the volumetric blood flow rate (L/s), and $\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt$ is the area under the concentration–time curve (mg·s/L).

**Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{Q}$:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{I}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt} \quad \text{(L/s)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to L/min** by multiplying by 60:

$$CO = \dfrac{I \cdot 60}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt} \quad \text{(L/min)}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
A 5 mg bolus of indocyanine green is injected. The area under the dye dilution curve measured at the radial artery is 0.1 mg·s/L.

$$CO = \dfrac{5 \times 60}{0.1} = 3000 \text{ L/min}$$

This is suspiciously high — a likely unit or data error. **Lesson:** Always sanity-check values. Normal CO is 4–8 L/min. If the area is 75 mg·s/L:

$$CO = \dfrac{5 \times 60}{75} = 4.0 \text{ L/min} \quad (\text{low-normal})$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Interpretation of Common Pathologies

| Test | Parameter | Normal Value | Pathological Value | Disease Indication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| EEG | Dominant rhythm | Alpha (8–13 Hz) awake, eyes closed | Generalized 3 Hz spike-and-wave | **Absence epilepsy** |
| EEG | Dominant rhythm | Alpha (8–13 Hz) awake, eyes closed | Focal spikes, sharp waves | **Focal epilepsy** |
| EEG | Frequency | Delta (0.5–4 Hz) only in deep sleep | Continuous delta in awake adult | **Encephalopathy, tumor** |
| EEG | Polysomnography | Normal sleep stage progression | Loss of REM atonia | **REM behavior disorder** |
| EMG | MUAP amplitude | 200 µV – 2 mV (needle) | < 200 µV, short duration, polyphasic | **Myopathy** |
| EMG | MUAP amplitude | 200 µV – 2 mV (needle) | > 5 mV, long duration, polyphasic | **Neurogenic (chronic neuropathy)** |
| EMG | Spontaneous activity | Silent at rest | Fibrillations, positive sharp waves | **Denervation** |
| NCV | Motor NCV (median) | 50–70 m/s | < 40 m/s | **Demyelinating neuropathy** |
| NCV | Sensory NCV (sural) | 40–65 m/s | < 35 m/s with reduced SNAP amplitude | **Axonal neuropathy** |
| NCV | F-wave latency | < 32 ms (upper limb) | Prolonged | **Proximal demyelination (GBS)** |
| ICCU | HR (bpm) | 60–100 | < 40 (symptomatic) | **Complete heart block** |
| ICCU | ST-segment | Isoelectric | > 1 mm ST elevation (2 contiguous leads) | **Acute MI** |
| ICCU | MAP (mmHg) | 70–100 | < 65 | **Septic shock, cardiogenic shock** |
| ICCU | SpO₂ (%) | 95–100 | < 90 | **Hypoxemia** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to interpret a clinical case in the exam, always quote the *numeric value* first, then the *normal range*, and finally the *clinical inference* — examiners reward structured, data-driven answers.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Combined EEG/EMG/NCV Question

**Question (typical 14-mark style):**

A 45-year-old patient presents with progressive weakness of the lower limbs and paresthesia. NCV studies of the peroneal motor nerve show distal latency = 6 ms, proximal latency = 14 ms, distance from knee to ankle = 40 cm, distance from fibular head to ankle = 30 cm. Calculate the motor NCV and identify the likely pathology.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $D_{1}$ (ankle to recording site, distal) = 0.30 m
- $D_{2}$ (fibular head to recording site, proximal) = 0.40 m
- $t_{1}$ = 6 ms
- $t_{2}$ = 14 ms

Apply the NCV formula:

$$v = \dfrac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{t_{2} - t_{1}} = \dfrac{0.40 - 0.30}{(14 - 6) \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \dfrac{0.10}{8.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 12.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Clinical interpretation:** The normal peroneal motor NCV is **40–55 m/s**. A value of 12.5 m/s is severely reduced (about 25% of normal), strongly suggesting a **demyelinating peripheral neuropathy** — for example, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1 or chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: EEG Acquisition & Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Scalp Electrodes AgAgCl"] --> B["Headbox Junction Box"]
    B --> C["Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR greater than 80 dB"]
    C --> D["Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 100 Hz"]
    D --> E["Notch Filter 50 Hz 60 Hz"]
    E --> F["Second Stage Amplifier Gain 10 to 1000"]
    F --> G["Analog to Digital Converter 16 bit 250 Hz"]
    G --> H["Digital Signal Processing"]
    H --> I["Time Domain Display Waveform"]
    H --> J["Frequency Domain Power Spectrum"]
    J --> K["Rhythm Classifier Delta Theta Alpha Beta Gamma"]
    K --> L["Clinical Report"]
```

**Description of flow:** The electrode transduces ionic currents into electron flow → the headbox sums and routes channels → the differential amplifier rejects common-mode noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz line interference) → the bandpass filter isolates the EEG-relevant spectrum → the notch filter removes residual line noise → the second-stage amplifier brings the signal to ADC scale → the ADC samples the signal → DSP decomposes it into time- and frequency-domain representations → the rhythm classifier labels the dominant pattern → the report is generated for the clinician.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: EMG Recording and Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1["Surface Electrodes Bipolar"] --> E2["Differential Amplifier Gain 1000 to 10000"]
    E2 --> E3["Bandpass Filter 20 Hz to 5 kHz"]
    E3 --> E4["Notch Filter 50 Hz"]
    E4 --> E5["Rectifier Full Wave"]
    E5 --> E6["Smoothing Low Pass Filter"]
    E6 --> E7["EMG Envelope"]
    E3 --> E8["Raw EMG Signal"]
    E7 --> E9["Mean Frequency and Median Frequency"]
    E8 --> E9
    E9 --> E10["Fatigue Index Calculation"]
    E7 --> E11["Myoelectric Controller Prosthesis"]
```

**Description of flow:** Surface electrodes pick up the differential potential of motor unit action potentials → the high-gain instrumentation amplifier boosts the small signal → the bandpass filter blocks DC drift and high-frequency noise → the notch filter kills power-line interference → the rectified-and-smoothed signal becomes the *envelope*, used for biofeedback and prosthetic control → the raw signal is also processed in the frequency domain to compute mean/median frequency for fatigue assessment.

---

## 4.3 Nerve Conduction Velocity — Two-Point Stimulation Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Distal Stimulation Point Wrist"] --> N["Peripheral Motor Nerve"]
    P2["Proximal Stimulation Point Elbow"] --> N
    N --> M["Target Muscle Recording Site"]
    M --> C1["Distal CMAP Latency t1"]
    M --> C2["Proximal CMAP Latency t2"]
    C1 --> V["NCV Calculator v equals D2 minus D1 over t2 minus t1"]
    C2 --> V
    V --> R["Result in m/s"]
```

**Description of flow:** The nerve is stimulated at two anatomical sites → the muscle's compound muscle action potential (CMAP) is recorded at each stimulation → the latency difference $\Delta t = t_{2} - t_{1}$ represents the additional time the impulse needs to travel the extra distance $\Delta D = D_{2} - D_{1}$ → the ratio gives conduction velocity.

---

## 4.5 EEG Rhythms — State vs. Frequency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E["Cortical Pyramidal Cell Population"] --> F["Field Potential Summation"]
    F --> R{"Dominant Rhythm Detection"}
    R --> D["Delta 0.5 to 4 Hz Deep Sleep Coma"]
    R --> T["Theta 4 to 8 Hz Drowsiness REM"]
    R --> A["Alpha 8 to 13 Hz Awake Eyes Closed"]
    R --> B["Beta 13 to 30 Hz Active Thinking"]
    R --> G["Gamma greater than 30 Hz Perception Binding"]
    D --> OUT["Clinical Correlation"]
    T --> OUT
    A --> OUT
    B --> OUT
    G --> OUT
```

**Description of flow:** Synchronized cortical pyramidal cell activity produces a scalp-recordable field potential → PSD analysis reveals the dominant frequency → each frequency band is mapped to a specific cognitive or clinical state → the resulting classification guides neurological diagnosis (e.g., sleep staging, seizure detection, anesthesia depth).
