# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – July 2023]`
**Define EEG. List the five standard EEG rhythms with their frequency ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** The Electroencephalogram (EEG) is the non-invasive recording of the summed post-synaptic electrical activity of cortical neurons, captured via surface electrodes placed on the scalp, with amplitudes of **10–300 µV** and a usable bandwidth of **0.5–100 Hz**.

**Five rhythms (2 marks):**

| Rhythm | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz |
| Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz |
| Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz |
| Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz |

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)? State the typical amplitude and duration of a normal needle EMG MUAP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the algebraic sum of action potentials generated by all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit during voluntary or stimulated activation. (1 mark)

For needle EMG, the typical amplitude is **200 µV to 2 mV** (1 mark) and the duration is **2 to 15 ms** (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In NCV numerical problems, students commonly lose marks for:
> 1. *Failing to convert units* (ms to s, mm to m) — this is the #1 reason for wrong answers.
> 2. *Confusing motor and sensory NCV* — motor requires two-point stimulation; sensory can use a single site.
> 3. *Not stating the normal range* and *clinical interpretation* in the final answer.
> 4. *Skipping the diagram* — a labeled nerve-muscle diagram earns 1–2 easy marks.

---

### **Question B-A (14 marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
Explain the **10-20 international electrode placement system** used in EEG recording. With a neat block diagram, describe the **EEG acquisition system** and the function of each block.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
Define **Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV)**. Derive the formula for motor NCV using the two-point stimulation method. The median motor nerve is stimulated at the wrist (3 ms latency) and at the elbow (8 ms latency). The distance from the wrist to the recording muscle is 7 cm, and from the elbow to the muscle is 32 cm. Calculate the NCV and state whether it is normal.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**10-20 System (3 marks):**
The 10-20 system is an internationally standardized method for placing scalp electrodes, defined by the *International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)*, using two anatomical landmarks:
- **Nasion** (frontonasal junction)
- **Inion** (occipital protuberance)
- **Pre-auricular points** (left and right)

Electrodes are placed at 10% or 20% intervals of the total nasion-to-inion and pre-auricular-to-pre-auricular distances. The system uses **21 electrodes** (extended to 64+ in high-density EEG).

**Naming convention:**
- Letter = region: Fp (frontal pole), F (frontal), C (central), P (parietal), T (temporal), O (occipital)
- Number = hemisphere: odd = left, even = right, "z" = midline
- Example: **C3** = central electrode on the left hemisphere; **Fz** = midline frontal.

**Block diagram of EEG acquisition (3 marks):**
```
[Electrodes] → [Headbox] → [Differential Amplifier] → [Bandpass Filter 0.5–100 Hz]
→ [50/60 Hz Notch Filter] → [Second Stage Amplifier] → [ADC] → [DSP] → [Display]
```
*See SECTION 4.1 for a detailed Mermaid version.*

**Function of each block (1 mark):**
- **Electrodes:** Transduce ionic currents to electronic signals.
- **Differential amplifier:** Rejects common-mode noise (50/60 Hz, ambient EMI), provides high CMRR.
- **Bandpass filter:** Limits bandwidth to 0.5–100 Hz.
- **Notch filter:** Removes residual line interference.
- **ADC:** Converts analog signal to digital for processing.
- **DSP/Display:** Stores, analyzes, and visualizes the EEG.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Definition of NCV (1 mark):**
Nerve conduction velocity is the speed at which an action potential propagates along a peripheral nerve, expressed in **m/s**.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The derivation is given in SECTION 3.1. The final formula is:

$$v = \dfrac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{t_{2} - t_{1}}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
- $D_{1}$ = 0.07 m, $D_{2}$ = 0.32 m
- $t_{1}$ = 3 ms = 0.003 s, $t_{2}$ = 8 ms = 0.008 s

$$v = \dfrac{0.32 - 0.07}{(8 - 3) \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.005} = 50 \text{ m/s}$$

**Clinical interpretation (1 mark):**
The normal median motor NCV is **50–70 m/s**. A value of 50 m/s is at the **lower end of normal** — the nerve is functional, with possibly minimal demyelinating changes if symptoms are present.

---

### **Question B-B (14 marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
What is **EMG**? Explain the **motor unit** and the different types of EMG electrodes. With a block diagram, describe the **EMG acquisition system**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
Describe the **architecture of a modern Intensive Cardiac Care Unit (ICCU) monitoring system**. List and explain the major parameters monitored, the standard lead configurations, and the alarm hierarchy used.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**EMG Definition (1 mark):**
Electromyography (EMG) is the recording of the electrical activity generated by skeletal muscle during voluntary or evoked contraction, producing signals of amplitude 50 µV – 5 mV and frequency 20 Hz – 5 kHz.

**Motor Unit (2 marks):**
A motor unit comprises **one alpha motor neuron** (cell body in the anterior horn of the spinal cord), **its axon**, and **all the muscle fibers it innervates**. It is the smallest functional unit that can be voluntarily activated. The number of fibers per motor unit ranges from 3 (extraocular muscles — fine control) to over 1000 (gastrocnemius — gross force).

**Types of electrodes (2 marks):**
- **Surface (skin) electrodes:** Ag/AgCl discs, non-invasive, capture *summed* activity of multiple motor units; used in kinesiology, biofeedback, prosthetics.
- **Needle (intramuscular) electrodes:**
  - **Concentric needle** — single recording surface within a cannula
  - **Monopolar needle** — single needle + a separate reference
  - **Single-fiber EMG (SFEMG)** — has a tiny recording surface (~25 µm) to isolate individual muscle fibers; used in myasthenia gravis diagnosis.

**EMG acquisition block diagram (2 marks):**
```
[Electrodes (Surface / Needle)] → [Differential Amplifier (Gain 10³–10⁴, CMRR > 90 dB)]
→ [Bandpass Filter 20 Hz–5 kHz] → [Notch Filter 50 Hz] → [ADC] → [DSP]
→ (a) Time-domain display; (b) Frequency-domain (MPF, MF) analysis;
→ (c) Rectified-Smoothed Envelope → Prosthetic Controller
```

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**ICCU Architecture (2 marks):**
A modern ICCU monitoring system has three layers:
1. **Patient/sensor layer** — disposable electrodes, cuffs, probes.
2. **Bedside monitor layer** — multi-parameter acquisition, processing, alarm engine, local display.
3. **Central station layer** — multi-bed display, event storage, network gateway (HL7/FHIR) to EHR.
4. *Optional:* **Wireless telemetry layer** for ambulatory patients.

**Parameters monitored (2 marks):**
ECG (Lead II, V), Heart Rate, NIBP/IBP, SpO₂, Respiration Rate, Temperature, EtCO₂ (capnography), PCWP, Cardiac Output.

**Lead configurations (1.5 marks):**
- 3-lead (rhythm monitoring — Lead II preferred for P-wave visibility)
- 5-lead (12-lead subset — Mason-Likar)
- 12-lead (full diagnostic ECG)
- Precordial leads (V1–V6) for ST-segment monitoring

**Alarm hierarchy (1.5 marks):**
- **Yellow (Warning) alarms** — moderate deviation, e.g., HR 110, SpO₂ 90%
- **Red (Crisis) alarms** — life-threatening, e.g., asystole, ventricular fibrillation
- **Technical alarms** — equipment malfunction (lead-off, low battery)
- **Smart alarms** — patient-specific, learn individual baselines to reduce false alarms.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Checklist:**

- **EEG Origin:** Cortical pyramidal neurons, post-synaptic potentials, not action potentials. **Frequency range: 0.5–100 Hz; Amplitude: 10–300 µV.**
- **Five EEG rhythms:** Delta (<4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30). *Mnemonic: "**D**ark **T**ea **A**llows **B**etter **G**rowth."*
- **10-20 System:** Odd numbers = left, even = right, "z" = midline.
- **Alpha blocking (desynchronization):** Alpha rhythm disappears when eyes open.
- **EMG frequency:** 20 Hz – 5 kHz. *Much higher than EEG.*
- **EMG electrodes:** Surface (non-invasive) vs. Needle (concentric, monopolar, SFEMG).
- **Motor Unit = 1 motor neuron + all its innervated muscle fibers.**
- **NCV formula (two-point):** $v = (D_{2} - D_{1})/(t_{2} - t_{1})$ — *always convert units to SI before computing.*
- **Normal motor NCV:** Upper limb 50–70 m/s, Lower limb 40–55 m/s.
- **NCV < 70% of normal** → strongly suggests demyelinating neuropathy.
- **F-wave:** Tests *proximal* nerve segments (anterior horn to distal).
- **H-reflex:** Electrical analog of stretch reflex; tests S1 root.
- **ICCU parameters:** ECG, HR, NIBP/IBP, SpO₂, RR, TEMP, CO, PCWP.
- **Preferred ECG lead for ICU rhythm monitoring:** Lead II (best P-wave visibility).
- **CMRR** of biopotential amplifiers should be **> 80 dB (EEG/EMG)** and **> 90 dB (ECG)**.
- **Sampling rate rule:** $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist). Practical sampling: **250 Hz for EEG, 1 kHz for EMG, 500 Hz for ECG.**
- **HR formula:** $\text{HR (bpm)} = 60 / R\text{-}R$ (s).
- **MAP formula:** $\text{MAP} = D + (S - D)/3$ ≈ 93 mmHg at BP 120/80.
- **Fick CO:** $CO = VO_{2} / (Ca - Cv)$.
- **Indicator dilution CO:** $CO = (I \cdot 60) / \int C(t) dt$.
- **Cardiac Index:** $CI = CO / \text{BSA}$ (Dubois formula).
- **Alarm hierarchy:** Yellow (warning) < Red (crisis) < Technical (equipment).
- **HL7/FHIR:** Modern hospital network standards for EHR data exchange.
- **Mason-Likar modification:** Limb electrodes moved to torso to reduce motion artifact in CCU.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU exam tip:** If a question asks for a "block diagram" of *any* biopotential recording system, the canonical answer is always: **Electrode → Differential Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → Notch Filter → ADC → Display/DSP.** Memorize this sequence — it scores full marks regardless of the specific biopotential (EEG, EMG, ECG, EOG, ERG).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bedside and Central Monitoring Systems. Sources of Electrical Hazards and Safety Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bedside and Central Monitoring Systems. Sources of Electrical Hazards and Safety Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bedside Patient Monitoring System (BPMS)

A **Bedside Patient Monitoring System (BPMS)** is a critical-care electronic instrumentation assembly located physically adjacent to a patient's bed, designed to continuously acquire, condition, display, and record multiple physiological parameters in real time, providing immediate visual and audible alerts when any parameter deviates from pre-set physiological limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A bedside monitor is a multi-parameter acquisition unit that measures vital signs such as ECG, Respiration, SpO₂, NIBP, Temperature, and IBP, displays waveforms (time-domain) and numerics, and provides an alarm subsystem based on threshold comparison."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **cockpit of an aircraft**. The pilot does not stare at a single gauge; instead, the cockpit dashboard consolidates **air speed, altitude, fuel, engine temperature, and warnings** into one organized display. Similarly, a bedside monitor is the **"patient's cockpit"** — it is the ICU/CCU-equivalent of an aircraft dashboard. A nurse cannot continuously measure heart rate, blood oxygen, breathing rate, and body temperature manually; the monitor does this 24/7 with sub-second sampling rates, much like an autopilot system in a modern jet.

The key parameters monitored include:
- **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** — cardiac electrical activity
- **Respiration Rate (RR)** — breathing frequency
- **SpO₂ (Oxygen Saturation)** — blood oxygen percentage
- **NIBP / IBP (Blood Pressure)** — non-invasive/invasive arterial pressure
- **Temperature (T1, T2)** — core and skin temperature
- **EtCO₂** — end-tidal carbon dioxide

## 1.2 Central Monitoring System (CMS)

A **Central Monitoring System (Central Station)** is a remote, networked display-and-recording console installed at the **nurse's station** that simultaneously receives data from multiple bedside monitors (typically 4, 8, 16, or 32 beds) over a wired (RS-485 / Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi 802.11 a/b/g/n) communication link. The CMS aggregates waveforms, numerics, and alarms, providing centralized surveillance of an entire ward (ICU, CCU, NICU, OR, or emergency department).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Central Station?** In a 32-bed ICU, sending 32 dedicated nurses to 32 patients is economically and logistically infeasible. The CMS allows **one trained nurse to monitor 32 patients** from a single console, dramatically improving the nurse-to-patient ratio while maintaining alarm awareness.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **air traffic control (ATC) tower**. Multiple aircraft (bedside monitors) transmit telemetry (telemetry data) to a single ground station (CMS). The ATC operator sees all flight paths, altitudes, and emergency squawks on radar. The CMS plays exactly the same role in a hospital ward.

## 1.3 Telemetry Systems

**Biomedical Telemetry** refers to the wireless transmission of physiological signals from an **ambulatory patient** (using a battery-powered body-worn transmitter) to a **remote receiver** connected to the central station. This allows continuous monitoring of mobile patients who cannot remain bed-bound (e.g., post-operative recovery, cardiac step-down units, stress test labs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Frequencies:** Medical telemetry in India uses **WMTS (Wireless Medical Telemetry Service) bands** of 174–216 MHz (VHF), 450–470 MHz (UHF), and 1.4 GHz, with Wi-Fi bands at 2.4/5 GHz under controlled hospital spectrum allocation.

## 1.4 Sources of Electrical Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 60601-1):** An *electrical hazard* in medical equipment is any condition in which leakage current, earth current, or applied current through the patient exceeds the **Maximum Allowable Patient Auxiliary Current** of **10 µA (Type CF)** or **100 µA (Type B)** as defined in IEC 60601-1, 3rd Edition.

### Conceptual Analogy

The human body is essentially a **saline-filled electrolytic bag** wrapped in skin (a resistor). When external voltage is applied, current flows through the body proportional to voltage divided by body impedance. Even **tiny currents** can be lethal if they pass through the heart.

### The Two Principal Classes of Shock

| Hazard Class | Current Path | Threshold Current | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macroshock** | Skin → Body → Skin (or limb-to-limb) | **≥ 1 mA** (perceptible) ; **≥ 100 mA** (ventricular fibrillation for 1 s) | Shock received by intact skin surface; high currents required. |
| **Microshock** | Catheter/electrode → Heart → Blood | **≥ 10 µA** directly across myocardium | Shock from intracardiac catheter; extremely low current lethal. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Macroshock needs currents as large as you can feel; Microshock needs currents a thousand times smaller, because it bypasses skin resistance by entering through a saline-filled catheter that touches the heart directly.

### Primary Sources of Electrical Hazards

1. **Leakage Currents** — Capacitive coupling (≈ 10 nA) and resistive leakage from power line to chassis.
2. **Ground Faults** — Loss of protective earth; chassis becomes energized at mains voltage.
3. **Mains Power Line Voltage** — Direct contact with 230 V AC, 50 Hz.
4. **Defibrillator Discharge** — Up to **360 J** into 50 Ω delivers ≈ **5 kV / 70 A**.
5. **ESD (Electrostatic Discharge)** — Up to **15 kV** transient pulses.
6. **Electrosurgical Units (ESU)** — RF leakage up to 300 W at 500 kHz.
7. **Inductive/Capacitive Coupling** — From adjacent power cables to patient leads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Bedside Monitor

A bedside monitor is built on a **modular architecture** consisting of the following subsystems:

1. **Signal Acquisition Module (SAM)**
   - Front-end amplifier, isolation, and signal conditioning.
   - ECG: differential gain 1000, CMRR ≥ 100 dB, bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz.
   - SpO₂: dual-wavelength pulse oximeter (660 nm red, 940 nm IR).
   - NIBP: oscillometric method using cuff and pressure transducer.

2. **Signal Processing Unit (DSP/MCU)**
   - Sampling (≥ 500 samples/sec for ECG).
   - Filtering (notch 50 Hz, low-pass, high-pass).
   - Parameter extraction (R-peak detection, SpO₂ ratio-of-ratios).
   - **Sampling theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist).

3. **Display Subsystem**
   - TFT/LCD color screen ≥ 12 inches.
   - Multi-trace display (e.g., 6 waveforms simultaneously).
   - Trend graphs (1 h, 24 h, 7 days, 30 days).

4. **Alarm Subsystem**
   - **Three-tier priority**: High (life-threatening, red flashing), Medium (caution, yellow), Low (informational, cyan).
   - Audible: 45–85 dB at 1 m, distinct tonal patterns.
   - Visual: Flashing numeric, color-coded.

5. **Memory / Recording**
   - Internal SSD ≥ 128 GB.
   - USB / SD card slot for event export.
   - HL7 / DICOM for hospital information system (HIS) integration.

6. **Communication Interface**
   - RS-232, RS-485 (legacy), Ethernet (TCP/IP), Wi-Fi, Bluetooth.

## 2.2 Central Monitoring System Architecture

A CMS consists of:

- **Central Station Computer** (industrial PC, multi-core, RAID storage).
- **Multi-bed display** (typically 4–32 beds tiled, with a "bed overview" screen showing mini-traces for all beds).
- **Network switch** connecting bedside monitors via LAN.
- **HL7 / DICOM gateway** to Hospital Information System (HIS).
- **Review stations** for off-line analysis of stored waveforms.

### The "Why" Behind the Network

Each bedside monitor is a **client**; the CMS is the **server**. Data flow follows:

$$\text{Bedside Monitor (Client)} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP/IP}} \text{Switch} \xrightarrow{\text{Ethernet}} \text{CMS (Server)} \xrightarrow{\text{HL7}} \text{HIS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **HL7 (Health Level 7)** is the international standard (ANSI) for healthcare data interchange. Version 2.x uses pipe-delimited ASCII messages; Version 3 uses XML.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus for biomedical instrumentation.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{V}{Z_{body}}$ | $I$ = current, $V$ = applied voltage, $Z_{body}$ = body impedance | A, V, Ω | Body current calculation |
| 2 | $Z_{body} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{skin}} + R_{internal}$ | $C_{skin}$ ≈ 100 pF, $R_{internal}$ ≈ 300 Ω | Ω | Body impedance model |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | $E$ = energy stored, $C$ = capacitance, $V$ = voltage | J, F, V | Defibrillator energy |
| 4 | $SpO_2 = f\!\left(\dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} \Big/ \dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}}\right)$ | Ratio of ratios | % | Pulse oximetry |
| 5 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Sampling frequency vs signal bandwidth | Hz | Nyquist theorem |
| 6 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Noise specification |
| 7 | $I_{leak} = \dfrac{V_{mains}}{Z_{isolation}}$ | Leakage current | A | Safety analysis |
| 8 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB | ECG amplifier |
| 9 | $I_{fibrillation} \approx \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{t}}$ | $K$ ≈ 0.067 A·s^½, $t$ = shock duration | A | Let-go / fibrillation curves |
| 10 | $P_{RF} = I_{ESU}^2 \cdot R_{tissue}$ | ESU power dissipation | W | Electrosurgery safety |

### Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **Equations 1–3** underpin **patient safety**: hospital-grade isolation is rated to keep $I_{leak} < 10 \, \mu A$ for CF-type applied parts.
- **Equation 4** is the foundation of every pulse-oximeter chip (e.g., MAX30100, AFE4490).
- **Equations 5–6** drive **A/D converter selection** in monitors.
- **Equation 7** is the design constraint for **isolation amplifiers** (ADuM1411, ISO124).
- **Equation 9** is the empirical basis for **IEC 60479 macroshock thresholds** (50 Hz AC, fibrillating threshold ≈ 35 mA·√s).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Body Impedance and Current Calculation

We start with the **canonical human body model** as a series combination of skin impedance and internal tissue impedance.

### Step 1 — Identify the Equivalent Circuit

The human body from hand to hand can be modelled as:

$$Z_{body} = Z_{skin} + R_{internal} + Z_{skin}$$

Skin impedance $Z_{skin}$ is a parallel combination of skin resistance $R_{skin}$ and skin capacitance $C_{skin}$:

$$Z_{skin} = \dfrac{R_{skin}}{1 + j\omega R_{skin} C_{skin}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Realistic Values

For dry intact skin: $R_{skin} \approx 100 \, k\Omega$, $C_{skin} \approx 100 \, pF$, $R_{internal} \approx 300 \, \Omega$, mains frequency $f = 50 \, Hz$ so $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.16 \, rad/s$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Skin Capacitive Reactance

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_{skin}} = \dfrac{1}{314.16 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{7} \, \Omega = 31.8 \, M\Omega$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Skin Impedance Magnitude

Since $R_{skin} \ll X_C$, the parallel combination is dominated by the smaller resistance:

$$\vert Z_{skin} \vert \approx R_{skin} = 100 \, k\Omega$$

### Step 5 — Total Body Impedance

$$\vert Z_{body} \vert = 2 \vert Z_{skin} \vert + R_{internal}$$

$$\vert Z_{body} \vert = 2 \times 100 \times 10^{3} + 300$$

$$\vert Z_{body} \vert = 200{,}300 \, \Omega \approx 200 \, k\Omega$$

### Step 6 — Compute Current from Mains Touch

If a person touches a live 230 V AC line with dry hands:

$$I = \dfrac{V}{Z_{body}} = \dfrac{230}{200{,}300}$$

$$I \approx 1.15 \times 10^{-3} \, A = 1.15 \, mA$$

This is **just at the perception threshold** (1 mA at 50 Hz). With wet/broken skin, $R_{skin}$ drops to ≈ 1 kΩ, giving:

$$I_{wet} = \dfrac{230}{2 \times 1{,}000 + 300} = \dfrac{230}{2{,}300} \approx 100 \, mA$$

which is **above the ventricular fibrillation threshold** for > 1 s exposure — **lethal macroshock**.

### Step 7 — Microshock Derivation

For an intracardiac catheter, the current enters directly into the blood pool, **bypassing the skin**. The path is:

$$Z_{microshock} = R_{catheter} + R_{blood} + R_{myocardium}$$

Typical values: $R_{catheter} \approx 1 \, k\Omega$, $R_{blood} + R_{myocardium} \approx 500 \, \Omega$. Total ≈ 1.5 kΩ.

For 10 µA through this path:

$$V_{required} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 1{,}500 = 15 \, mV$$

A leakage voltage of just **15 millivolts** is lethal in microshock! This is why **isolation of CF-type applied parts** is mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation: Defibrillator Energy Delivery

A defibrillator stores energy in a capacitor $C$ charged to voltage $V$, then delivers to patient thoracic impedance $R_t \approx 50 \, \Omega$.

### Step 1 — Energy Stored in Capacitor

$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

For a 360 J biphasic defibrillator with $C = 32 \, \mu F$:

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 360}{32 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V = \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^{7}} \approx 4{,}743 \, V$$

### Step 2 — Peak Current Delivered

The discharge is a damped exponential $I(t) = \dfrac{V}{R_t} e^{-t/RC}$:

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V}{R_t} = \dfrac{4{,}743}{50} \approx 94.9 \, A$$

### Step 3 — Time Constant

$$\tau = R_t C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \, ms$$

So 99% of energy is delivered in ≈ $5\tau = 8 \, ms$ — well within the **20–30 ms** myocardial refractory period range used clinically.

## 3.3 Derivation: SpO₂ Ratio-of-Ratios

Pulse oximetry uses two wavelengths: red ($R$, 660 nm) and infrared ($IR$, 940 nm). The DC component captures tissue thickness; the AC component captures the pulsatile arterial blood.

### Step 1 — Define the Modulation Ratios

For each wavelength, compute the ratio of pulsatile (AC) to steady (DC) absorbance:

$$\rho_{R} = \dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}}, \quad \rho_{IR} = \dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Ratio of Ratios

$$R = \dfrac{\rho_{R}}{\rho_{IR}} = \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$$

### Step 3 — Calibrate Against Co-oximetry

Empirically, the Beer–Lambert-derived relationship is approximately linear in the 70–100% range:

$$SpO_2 \,(\%) \approx 110 - 25 \cdot R$$

For example, if $R = 0.8$:

$$SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25 \times 0.8 = 110 - 20 = 90\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safety Standards Summary

| Standard | Scope | Key Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IEC 60601-1** | General safety of medical electrical equipment | Earth leakage current (Type B) | ≤ 500 µA normal, ≤ 1 mA single-fault |
| **IEC 60601-1** | Patient auxiliary current (Type CF) | Normal | **≤ 10 µA** ; single-fault **≤ 50 µA** |
| **IEC 60601-2-25** | ECG safety | Defibrillator-proof applied part | Required |
| **NFPA 99** | Health care facilities | Isolated power systems (IPS) | Required for wet locations (OR) |
| **IEC 60479** | Effects of current on human body | Ventricular fibrillation threshold | 35 mA·√s at 50 Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Type B, BF, CF classification** (IEC 60601-1):
> - **B (Body):** No patient contact; e.g., MRI, X-ray.
> - **BF (Body Floating):** Conductive contact with patient, not directly with heart; e.g., NIBP cuff, SpO₂ probe.
> - **CF (Cardiac Floating):** Direct contact with heart; highest isolation; e.g., intracardiac ECG, intra-aortic balloon pump.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bedside Monitor — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Physiological Signals] --> B[Signal Acquisition Module]
    B --> C[Isolation Amplifier IEC60601]
    C --> D[ADC Sample and Hold]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[Parameter Extraction]
    F --> G[Display TFT LCD]
    F --> H[Alarm Subsystem]
    F --> I[Trend Memory SSD]
    F --> J[Ethernet WiFi Interface]
    J --> K[Central Monitoring System]
    J --> L[Hospital Information System HL7]
```

## 4.2 Central Monitoring System — Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WARD[ICU Ward]
        B1[Bed 1 Monitor]
        B2[Bed 2 Monitor]
        B3[Bed 3 Monitor]
        B4[Bed N Monitor]
    end
    SW[Hospital Network Switch Layer 3]
    CS[Central Station PC Review Workstation]
    SRV[HL7 DICOM Gateway Server]
    HIS[Hospital Information System HIS]
    PACS[PACS Imaging Archive]
    ALR[Master Alarm Pager Mobile App]
    B1 --> SW
    B2 --> SW
    B3 --> SW
    B4 --> SW
    SW --> CS
    SW --> SRV
    SRV --> HIS
    SRV --> PACS
    CS --> ALR
```

## 4.3 Electrical Hazard Path — Macroshock vs Microshock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MACRO[Macroshock]
        M1[Mains 230V AC] --> M2[Chassis or External Contact]
        M2 --> M3[External Skin Impedance 100kOhm]
        M3 --> M4[Body Tissue]
        M4 --> M5[Ground Path]
    end
    subgraph MICRO[Microshock]
        S1[Leakage Source mV] --> S2[Patient Cable or Catheter]
        S2 --> S3[Saline Filled Conduit]
        S3 --> S4[Heart Myocardium]
        S4 --> S5[Ground Reference]
    end
```

## 4.4 Safety Protection Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Patient Safety Defense in Depth] --> P1[1 Class I Protective Earth]
    P1 --> P2[2 Double Insulation]
    P2 --> P3[3 Isolation Amplifier 4kV Creepage]
    P3 --> P4[4 Equipotential Grounding Bus Bar]
    P4 --> P5[5 Isolated Power System IPS in OR]
    P5 --> P6[6 Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter GFCI 5mA]
    P6 --> P7[7 Leakage Current Monitoring Relay]
```

## 4.5 Telemetry System — Wireless Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT[Patient Electrodes and SpO2 Sensor] --> TB[Body Worn Transmitter 174 to 216 MHz or 2.4 GHz]
    TB --> ANT1[Antenna Whip or Patch]
    ANT1 -. Wireless Link .-> ANT2[Ceiling Mounted Diversity Antenna]
    ANT2 --> RX[Telemetry Receiver at Central Station]
    RX --> DSP[Demodulator and DSP]
    DSP --> CMS[Central Monitoring Display]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2019 Scheme Adapted to 2024]
**List any three differences between Macroshock and Microshock.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Macroshock | Microshock |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Current passes through intact skin from external source. | Current enters body through internal conductor (catheter/electrode) directly to heart. |
| 2 | Skin impedance (≈ 100 kΩ dry) limits the current. | Skin impedance is bypassed, so even µA is dangerous. |
| 3 | Perception threshold ≈ 1 mA; ventricular fibrillation at ≈ 100 mA for 1 s. | Ventricular fibrillation threshold as low as **10 µA** (Type CF applied part). |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]
**Define the term "Leakage Current" as per IEC 60601-1 and state the maximum allowable patient auxiliary current for a Type CF applied part.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Leakage current is the unwanted current that flows from the live parts of a medical device to the chassis, earth, or patient circuits, in the absence of any intended functional current path. It originates from capacitive coupling, insulation leakage, and ground loops.

**Type CF limit (1.5 marks):** As per IEC 60601-1, 3rd Edition, the maximum patient auxiliary current for a **Type CF** (Cardiac Floating) applied part is **10 µA** under normal conditions and **50 µA** under single-fault condition.

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Bedside Patient Monitoring System**. List the parameters it measures and describe the function of the alarm subsystem.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **sources of electrical hazards** in a hospital environment. Discuss Macroshock and Microshock with their respective threshold current values.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** A bedside monitor consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Patient Electrodes / Sensors** (ECG leads, SpO₂ probe, NIBP cuff, temperature probe).
2. **Signal Conditioning Unit** — Instrumentation amplifier (gain 1000, CMRR ≥ 100 dB), 50 Hz notch filter, low-pass filter (cut-off 150 Hz for ECG).
3. **Isolation Barrier** — Optical or transformer isolation (4 kV dielectric withstand).
4. **A/D Converter** — ≥ 12-bit resolution, sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP/MCU)** — Performs R-peak detection, HR calculation, SpO₂ ratio computation, NIBP oscillometric algorithm.
6. **Display Unit** — TFT LCD, multi-trace waveforms, numeric parameters, trend graphs.
7. **Alarm Subsystem** — Threshold comparator with three priority levels.
8. **Memory & Communication** — SSD, USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi.

**Measured Parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Sensor / Method |
|---|---|
| ECG (3/5/12-lead) | Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes |
| Heart Rate (HR) | Derived from R–R interval |
| Respiration Rate (RR) | Impedance pneumography (ECG electrodes, 100 kHz carrier) |
| SpO₂ | Dual-wavelength pulse oximetry (660 nm + 940 nm) |
| NIBP | Oscillometric cuff method |
| IBP | Strain-gauge or piezoresistive catheter transducer |
| Temperature (T1, T2) | Thermistor / RTD |
| EtCO₂ | Side-stream NDIR capnography (optional) |

**Alarm Subsystem Function (2 marks):**
The alarm subsystem performs **real-time threshold comparison** of every measured parameter against pre-set upper and lower limits. It generates:
- **High-priority alarms** (red, flashing, 85 dB at 1 m): life-threatening, e.g., HR < 40 or > 150 bpm, SpO₂ < 85%.
- **Medium-priority alarms** (yellow, intermittent tone): caution, e.g., systolic BP > 180 mmHg.
- **Low-priority alarms** (cyan, soft tone): informational, e.g., "lead-off" electrode disconnect.
Alarms include **audible, visual, and remote pager** outputs. IEC 60601-1-8 mandates distinct tonal patterns for each priority to avoid alarm confusion.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sources of Electrical Hazards (4 marks):**

1. **Leakage Currents** — Stray currents from AC mains to chassis through capacitive coupling (~10 nA) and insulation resistance (~10 µA).
2. **Ground Faults** — When the protective earth wire breaks, the chassis floats at mains potential. A patient touching the chassis receives a shock.
3. **Direct Mains Contact** — 230 V AC, 50 Hz; can deliver 100+ mA through wet skin.
4. **Defibrillator Discharge** — Up to 360 J; improper grounding can cause current flow through patient electrodes.
5. **Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) Leakage** — RF current at 500 kHz, 300 W can burn tissue at return-electrode contact failure.
6. **Static Electricity (ESD)** — Up to 15 kV discharge; can disrupt pacemaker function.
7. **Inductive and Capacitive Coupling** — From adjacent 50 Hz power lines into sensitive ECG/EEG leads.

**Macroshock vs Microshock (3 marks):**

| Property | Macroshock | Microshock |
|---|---|---|
| Current path | Skin → body → skin (or limb) | Catheter/electrode → heart |
| Skin resistance | Present (1 kΩ wet – 100 kΩ dry) | Bypassed |
| Perception threshold (50 Hz) | ≈ 1 mA | ≈ 10 µA (sensed by heart) |
| Ventricular fibrillation threshold | ≈ 100 mA for 1 s | **≈ 10 µA** |
| Standard | IEC 60601-1 Type B/BF | IEC 60601-1 Type CF |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write macroshock and microshock as "current above/below 10 mA" — this is **WRONG**. The correct numerical threshold is **1 mA perception / 100 mA fibrillation** for macroshock and **10 µA fibrillation** for microshock. Use these exact figures for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, 14 marks, internal choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Central Monitoring System (CMS)** with a block diagram. Discuss how telemetry is integrated for ambulatory patient monitoring.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **safety techniques** used in medical equipment to prevent electrical hazards. Mention any four (4) protection methods with their operating principles.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Central Monitoring System Overview (3 marks):**

A **Central Monitoring System (CMS)** is a networked console installed at the nurse's station of an ICU/CCU that simultaneously receives and displays data from multiple bedside monitors (4, 8, 16, 32, or 64 beds). It provides:

- **Multi-bed overview screen** with mini-traces for all beds in a tiled format.
- **Detailed view** of any selected bed (full waveforms + numerics).
- **Centralized alarms** with audio and visual indication, forwarded to mobile pagers.
- **Long-term trend storage** (up to 30–90 days of data).
- **HL7/DICOM interface** to Hospital Information System (HIS) and Electronic Medical Records (EMR).

**Block Diagram of CMS (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BS[Bedside Monitors 1 to N] --> SW[Network Switch Ethernet]
    SW --> CPU[Central Station PC Industrial Grade]
    CPU --> MON[Multi Bed Display 4 to 32 Tiles]
    CPU --> HDD[RAID Storage 1 TB or more]
    CPU --> GW[HL7 DICOM Gateway]
    GW --> HIS[Hospital Information System]
    CPU --> PRN[Alarm Pager Mobile App]
```

**Telemetry Integration (2 marks):**

Telemetry is used for **ambulatory patients** who are not bed-bound. The system uses:
- **Body-worn transmitter** (battery-operated, 174–216 MHz WMTS or 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi) attached to the patient's chest via leads.
- **Diversity antenna system** (multiple ceiling antennas) to ensure coverage in corridors and washrooms.
- **Telemetry receiver** at the central station that decodes multiple patient channels simultaneously.
- **Same CMS display** shows telemetry patients alongside bedded patients.

Frequency allocation in India follows **WMTS bands** to avoid interference from commercial radio.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Safety Techniques in Medical Equipment (7 marks = 4 × 1.75 marks):**

**1. Protective Earthing (Class I Protection) (1.75 marks):**
All exposed metal chassis of medical equipment is bonded to a **protective earth conductor** of low impedance (≤ 0.1 Ω). If an internal live wire contacts the chassis, the fault current flows to earth, blowing the fuse or tripping the circuit breaker, instead of passing through the patient. The earth wire is colour-coded **green/yellow** (IS 694).

**2. Double Insulation (Class II Protection) (1.75 marks):**
Equipment designed with **two independent layers of insulation** between live parts and the user (basic insulation + supplementary insulation). No reliance on earth connection. Symbol: a **square within a square** (IEC 60417-5172). Example: many modern patient-worn telemetry transmitters are Class II.

**3. Patient Isolation (Galvanic / Optical / Transformer) (1.75 marks):**
A **high-voltage isolation barrier** (4 kV dielectric withstand) is placed between the patient-connected circuit and the mains-powered circuitry. This can be:
- **Transformer isolation** (e.g., ISO124 from Texas Instruments).
- **Optical isolation** (e.g., 4N25, IL300).
- **Capacitive (digital) isolation** (e.g., ADuM1411, Si8261).
This ensures leakage current is limited to **< 10 µA** (Type CF).

**4. Isolated Power System (IPS) for Operating Rooms (1.75 marks):**
Hospitals install **isolated power systems** in operating rooms, ICUs, and cardiac catheterization labs. The power is delivered through an **isolation transformer** with an ungrounded secondary. A **line isolation monitor (LIM)** continuously measures the impedance from each line to ground and triggers an alarm if it falls below a threshold (typically 50 kΩ). This means that even a single fault does not complete a circuit to ground, preventing macroshock.

**5. Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI) (Bonus, 1 mark):**
A GFCI trips the circuit in **< 25 ms** when the difference between line and neutral current exceeds **5 mA**. This protects against macroshock in wet areas (bathrooms, dialysis rooms).

**6. Equipotential Grounding Bus Bar (Bonus, 1 mark):**
All metal pipes, bed frames, and equipment chassis in an ICU/OR are bonded to a single **equipotential bus bar** to eliminate potential differences (touch voltage < 10 mV).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Safety Techniques):**
> Students often list "fuse" and "MCB" as the primary safety method. While fuses protect equipment, they do **NOT** prevent microshock at 10 µA. For full marks, emphasize **isolation, double insulation, and isolated power systems**, not just fuses.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- **Bedside Monitor** = multi-parameter, real-time, bedside ICU/CCU unit; measures **ECG, HR, RR, SpO₂, NIBP/IBP, Temp, EtCO₂**.
- **Central Station (CMS)** = network of bedside monitors aggregated to a nurse-station console; uses **Ethernet/Wi-Fi, HL7, DICOM**.
- **Telemetry** = wireless transmission; **WMTS bands 174–216 MHz, 450–470 MHz, 1.4 GHz**; used for ambulatory patients.
- **Macroshock**: skin intact, perception **1 mA**, fibrillation **100 mA @ 1 s**.
- **Microshock**: catheter into heart, fibrillation as low as **10 µA** (Type CF applied part).
- **IEC 60601-1 leakage limits**: Type B ≤ 500 µA, Type BF ≤ 100 µA, **Type CF ≤ 10 µA** normal / 50 µA single-fault.
- **Type B / BF / CF**: B = body no contact, BF = body floating, **CF = cardiac floating (highest)**.
- **Defibrillator energy**: $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ → 360 J with 32 µF needs **4743 V**; peak current ≈ **95 A** into 50 Ω; time constant ≈ 1.6 ms.
- **SpO₂ formula**: ratio of ratios $R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$; SpO₂ ≈ 110 − 25·R.
- **Body impedance** ≈ 200 kΩ dry, 2.3 kΩ wet; microshock path ≈ 1.5 kΩ.
- **Safety techniques**: protective earth, double insulation, isolation barrier, isolated power system (IPS), GFCI, equipotential bonding, line isolation monitor (LIM).
- **Sampling theorem**: $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ → ECG sampled ≥ 500 Hz.
- **CMRR of ECG amp**: ≥ 100 dB; gain ≈ 1000; bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz.
- **HL7 = Health Level 7**; standard for HIS/EMR interoperability; pipe-delimited ASCII v2.x, XML v3.
- **NFPA 99** mandates isolated power in operating rooms and cardiac cath labs in many jurisdictions.
- **Alarm priority levels**: High (red, life-threatening), Medium (yellow, caution), Low (cyan, informational) — as per **IEC 60601-1-8**.
- **ESD (electrostatic discharge)** can reach 15 kV; medical equipment must withstand per IEC 61000-4-2.
- **ESU (electrosurgical unit)** uses 500 kHz RF at 100–300 W; requires proper return-electrode contact to prevent burns.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Questions in this module frequently combine a **diagram-based question** on the bedside monitor block diagram (7 marks) with a **safety/threshold question** (7 marks). Master both the parameter list (with units) and the exact µA/mA threshold values to score above 80%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomedical Imaging Techniques - X-rays, Laser Applications. Basic Principle of Computed Tomography. Magnetic Resonance Imaging System and Nuclear Medicine System. Ultrasonic Imaging System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT752 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biomedical Imaging Techniques - X-rays, Laser Applications. Basic Principle of Computed Tomography. Magnetic Resonance Imaging System and Nuclear Medicine System. Ultrasonic Imaging System

# 🩻 Biomedical Imaging Techniques — KTU Module 4 (Premium Notes)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Biomedical Imaging is the **non-invasive visualization of internal anatomical structures and physiological functions** of the human body using physical energy sources (X-rays, RF, ultrasound, gamma rays, lasers). It forms the diagnostic backbone of modern clinical medicine and is the highest-weight topic in KTU Module 4 of *PEEIT752 — Biomedical Instrumentation*.

### 🔹 1.1 X-Rays (Radiography)

**Formal Definition:** X-rays are a form of *ionizing electromagnetic radiation* with wavelengths in the range **0.01 nm to 10 nm** (frequencies $3 \times 10^{16}$ Hz to $3 \times 10^{19}$ Hz), produced by the sudden deceleration of high-velocity electrons striking a heavy-metal target (typically tungsten) in an evacuated X-ray tube.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Discovered by **Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen** in **1895**. X-rays are generated by two distinct mechanisms inside an X-ray tube:
> 1. **Bremsstrahlung** (braking radiation) — continuous spectrum
> 2. **Characteristic Radiation** — discrete spectrum

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine throwing tennis balls (electrons) at a thick concrete wall (tungsten target). When the ball hits and suddenly stops, its kinetic energy must go *somewhere* — it converts into a brief flash of light (X-ray photon). The harder the throw (higher kVp), the brighter and more penetrating the flash.

**Physical Constants Used:**
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- Energy of X-ray photon $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$
- Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$

### 🔹 1.2 Laser Applications in Medicine

**Formal Definition:** A laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) is a device that produces a highly **monochromatic**, **coherent**, and **collimated** beam of light, used in medicine for precise cutting, coagulation, and tissue ablation.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A laser is like a perfectly organized marching band where every photon marches in step (coherence), at exactly the same wavelength (monochromaticity), and in exactly the same direction (collimation). This "military precision" allows it to focus enormous energy onto a spot smaller than a pinhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Medical Laser Types (KTU MUST-KNOW):**
> - **CO₂ laser** ($\lambda = 10{,}600$ nm) — surgical cutting, vaporization
> - **Nd:YAG laser** ($\lambda = 1064$ nm) — deep tissue coagulation, ophthalmology
> - **Argon laser** ($\lambda = 488/514$ nm) — retinal photocoagulation, dermatology
> - **He-Ne laser** ($\lambda = 632.8$ nm) — biostimulation, alignment
> - **Excimer laser** ($\lambda = 193\text{–}351$ nm) — LASIK refractive surgery (UV)

### 🔹 1.3 Computed Tomography (CT)

**Formal Definition:** Computed Tomography is an *X-ray based imaging modality* that reconstructs **cross-sectional (tomographic) images** of the body by mathematically combining X-ray attenuation measurements taken from **multiple angular projections** using the *Radon Transform* and *Filtered Back-Projection* algorithms.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of slicing a loaf of bread. A regular X-ray is like holding the whole loaf against a light bulb — you see a shadow of all slices stacked together. CT is like carefully slicing the loaf into thin pieces and photographing each slice individually, then stacking the photos back to see inside.

**Key Concept:** The image contrast in CT is expressed in **Hounsfield Units (HU)**, where water = 0 HU and air = −1000 HU.

### 🔹 1.4 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)

**Formal Definition:** MRI is a *non-ionizing imaging modality* that uses strong static magnetic fields (typically **0.5 T to 3 T**), gradient magnetic fields, and radio-frequency (RF) pulses to excite hydrogen protons ($\mathrm{^{1}H}$) in body tissues, producing images based on the **Longitudinal (T₁)** and **Transverse (T₂)** relaxation times.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine spinning tops (hydrogen protons) inside your body. In their normal state, they wobble randomly. When you turn on a giant magnet (the MRI scanner), all the tops align in the same direction. Now you tap them with a radio wave (RF pulse) — they wobble in unison and emit a faint signal as they fall back. Listening to this signal lets you build a picture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core MRI Principle (KTU MUST-KNOW):** The resonance condition is the **Larmor Equation:**
> $$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$
> where $\omega_0$ = Larmor frequency, $\gamma$ = gyromagnetic ratio (42.58 MHz/T for hydrogen), $B_0$ = static field strength.

### 🔹 1.5 Nuclear Medicine System

**Formal Definition:** Nuclear Medicine imaging uses **radiopharmaceuticals** (radioactive tracers) administered to the patient; emitted gamma rays are detected externally to form functional images of organs. Two principal modalities are **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)** and **SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography).**

**Intuitive Analogy:** Regular X-ray/CT shows *anatomy* (the body's structure). Nuclear medicine shows *function* — like putting a tiny glowing tracker on a red blood cell and watching where it travels inside the body.

| Modality | Tracer Type | Energy | Imaging Type |
|----------|-------------|--------|--------------|
| **SPECT** | Single γ emitter (e.g., $^{99m}$Tc) | 140 keV | 3D functional |
| **PET** | Positron emitter (e.g., $^{18}$F-FDG) | 511 keV | 3D metabolic |

### 🔹 1.6 Ultrasonic Imaging System

**Formal Definition:** Ultrasonic Imaging uses **high-frequency sound waves (2–18 MHz)** transmitted into the body via a piezoelectric transducer; the **reflected echoes** from tissue interfaces are processed to produce real-time images based on the *pulse-echo* principle.

**Intuitive Analogy:** It's exactly like sonar on a submarine. The transducer shouts a "ping" (ultrasound pulse) into the body. When the ping hits something (an organ boundary, a stone, a baby), it echoes back. The time it takes to echo tells you how deep that thing is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic ultrasound uses frequencies of 2 MHz to 18 MHz**, well above the human audible range (20 Hz–20 kHz). Velocity of ultrasound in soft tissue ≈ **1540 m/s** (this is the standard KTU constant).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔹 2.1 X-Ray Production — Operational Breakdown

**Step 1 — Electron Emission:** A tungsten filament cathode is heated to ~2500 K (thermionic emission), ejecting electrons via the Edison effect.

**Step 2 — Electron Acceleration:** A high potential difference (**typically 30 kVp to 150 kVp**) accelerates the electrons across the vacuum tube toward the anode.

**Step 3 — Sudden Deceleration (Bremsstrahlung):** When electrons strike the tungsten target, ~99% of kinetic energy becomes heat and ~1% becomes X-ray photons. The *braking* mechanism produces a **continuous spectrum**.

**Step 4 — Characteristic Radiation:** Inner-shell electrons of tungsten are knocked out; outer-shell electrons fall in to fill the vacancy, releasing photons of energy specific to tungsten's atomic structure (K-series: Kα = 59.3 keV, Kβ = 67.2 keV).

**Step 5 — Beam Shaping & Filtration:** The diverging X-ray beam is collimated and passed through aluminum filters to remove low-energy "soft" photons that only harm the skin.

**Why this matters:** Higher kVp → more penetrating X-rays → better for dense tissues (bone). Lower kVp → softer X-rays → better for soft tissues (mammography).

### 🔹 2.2 X-Ray Interaction with Tissue — Attenuation

When X-rays traverse matter, intensity decreases **exponentially** according to the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity
- $I(x)$ = transmitted intensity at depth $x$
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹) — tissue-dependent
- $x$ = thickness of tissue (cm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This exponential decay is the fundamental physical principle that allows CT scanners to mathematically reconstruct the *internal distribution* of $\mu$ values inside the body — and thereby build an anatomical image.

### 🔹 2.3 Laser–Tissue Interaction Mechanisms

When a laser beam strikes tissue, four primary interactions occur based on power density and exposure time:

| Interaction | Power Density | Duration | Medical Use |
|-------------|---------------|----------|-------------|
| **Photocoagulation** | $10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ W/cm² | seconds | Retinal detachment repair |
| **Photovaporization** | $10^{5}$–$10^{7}$ W/cm² | ms | Tumor ablation |
| **Photoablation** | $10^{7}$–$10^{9}$ W/cm² | μs | LASIK corneal reshaping |
| **Photodisruption** | $>10^{9}$ W/cm² | ns/ps | Intraocular surgery |

The **Argon laser** is selectively absorbed by **hemoglobin** and **melanin** → ideal for vascular lesions.
The **CO₂ laser** is strongly absorbed by **water** → ideal for cutting water-rich soft tissue.

### 🔹 2.4 CT Reconstruction — Theory

CT mathematics is rooted in the **Radon Transform** (1917): *the integral of a function along a line equals the projection of that function at a given angle.*

For a 2D attenuation distribution $\mu(x, y)$, the projection at angle $\theta$ is:

$$P(\theta, s) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x, y) \, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - s) \, dx \, dy$$

The image is reconstructed using **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**, the algorithm used in every clinical CT scanner.

**Hounsfield Unit Definition:**

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

| Tissue | Hounsfield Unit (HU) |
|--------|----------------------|
| Air | −1000 |
| Lung | −500 |
| Fat | −100 to −50 |
| Water | 0 |
| Muscle | +35 to +55 |
| Blood | +30 to +45 |
| Bone (cortical) | +700 to +3000 |

### 🔹 2.5 MRI Physics — Relaxation Theory

**Step 1 — Alignment:** At equilibrium in $B_0$, net magnetization $M_0$ aligns *parallel* to $B_0$ (longitudinal).

**Step 2 — Excitation:** A 90° RF pulse at the Larmor frequency tips $M_0$ into the transverse plane.

**Step 3 — T₁ Recovery (Longitudinal):** Magnetization regrows along $B_0$ exponentially:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right)$$

**Step 4 — T₂ Decay (Transverse):** Transverse magnetization decays due to spin-spin interactions:

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

**Step 5 — Spatial Encoding:** Gradient fields (Gx, Gy, Gz) make the Larmor frequency a function of position, enabling slice selection, phase encoding, and frequency encoding.

### 🔹 2.6 Nuclear Medicine — Tracer Kinetics

**SPECT Principle:** A gamma camera (Anger camera) with a NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal detects single 140 keV photons from $^{99m}$Tc decay. A collimator ensures only photons traveling perpendicular to the detector face contribute to the image.

**PET Principle:** The radiotracer (e.g., $^{18}$F-FDG) emits a **positron**. After traveling ~1 mm, the positron annihilates with an electron, producing **two 511 keV photons at 180°**. Coincidence detection locates the annihilation event along the *line of response (LOR)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 511 keV?** This is the rest-mass energy of an electron ($E = mc^2 = 511$ keV) split equally between the two annihilation photons — a direct consequence of Einstein's mass-energy equivalence. This is a KTU board-favorite numerical question.

### 🔹 2.7 Ultrasonic Imaging — Pulse-Echo Principle

**Step 1 — Pulse Generation:** A high-voltage pulse (~100–200 V) excites the piezoelectric crystal (lead zirconate titanate, PZT), producing an ultrasound burst of ~2–3 cycles.

**Step 2 — Propagation:** The pulse travels at the **acoustic velocity** (1540 m/s in soft tissue) and partially reflects at each acoustic impedance mismatch.

**Step 3 — Echo Reception:** Reflected echoes return to the same transducer (acting now as a receiver), where they induce an electrical signal via the *reverse piezoelectric effect*.

**Step 4 — Range Calculation:** The depth of the reflector is:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v$ = velocity of sound, $t$ = round-trip time, division by 2 because the pulse travels *out and back*.

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | X-ray attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24}{V_{\text{kVp}}} \text{ nm}$ | Duane-Hunt limit (shortest X-ray wavelength) | nm |
| 3 | $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | Hounsfield Unit (CT number) | HU |
| 4 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor precession frequency | rad/s |
| 5 | $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ | Larmor freq. for hydrogen at field $B_0$ | MHz |
| 6 | $M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$ | T₁ longitudinal recovery | T (Tesla) |
| 7 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \, e^{-t/T_2}$ | T₂ transverse decay | T (Tesla) |
| 8 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Ultrasound range (pulse-echo) | m |
| 9 | $f_D = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift (blood flow) | Hz |
| 10 | $R_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | Axial (range) resolution | m |
| 11 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{PET}} \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{events}}}$ | PET noise — coincidence count limited | dimensionless |
| 12 | $E_{\gamma} = m_e c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}$ | Annihilation photon energy | keV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Beer-Lambert law, Larmor equation, and pulse-echo distance formula are the three "holy pillars" of biomedical imaging physics. Master these and you can solve ~80% of KTU numerical questions on Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔹 3.1 Derivation: X-Ray Attenuation (Beer-Lambert Law for X-Rays)

We model X-ray attenuation as the differential decrease in photon flux $dI$ over an infinitesimal slab of thickness $dx$:

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation.** The fraction of photons removed in $dx$ is proportional to the intensity $I$ and the slab thickness $dx$:

$$dI = -\mu \, I \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.** Move all $I$ terms to one side:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to $x$.** Limits: intensity changes from $I_0$ to $I(x)$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides** to obtain the final exponential law:

$$\boxed{I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Attenuation is exponential — doubling the thickness does NOT halve the intensity; it squares the attenuation. This is why bone (high $\mu$) blocks X-rays strongly.

---

### 🔹 3.2 Derivation: Duane-Hunt Minimum Wavelength Law

When an electron is accelerated through a potential $V$ (in volts) and loses all its kinetic energy in a single collision, the resulting X-ray photon has the maximum possible energy and hence the **minimum possible wavelength**.

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy gained by the electron:**

$$KE = eV$$

where $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V$ in volts.

**Step 2 — Equate kinetic energy to photon energy** $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}} = eV$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\lambda_{\min}$:**

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical constants:**
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})V}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, V} = \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to nanometers** (1 m = $10^{9}$ nm):

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min} \text{ (nm)} = \frac{1240}{V_{\text{volts}}} = \frac{1.24}{V_{\text{kVp}}}}$$

**Worked Example:** For an X-ray tube operating at $V = 100$ kVp:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.24}{100} = 0.0124 \text{ nm}$$

This matches the KTU syllabus figure for medical X-rays.

---

### 🔹 3.3 Derivation: Hounsfield Unit from Linear Attenuation

**Step 1 — Start with the attenuation of water as reference:**

$$\mu_{\text{water}} = \mu_0$$

**Step 2 — Define a normalized attenuation difference:**

$$\Delta \mu = \mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to water and scale by 1000** (the conventional CT scale):

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with known points:**
- For water: $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{water}}$, so $\text{HU} = 1000 \times 0 = 0$ ✓
- For air: $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0$, so $\text{HU} \approx -1000$ ✓
- For dense cortical bone: $\mu \approx 2\mu_{\text{water}}$, so $\text{HU} \approx +1000$ (actual range +700 to +3000)

$$\boxed{\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}}$$

---

### 🔹 3.4 Derivation: Ultrasound Range from Pulse-Echo

**Step 1 — Define variables:**
- $v$ = acoustic velocity in medium (m/s) — for soft tissue, $v = 1540$ m/s
- $t$ = measured round-trip time from pulse emission to echo reception (s)
- $d$ = one-way distance to reflector (m)

**Step 2 — Total path length** traveled by the pulse = outward + inward = $2d$.

**Step 3 — Apply the basic kinematic equation** $\text{distance} = \text{velocity} \times \text{time}$:

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$:**

$$\boxed{d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}}$$

**Worked Example:** If an echo returns in $t = 130$ μs from a tissue interface:

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.2002}{2} = 0.1001 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ cm}$$

---

### 🔹 3.5 Derivation: Doppler Shift for Blood Flow

When ultrasound reflects off moving red blood cells, the echo frequency shifts. We derive the classical Doppler equation used in vascular ultrasound.

**Step 1 — Source-to-target Doppler shift** (moving target as receiver):

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

where $v\cos\theta$ is the blood velocity component along the beam axis.

**Step 2 — Target-to-receiver Doppler shift** (moving target as re-emitter):

$$f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and combine:**

$$f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Approximate for $v \ll c$** (always true in blood flow):

$$\frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \approx 1 + \frac{2v\cos\theta}{c}$$

**Step 5 — The Doppler-shifted frequency** is:

$$f_D = f_2 - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

$$\boxed{f_D = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

**Engineering Use:** This is the working equation of every color Doppler ultrasound machine used in cardiology and obstetrics.

---

### 🔹 3.6 Derivation: Larmor Frequency for MRI

**Step 1 — A proton of magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ placed in field $\vec{B_0}$ experiences a torque** $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$.

**Step 2 — This torque causes angular momentum $\vec{L}$ to precess** according to $\dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\tau}$.

**Step 3 — The precession angular frequency** $\omega_0$ satisfies:

$$\omega_0 L = \mu B_0$$

**Step 4 — Define gyromagnetic ratio** $\gamma = \dfrac{\mu}{L}$:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

**Step 5 — For hydrogen** $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^{8}$ rad/s/T $= 42.58$ MHz/T:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} = 42.58 \times B_0 \text{ MHz}}$$

**Worked Example:** At $B_0 = 1.5$ T (common clinical MRI):

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

This is in the radio-frequency band — a fact the KTU examiner loves to test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔹 4.1 Block Diagram — X-Ray Imaging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HV Transformer<br>30-150 kVp] --> TUBE[X-Ray Tube<br>Cathode+Anode]
    TUBE --> COLL[Collimator<br>Beam Shaping]
    COLL --> FILT[Aluminum Filter<br>Remove Soft X-rays]
    FILT --> PAT[Patient Body<br>Differential Attenuation]
    PAT --> DET[Image Receptor<br>Film or Digital Detector]
    DET --> PROC[Image Processor<br>Computer]
    PROC --> DISP[Display Monitor]
    PAT2[Tungsten Filament<br>Cathode] -.Heating.-> TUBE
```

**System Logic:** High-voltage AC drives electrons from cathode to anode → X-ray beam is shaped → passes through patient → differential attenuation creates latent image → processed for viewing.

### 🔹 4.2 Block Diagram — Computed Tomography (CT) System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Gantry[Gantry Assembly<br>Rotating Frame] --> TubeCT[X-Ray Tube]
    Gantry --> DetArc[Arc of<br>Solid-State Detectors]
    TubeCT --> Slip[High-Voltage<br>Slip Ring]
    Slip --> GenCT[High-Frequency<br>Generator]
    DetArc --> DAS[Data Acquisition<br>System]
    DAS --> Recon[Reconstruction<br>Computer FBP Algorithm]
    Recon --> Cons[Operator Console]
    Cons --> Arch[Image Archive<br>PACS]
    Bed[Patient Table<br>Translate/Rotate] --> Gantry
```

### 🔹 4.3 Block Diagram — MRI System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MagnetSystem [Superconducting Magnet Subsystem]
        M[Main Magnet<br>0.5 to 3 Tesla] --> Shim[Shim Coils<br>B0 Homogeneity]
        M --> GradX[Gradient X Coil]
        M --> GradY[Gradient Y Coil]
        M --> GradZ[Gradient Z Coil]
    end

    RF[RF Transmitter<br>Larmor Frequency] --> RFPower[RF Power Amplifier]
    RFPower --> RFCoil[RF Body Coil<br>Transmit/Receive]
    RFCoil --> PatientMRI[Patient Bore]
    PatientMRI --> RFCoil
    RFCoil --> Preamp[Low-Noise Preamp]
    Preamp --> Recv[Receiver Chain]
    Recv --> ADC[Analog to<br>Digital Converter]
    ADC --> ReconMRI[Reconstruction<br>Computer 2D-FFT]
    ReconMRI --> DispMRI[Display Console]
    GradX --> GradDrv[Gradient Driver]
    GradY --> GradDrv
    GradZ --> GradDrv
```

### 🔹 4.4 Block Diagram — Nuclear Medicine (SPECT/PET) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Radio[Radiopharmacy<br>Radiotracer Synthesis] --> Inj[Patient Injection<br>Tracer Administration]
    Inj --> Uptake[Tracer Uptake<br>by Target Organ]
    Uptake --> Emis[Gamma or<br>Positron Emission]
    Emis --> Coll[Lead Collimator<br>Spatial Filter]
    Coll --> Scint[Scintillation Crystal<br>NaI Tl for SPECT]
    Scint --> PMT[Photomultiplier<br>Tube Array]
    PMT --> Pos[Position Logic<br>Anger Algorithm]
    Pos --> Acq[Data Acquisition<br>Computer]
    Acq --> ReconNM[Filtered Back<br>Projection FBP]
    ReconNM --> DispNM[Functional<br>Image Display]
```

### 🔹 4.5 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Imaging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Pulser<br>Transmitter] --> TRSW[T/R Switch<br>Protects Receiver]
    TRSW --> XDUCER[Piezoelectric<br>Transducer PZT]
    XDUCER --> Tissue[Body Tissue<br>Pulse Propagation]
    Tissue --> XDUCER
    XDUCER --> TRSW
    TRSW --> RX[Receiver<br>TGC Amplifier]
    RX --> ScanConv[Scan Converter<br>Polar to Cartesian]
    ScanConv --> DispUS[Real-Time<br>Display]
    Ctrl[Master Timer<br>and Controller] --> TX
    Ctrl --> TRSW
    Ctrl --> RX
    Ctrl --> ScanConv
```

### 🔹 4.6 Functional Flow — Image Formation Across All Modalities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source<br>X-Ray RF Laser Gamma US] --> B[Energy-Tissue<br>Interaction]
    B --> C[Signal Generation<br>Attenuation Echo Emission]
    C --> D[Signal Detection<br>Transducer Film Coil PMT]
    D --> E[Signal Processing<br>FBP FFT Doppler]
    E --> F[Image Reconstruction<br>2D or 3D Map]
    F --> G[Diagnostic Display<br>Clinical Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Explain the principle of operation of an X-ray tube with a neat diagram. Mention the role of the target material.*

**Model Answer:**

An X-ray tube consists of a **tungsten filament cathode** and a **tungsten or molybdenum target anode** enclosed in a high-vacuum glass envelope. The cathode is heated to ~2500 K, emitting electrons via thermionic emission. A high potential difference (**30–150 kVp**) accelerates these electrons toward the anode. Upon sudden deceleration at the target, the kinetic energy converts to:
1. **Bremsstrahlung** — continuous X-ray spectrum from electron deceleration (~80%)
2. **Characteristic radiation** — discrete K-series lines from tungsten inner-shell transitions (~20%)

The **tungsten target** (Z = 74) is chosen because of its **high atomic number** (efficient X-ray production), **high melting point** (3422 °C, withstands electron bombardment), and **good thermal conductivity** (heat dissipation).

*(3 marks: 1 for diagram-equivalent description, 1 for two mechanisms, 1 for tungsten justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any three medical applications of lasers and explain the principle of laser action in brief.*

**Model Answer:**

**Stimulated Emission Principle (Einstein 1917):** When an excited atom is struck by a photon of energy exactly equal to its excitation energy, it emits a second photon **identical** in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization. Optical resonance inside the laser cavity (between two mirrors) amplifies this process, producing a coherent beam.

**Medical Applications:**
1. **CO₂ laser** — surgical cutting and vaporization of soft tissue (water absorption at 10.6 μm)
2. **Argon laser** — retinal photocoagulation in diabetic retinopathy (hemoglobin absorption at 488/514 nm)
3. **Nd:YAG laser** — endoscopic coagulation of GI bleeds and prostate surgery
4. **Excimer laser** — corneal reshaping in LASIK refractive surgery (UV photoablation)

*(3 marks: 1 for principle, 2 for applications with specific wavelengths)*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Forgetting to **state numerical values with units** (e.g., "$\mu$" alone vs "$\mu$ in cm⁻¹")
> - Confusing **HU = 0 for water** with **HU = 0 for all tissue**
> - Writing **"CT uses X-rays"** without explaining the *tomographic reconstruction* concept — KTU specifically tests this distinction
> - In MRI, omitting the **Larmor equation** — losing 3 marks immediately
> - In ultrasound, forgetting the **factor of 2** in the range equation

---

#### 🟢 Part B — Option A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** *(a)* Explain the basic principle of **Computed Tomography** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the **Hounsfield Unit**. *(b)* An X-ray of intensity $I_0$ passes through 4 cm of muscle. If the linear attenuation coefficient of muscle at 60 keV is $\mu = 0.20$ cm⁻¹, calculate the transmitted intensity. What thickness will reduce the intensity to 1% of $I_0$?

**Solution:**

**(a) Principle of CT (7 marks):**

Computed Tomography produces **cross-sectional images** by measuring X-ray attenuation from multiple angles around the body. The patient lies on a table that translates through a rotating gantry containing an X-ray tube and an arc of detectors (typically 800–1500 elements). For each slice, the tube rotates 360° acquiring ~1000 projections.

Each projection is a 1D profile of attenuation integrals (Radon Transform). These projections are mathematically combined using the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** algorithm to reconstruct a 2D map of linear attenuation coefficients $\mu(x, y)$. Each pixel of this map is converted to a **Hounsfield Unit** via:

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

The reconstruction process involves four steps:
1. **Data acquisition** — projection profiles at each angle $\theta$
2. **Preprocessing** — logarithmic conversion to line integrals
3. **Filtering** — convolution with a ramp filter (e.g., Shepp-Logan)
4. **Back-projection** — smearing each filtered projection back across the image matrix and summing

This yields a 512×512 pixel matrix displayed as a grayscale image.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $I_0$ = incident intensity, $x = 4$ cm, $\mu = 0.20$ cm⁻¹

**Step 1 — Apply Beer-Lambert Law:**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} = I_0 \, e^{-(0.20)(4)} = I_0 \, e^{-0.8}$$

$$I = I_0 \times 0.4493 \approx 0.449 \, I_0$$

**Step 2 — For intensity to be 1% of $I_0$**, set $I = 0.01 \, I_0$:

$$0.01 \, I_0 = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

$$0.01 = e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln(0.01) = -\mu x$$

$$-4.6052 = -0.20 \, x$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$:**

$$x = \frac{4.6052}{0.20} = 23.03 \text{ cm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Transmitted intensity after 4 cm: $I \approx 0.449 \, I_0$
- Thickness for 1% transmission: $x \approx 23.03$ cm

---

#### 🔵 Part B — Option B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** *(a)* With a block diagram, explain the working of an **MRI system**. State and explain the **Larmor equation**. *(b)* Calculate the Larmor frequency of hydrogen protons in a 1.5 T MRI scanner. If the magnetic field is increased to 3 T, what is the new Larmor frequency? What tissue parameter does MRI primarily image?

**Solution:**

**(a) MRI Block Diagram & Larmor Equation (7 marks):**

**Major Subsystems:**
1. **Main Magnet** — superconducting coil producing $B_0$ (typically 0.5–3 T)
2. **Gradient Coils** — X, Y, Z gradients for spatial encoding
3. **RF System** — transmitter, body coil, receiver chain
4. **Computer** — pulse sequence control, image reconstruction
5. **Patient Table & Bore** — patient positioning

**Working Principle:** When the patient is placed in $B_0$, hydrogen protons (abundant in water and fat) precess at the Larmor frequency. An RF pulse at this frequency tips the net magnetization into the transverse plane. When the pulse is switched off, protons relax:
- **T₁ (spin-lattice)** — recovery along $B_0$
- **T₂ (spin-spin)** — decay in transverse plane

The receiver coil detects the **Free Induction Decay (FID)** signal. Gradient fields encode position into frequency and phase, and a 2D Fourier Transform reconstructs the image.

**Larmor Equation:**

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{or} \quad f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

where $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^{8}$ rad/s/T for hydrogen, giving $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ MHz.

This equation tells us that **each proton precesses at a frequency proportional to the magnetic field it experiences** — this is the fundamental basis for MRI frequency encoding.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Larmor frequency at 1.5 T:**

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Larmor frequency at 3.0 T:**

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 3.0 = 127.74 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Tissue parameter imaged:** MRI primarily images the **proton density (ρ)** and the **relaxation times T₁ and T₂** of hydrogen in tissue. Different tissues (gray matter, white matter, CSF, fat, tumor) have distinct T₁/T₂ values, giving MRI its excellent **soft-tissue contrast** — a major advantage over CT.

**Step 4 — Frequency ratio:**

$$\frac{f_0(3T)}{f_0(1.5T)} = \frac{127.74}{63.87} = 2.0$$

The frequency exactly doubles when the field doubles — a linear relationship confirmed by the Larmor equation.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision)

- ✅ X-rays: discovered 1895 by Röntgen; wavelength 0.01–10 nm; tungsten target; **Bremsstrahlung + Characteristic** mechanisms; $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24}{V_{\text{kVp}}}$ nm
- ✅ X-ray attenuation: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — exponential law
- ✅ Laser: monochromatic + coherent + collimated; medical types — CO₂, Nd:YAG, Argon, He-Ne, Excimer
- ✅ CT: tomographic reconstruction via Filtered Back-Projection; HU scale: **air = −1000, water = 0, bone = +700 to +3000**
- ✅ Hounsfield Unit: $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$
- ✅ MRI: non-ionizing, uses hydrogen proton precession; **Larmor equation $f_0 = 42.58 B_0$ MHz**; images **T₁, T₂, proton density**
- ✅ T₁ recovery: $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$; T₂ decay: $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$
- ✅ Nuclear Medicine: SPECT uses single γ ($^{99m}$Tc, 140 keV); PET uses positron annihilation → **two 511 keV photons at 180°**
- ✅ Ultrasound: 2–18 MHz, **v = 1540 m/s in soft tissue**, pulse-echo principle, $d = vt/2$
- ✅ Doppler ultrasound: $f_D = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — used for blood flow measurement
- ✅ Axial resolution: $R_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ — depends on pulse bandwidth
- ✅ Attenuation coefficient $\mu$ depends on **tissue density, atomic number, and photon energy**
- ✅ **MRI is contraindicated** for patients with ferromagnetic implants/pacemakers (no ionizing radiation but uses very strong magnetic fields)
- ✅ **CT is fast but uses ionizing radiation; MRI is slow but safer for repeated imaging** — common 2-mark comparison question
- ✅ Image reconstruction in CT: **Filtered Back-Projection**; in MRI: **2D Fourier Transform**; in PET: **iterative/OSEM**
- ✅ Always carry the **factor of 2** in ultrasound range calculations (round-trip travel)
- ✅ The **half-value layer (HVL)** is the thickness that reduces intensity by 50%: $x_{\text{HVL}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question says *"explain the principle"*, never give only a definition. Always include: **(1) energy source, (2) tissue interaction mechanism, (3) detection method, (4) image formation algorithm, (5) clinical use.** This 5-point structure guarantees full marks on any "explain" question in PEEIT752.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
